Title: Hanborough to Blenheim & Evenlode Valley Walk (Lowland)
Summary:
(This walk has been prepared & written by
Jeremy Mohamed
as his assessment event as he seeks to become a Lowland & Hill Walks leader with OutdoorLads. Your support for Jeremy is sought)
This is a walk I’ve come to know well over the last few years — one I’ve mostly done alone — and I thought it would be nice to share it.
We’ll start in Long Hanborough and follow the community path into the
Blenheim Estate
, crossing into the parkland near Bladon before heading towards the lakes and cascades. This opening section adds a quieter approach into the estate before joining the main circular route.
We’ll then leave the estate and follow the River Evenlode out into the surrounding countryside, where the landscape opens up into fields, meadows and quieter paths. We’ll pass the remains of the
North Leigh Roman Villa
before continuing along riverside paths, with occasional glimpses of the railway line cutting through the valley.
Along the way, I’ll share a few stories and lesser-known bits about the area — the kind of things you won’t get on an official tour — just enough to add a bit of colour to the landscape as we go.
It’s a varied countryside walk combining estate parkland, river scenery and open Oxfordshire landscape — a route shaped by repetition, familiarity and a lot of wandering.
Approx. distance: 8.5 miles
Terrain: Fairly flat with the occasional short incline
Total ascent: Approx. 100m
Surfaces: Mix of estate paths, tracks and field paths
Dogs
Dogs are welcome, but must be kept on a lead within Blenheim Estate grounds.
Hanborough Station is just over an hour by direct train from London Paddington.
Photos
: AI-generated by leader, or courtesy of Lloyd Thomas.
Difficulty & Distance: Fairly flat terrain with the occasional short incline. The distance makes this a moderate walk overall.

Title: An Suidhe wind farm gravel cycle
Summary:
An Suidhe wind farm gravel cycle
Gravel cycling began over 100 years ago when the high passes in the tour de France were dusty roads with no tarmac. Gravel cycling features in many modern-day road cycling races such as the Strade Bianche and Paris-Roubaix.
Scotland is well served for Gravel routes with forestry commission land and new windfarm developments being popular destinations. This event features a mixture of both.
Join us on another route from the Gravel Rides Scotland book. The route takes us high above Lochs Fyne and Etive, with hopefully good views of the Argyll area.
Although fairly short there is considerable climbing involved on this cycle. A reasonable level of fitness and confidence on tricky surfaces is required.
The pictures shown are all from previous OutdoorLads gravel events (with the exception of MVDP showing off his gravel skills at the recent Benidorm cyclocross World Cup!).
Photos :
Taken by Brendan70

chat
and
notification
groups and bookmark our upcoming events list via our
Scottish Calendar
.
Difficulty & Distance: Short cycle with 600m of elevation

Title: River Dee, Chester City Walls and the Dukes Drive 2026
Summary:
An easy walk along the River Dee at Chester including a walk round the city walls.
We will be meeting at the River Dee at Eccleston an interesting village in the Duke of Westminster’s estate. We will walk into Chester along the riverside path for about 4 miles, passing Earls Eye Meadows and crossing the river on the Queens Park Suspension Bridge.
We will stop for lunch at the Groves, a delightful riverside area. There will be an opportunity to go to a local cafe for anyone who hasn’t brought a packed lunch. After lunch we will walk round the city walls and will see lots of Roman remains, the cathedral and the famous racecourse. For those who don’t want to walk round the walls there will be time for shopping instead.
We will then cross the River again and walk along the Dukes Drive back to Eccleston and our starting point.
If you are planning to use public transport I'm sure it will be possible for someone to meet you at the railway or bus station.
Don't worry if all the places have gone, sign up anyway and you will go on the waiting list. Then if someone pulls out you will get a place.
All photos taken by Andrew
Difficulty & Distance: 8 miles 6 hours

Title: Nine Edges Derbyshire Challenge Walk
Summary:
"If your dreams do not scare you, they are not big enough." (Ellen Johnson Sirleaf)
“The best moments in our lives are not the passive, receptive, relaxing times . . . The best moments usually occur if a person’s body or mind is stretched to its limits in a voluntary effort to accomplish something difficult and worthwhile” (Csikszentmihalyi, 1990).
A challenging, linear day walk of about 35 km (22 miles) with 933 m (3,061 feet)
of total ascent traverses nine different gritstone edges in the Peak District. The walk will take about 10 hours to complete including several stops along the way.
We traverse the following Peak District edges:
Derwent Edge
Stanage Edge
Upper Burbage Edge
Lower Burbage Edge
Froggatt Edge
Curbar Edge
Baslow Edge
Gardom’s Edge
Birchen Edge
The highest point is Back Tor at 538 m (1766 feet) near to the start of the walk. The route features great views across the High Peak from the edges, and it includes Derwent Dam, the Longshaw Estate, and woods between Baslow and Gardom’s Edge. The route takes us to the trig points and/or Ethels of
Back Tor, High Neb, Stanage Edge, Birchen Edge, Lost Lad, and White Path Moss
.
We begin at Ladybower Reservoir from Fairholmes car park, past Derwent Dam then up to Lost Lad and Back Tor at the start of Derwent Edge. From here we continue on to Stanage Edge, completing the two longest edges first.
Next, we traverse Upper and Lower Burbage Edges, before heading, via the Longshaw Estate, onto edge number five, Froggatt Edge. The next two, Curbar Edge then Baslow Edge, are beautifully linked.
Finally, we walk through beautiful woods to the final two, Gardom’s Edge and Birchen Edge. The last edge is just over 1 km from the trig point to the car park at the end of the walk.
Note
, this is a linear walk
. We will rendezvous
at the end point leaving half of the cars there to travel together to the start point to begin the walk. At the end of the walk we will convey the drivers who left their cars back to the start point. The drive from the finish at Birchen Edge car park to Fairholmes car park is 16 miles, about 30-minute drive, via Hathersage.
There are limited options to do this walk via public transport. The nearest train station to the start is Bamford and at the end of the walk it’s Grindleford station, which is closer to Froggatt Edge, so quite a distance away. The walk leader will facilitate a lift from and back to Derby for anyone who needs it.
Facilities on the walk
There are public toilets available at the start of the walk at Fairholmes car park and also when you come down from Lower Burbage Edge and walk through the Longshaw Estate. There’s also a small shop/cafe you can buy takeaway food at Fairholmes.
In addition, The Grouse Inn pub is open for takeaway food/drinks before Froggatt Edge where we could stop for refreshments and a toilet stop.
At the end of the walk, there are no toilets, but The Robin Hood pub allows us to finish in style.
We will walk at a reasonable average pace of about 3.5 kph (2.2 mph) with regular stops along the way, as well as longer breaks for a packed lunch and evening meal (you will need to bring both with you). This is a long and challenging walk but it is not a race!
There is a link to the
OS Maps
route for members, below.
Dogs are welcome
but they must be capable of walking the distance and owners must ensure dogs are well nourished and kept under control at all times.
This is a very challenging walk,
published in plenty of time to prepare as necessary.  Personal safety and wellbeing is paramount, so arrangements will be put in place to 'rescue' participants, if necessary. You must ensure you are fit and well enough to undertake the walk unaided, with suitable footwear. It is 37 km (23 miles) over 1200 metres of ascent within about 10 hours.
Note:
If there are no places left on this event, join the waiting list, as people frequently drop out during the week before an event, and you are likely to get a place.

Title: An Evening with the Stars in Nottinghamshire
Summary:
We have secured a booking for a group visit to
Sherwood Observatory
.
Since its recent re-opening, the Observatory has been booked solid with group tours so we are pleased to offer this opportunity in Autumn 2026.
The visit promises a 90 minute adventure you'll never forget including a chance to ask anything you want to know about space and beyond. Additionally there will be:
a 20+ minute planetarium show and presentation by the expert team
a captivating talk in the lecture room that will spark your imagination
a thrilling opportunity to gaze at the stars and planets or our closest star, the Sun, depending on the season, (weather dependent)
a tour of the radio astronomy facilities
a visit of exhibition in the Science Centre to learn more about human space flight and the history of the Reservoir
Photos: Sherwood Observatory and used with permission
Difficulty & Distance: Sit and watch the stars go by. The site is broadly accessible but visiting the main telescope involves climbing some steps

Summary:
Enjoy a summer theatre performance with Hope Mill Theatre School in their production of
Matilda
!
Follow Matilda as she starts at a new school and discovers her extraordinary abilities.

When booking, feel free to share your seat number in the comments below if you’d like to sit with others attending the event.

Title: Edward Carpenter Circular Walk
Summary:
Join Mark and Laurance for a scenic walk from the edges of Sheffield into the beautiful Cordwell Valley as we trace the route Edward Carpenter took between the city and his rural retreat at Millthorpe. This isn't just a hike; it’s a bit of a pilgrimage. We’ll be visiting the home where Carpenter lived openly with his partner, George Merrill, and where he famously hosted figures like E.M. Forster and George Bernard Shaw.
Route Highlights
Totley Hall Lane: Our starting point, leading us away from the city into the green "lung" of the Peak District fringes.
The Cordwell Valley: A stunning, often overlooked valley of ancient woodland and rolling fields.
Millthorpe: We’ll stop near Carpenter’s house where he lived the "simple life," grew his own veg, and handmade his famous sandals.
The Royal Oak: A perfect spot for a quick refreshment in the hamlet of Millthorpe.
Smeekley Wood: We will head back to Totley via this historic woodland
New Members:
If this is your first event or you are new to OutdoorLads we’d love to see you. Please message me if you have any questions.
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
Waitlist:
If this event is fully booked and you really want to attend, add yourself to the waitlist because places often become available. If you are on the list but can no longer come, please cancel your attendance as soon as possible to allow waitlisted people to attend in your place.
Image Credits: Most images taken by the event leader with permission for ODL use.
Edward Carpenter Public Domain,
https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=399785
Edward Carpenter and Merrill By Unknown author - Own work, Public Domain,
https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=52263389
Difficulty & Distance: Total distance is around 15km (9.5 miles) with 435 m (1430 feet) of ascent. Approximately 5 hours. Some paths over fields are uneven and may be boggy in wet conditions.

Title: Theatre Night: Boarding School Life as a Homo
Summary:
A relaxed Friday evening theatre trip to the Burton Taylor Studio, one of Oxford’s smaller and more intimate venues.
We’ll be seeing
An Adequate Abridgement of Boarding School Life as a Homo
— a sharp, fast-paced solo show exploring identity, sexuality and growing up in the British boarding school system.
We’ll meet beforehand for a quick drink at the Red Lion, then head over to the theatre together. After the show, we’ll grab some casual food nearby — something easy like Nando’s or similar.
Tickets are £14 (or £12 for certain concessions) and should be booked individually via the
Oxford Playhouse website
.
You are booking for the performance on Friday 24th April at 19:30.
Tickets are low cost, but the venue is small so early booking is recommended.

Title: Isle of Portland Climbing and Walking Weekend (3 nights)
Summary:
Join us for an exciting
three-night
weekend of climbing and walking on the tiny island of Portland, Dorset, well-known for its rugged beauty, long coastline, and incredible climbing. Our accommodation is located across the street from the beach, so a lovely setting in which to enjoy the summer!
David (
david209)
will lead the outdoor climbing events on Saturday and Sunday. Spaces are limited to 14 climbers on each day, including two leaders. It is a great opportunity for newer members and novice climbers to meet and learn from more experienced OutdoorLads.
Please sign-up to your preferred
climbing
events separately (below):
Saturday climbing
Sunday climbing
Peter (
MaccMan
) will lead the walking events on Saturday and Sunday.
Please sign up to your preferred
walking
events separately (below):
Saturday walk
- Maiden Castle and the South Dorset Ridgeway
Sunday walk
- Abbotsbury and Chesil Beach
The bunk rooms are simple, each with a small sink and private shower. Bedding and towels are provided. There is a spacious communal lounge and kitchen, as well as toilets, all on the first floor.
Please note
, this is a three-night hostel event from Friday evening to Monday morning to allow two full days of activities over the weekend.
Waitlist:
If an event is fully booked, please
add yourself to the waitlist.
Places often become available closer to the date. If you decide not to attend,
cancel your attendance
as soon as possible to allow someone else to take your place.
Regrettably, the hostel does not have disabled access due to very narrow hallways and all communal space being on the first floor with no lift access.
Dogs:
Sorry, dogs are not permitted at this accommodation.

Photos:
Courtesy of
www.unsplash.com
, The Bunker and Derventio, and used with permission and thanks.

Title: A Day of Climbing in Portland - Saturday (Beginners Welcome)
Summary:
Join
David
and
Neil
on the stunning island of Portland for some adrenaline-pumping sport climbing! This is a fantastic opportunity to get out on the famous limestone cliffs overlooking the English Channel. Whether you're a seasoned sport climber or have been leading indoors and want to venture on to some real rock, this event is perfect for you.
For those who haven't done sport climbing before, there will be a quick tutorial session at the start of the event, you just need to be competent at tying in, top rope belaying and have a pair of climbing shoes. If you've done three or more indoor climbing events in recent history, that's about all the experience you need. If you are unsure about the difficulty of the event, or whether you have necessary skills & experience, please message the event leader.
On this day, we will explore some of the best climbing areas that Portland has to offer. There will be a range of grades available to cater to all levels of experience, so if you haven't climbed outdoors before, just let the organiser know when you sign up so we can make sure we have a good mix of experiences.
The event is part of an OutdoorLads hostel weekend, with some members taking part in a walking event. After the day's climbing, we will return to the Bunker Hostel for a well-deserved rest and enjoy a friendly and sociable evening. Make sure that if you'd like to stay in the hostel, you
book the hostel event separately here.
IMPORTANT!
Please note that every person participating should be aware of (and accept) that those members perceived as ‘in charge’ or leading the event are not necessarily qualified, do not need to be qualified, and are not regarded by OutdoorLads, or themselves as ‘technical experts’ but are amateur climbers with some experience and who are happy to impart their knowledge. Any advice given should be considered with this in mind by the recipient.
Photos:
Taken by OutdoorLads members and used with full permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Climbing requires a reasonable level of fitness and agility

Title: Maiden Castle and the South Dorset Ridgeway
Summary:
Join us for a circular walk near Dorchester that includes Maiden Castle, a length of the South Dorset Ridgeway and the Hardy Monument!
The route is 11.5 miles / 18.5 kilometres, with 430 metres of ascent.
This walk is associated with an ODL hostel event on the Isle of Portland, but local ODL members who are not on the hostel event
are welcome to join this walk
.
Maiden Castle
is the largest and most impressive Iron Age hillfort in Britain, covering an area bigger than 50 football pitches.  Work on the complex defensive site began about 2,400 years ago and continued for 300 years.  When it was first built, the gleaming white chalk ramparts would have towered over the surrounding landscape.  At the hillfort’s peak of development, hundreds of people lived there in a settlement of thatched buildings. Many had central hearths for cooking, warmth and light.
The
South Dorset Ridgeway
is part of the
South West Coast Path National Trail
and was the original route until a lower level path was created.  It now provides an alternative, higher level, route with great views of the Jurassic Coast and also of the inland scenery of river valleys and chalk downland.  This landscape was well known to the author
Thomas Hardy
(1840-1928) who used it as the background to several of his novels.
The highpoint of today’s walk is the impressive
Hardy Monument
, built in 1844 in memory of Vice-Admiral Sir
Thomas Masterman Hardy
, Captain of HMS Victory at the Battle of Trafalgar.  Hardy is remembered because, amongst other things, it was in his arms that Nelson died, saying the immortal words ‘Kiss me, Hardy’.
The Hardy Monument is a stone tower, built on the summit of Black Down and designed to look like a spyglass, which Admiral Hardy would have used on board ship.  It is 22 metres / 72 feet high and may be open for us to climb its 120 steps when we pass by.  On a clear day you can see Devon and the the Isle of Wight, both of which are around 40 miles / 65 kilometres away, and to the north Pen Hill in the Mendip Hills, which is a similar distance away.
Dogs
: well-behaved dogs are welcome on the walk, but must be under close control at all times and on a lead in the vicinity of livestock or where wildlife may be disturbed.
Waitlist
: if this event is fully booked and you want to join us, add yourself to the wait list because places usually become available closer to the event date.

Title: Abbotsbury and Chesil Beach
Summary:
Join us for a circular walk along part of Chesil Beach, returning along part of the South Dorset Ridgeway!
The route is 9 miles / 15 kilometres, with 300 metres of ascent.
This walk is associated with an ODL hostel event on the Isle of Portland, but local ODL members who are not on the hostel event
are welcome to join this walk
.
One of the most significant features along Dorset’s 95-mile
Jurassic Coast
is
Chesil Beach
.  The name Chesil is derived from the Old English
ceosel
or
cisel
, meaning gravel or shingle.  Over 18 miles / 29 kilometres long and up to 220 yards / 200 metres wide, the beach is estimated to be 100 million tonnes of pebbles.  It rises to 16 yards / 15 metres above sea level.  Thought to date back some 6,000 years, the beach is the result of onshore currents and rising sea levels.  It is moving inland at a rate of 6 yards / 5 metres per century.
Interestingly, the pebbles are naturally graded in size from fist-sized near Portland (at the eastern end of the beach) to pea-sized at Bridport (at the western end of the beach). The pebbles are mainly a mix of flint and chert.  The beach shelters the
Fleet
, which is Britain’s largest tidal lagoon.  The water is up to 3 metres deep. The Fleet is home to many wading birds and the
Abbotsbury Swannery
.  This is a managed colony of nesting swans, thought to have been established by monks at the former monastery of Abbotsbury.
The
South Dorset Ridgeway
is part of the
South West Coast Path National Trail
and was the original route (the route around Portland was only created in 2003).  It now provides an alternative route to walking over the pebbles of Chesil Beach, with great views of the Jurassic Coast and also of the inland scenery of river valleys and chalk downland.  This landscape was well known to the author
Thomas Hardy
(1840-1928) who used it as the background to several of his stories.
The walk starts and ends in the village of
Abbotsbury
, which is known for its picturesque historic buildings and thatched cottages.  It was the site of a monastery, which fell victim to King Henry VIII’s dissolution of monastic establishments in the mid-16th century.  There are some remains of the abbey, including a large tithe barn (built circa 1400) and St Catherine’s Chapel, a 14th century pilgrimage chapel that stands on a hill between the village and the coast.
Dogs
: well-behaved dogs are welcome on the walk, but must be under close control at all times and on a lead in the vicinity of livestock or where wildlife may be disturbed.
Waitlist

Title: Mid-Summer Camping on the Canal
Summary:
Instead of our traditional July cider pub camping, join ODL as we visit a canal-side pub, between Pewsey and Devizes.  This is a new location for us, on the banks of the Kennett and Avon Canal, and not far from the Alton Barnes White Horse.
There is live music Friday night; currently scheduled to appear are The Blacksmiths, a 5 piece Hampshire based rock & folk band.  Here is a
video
of their song
Cam Ye' O'er Frae France.
Hikes will be confirmed closer to the event but may include this 9.75 mile
Vale of Pewsey
loop, this 8.25 mile
White Horse and All Cannings
loop, or for a real leg-stretcher this 12.5 mile
Honeystreet to West Kennet
loop.
Should you wish to not cook, the pub has a full menu (available Friday until 20.00, Saturday from 12.00 to 14.30 and 17.30 to 20.30 only; 12.00 to 16.00 Sunday) including burgers, "light bites", salads and traditional pub favourites.  A full drinks menu is available throughout the day.
Some of the site rules are:
Quiet hours are between 23.00 and 07.00
Campfires and barbecues are allowed, but must be raised off the ground
Tents must be a minimum of 3 metres apart
Cars can be parked at the pitches
If this is your first event, don't be put off if you lack some of the necessary equipment; just message me on
chris.keivit@outdoorlads.com
or
james.couldry@outdoorlads.com
and we will see what can be worked out!
Dogs:
The Inn's camping area is dog-friendly although they must be kept on a lead at all times (you
must
advise
support@outdoorlads.com
to book your four-legged friend, there is no additional charge).
Caravans and motorhomes:
Are welcome, subject to site availability; if you need an EHU, there is an additional charge of £6 per night (no charge if no EHU is needed). You
must contact
support@outdoorlads.com
to reserve for campervans and caravans. Fire drums are available for hire, and fire wood is available for purchase.  I will confirm closer to the event if we are able to bring in our own wood.
Want to Get Out More?
Why not check out the South Wales and South West Calendar
here
.
Campfires & BBQ's
: This event will include a campfire/BBQ at the site.  Whilst our event leaders will take safety precautions there is still a risk to members. Please consider your behaviour around the campfire for your own and others safety.
Photos:
provided by Outdoorlads Members.
Difficulty & Distance: Canalside Camping

Title: Summer Solstice Weekend Camping in the Cotswolds
Summary:
A lovely campsite nestled below the Cotswold escarpment. It's on a fruit farm which also has an onsite cafe and shop. Nearby are a ruined abbey, medieval church frescoes, idylic Cotswold hamlets and villages (used for film/tv period dramas), grand country houses with landscape features, a heritage steam railway (also used for film/tv) and a pub we can walk to across the fields. There is a village store within two miles for any supplies you need.
If we are blessed with good weather there are stunning views down the Vale of Evesham looking out towards the Malverns and into Wales. We will see and hear the steam train whistling and puffing its way through the landscape.
We have two evenings on this idyllic site and Sunday 21st will be the Summer Solstice - the longest day of the year. Late chats around the fire-pit but please note the owners ask there be no noise after 11pm and that all firewood is purchased from the onsite shop.
Lots of walking opportunities from the campsite, almost entirely off road and whilst the escarpment itself is only about 200m we may ascend and descend this multiples times. Opportunities for a pub stop on the way back and/or an extended lunch break of your choice in a nearby picture-postcard-perfect town with cafes, pubs, delis and a supermarket. There are a couple of optional exciting possibilities for our main walk on Saturday - these will incur an extra cost to be paid direct to the venue. More info will be published here once I can make a reservation for us.
There are also excellent road cycling routes in the area including extended flatter sections and hill climbs if that is your thing but this is not officially part of the event and at your own risk/judgement.
Dogs:
Dogs are welcome on the campsite but are subject to an additional charge of £1 per night each paid directly to the campsite on arrival. Dogs will need to be kept under control throughout the event. You must let me know if you intend to bring your dog as we must pre-inform the owners.
Gazebos:
Please note this campsite makes an additional charge for Gazebos.   You are welcome to bring one but will need to pay the fee of £14 per night directly to the campsite on arrival. I will bring one big gazebo/canopy for our camp.

Title: 3 Night hostel high in the hills
Summary:
A
three night
hostel over the Early May Bank Holiday weekend on the Pennine Way, in this very popular and comfortable hostel!
Come and enjoy spending your bank holiday in this stunning location, with fellow OutdoorLads. This weekend promises superb views of the North Pennines AONB, great walks in this beautiful landscape, and hopefully the late Spring weather will be kind to us. The area offers a variety of challenge in the walks, but we will get out for at least one proper fell walk on the Saturday - it's a great area for a really top-notch walk!
Alston shares the title of highest market town in England (along with Buxton) - even though there is no actual market held anymore - sitting high on the Pennine Way, with Northumberland to the east and the Eden Valley and on to the Lakes Fells to the west. Alston was historically a centre for mining, with lead, zinc, iron, copper and coal found in the vicinity. Remains of all can be seen in the area if you know where to look. It has also been used for filming of League Of Gentleman's Royston Vasey.
Our accommodation is a recently renovated, comfortable hostel right in the village, with facilities (shops, cafes, pubs) on the doorstep should we need them. There are a range of different sized bedrooms, comfortable social spaces, cycle storage and outside space to sit. We will be aiming to have a "proper" fell walk as the main activity on the Saturday.  This will be decided upon taking into account the weather and group wishes. Details of the walk will be shared at the Friday welcome meeting.
As we have a bonus day, options for Sunday include a shorter walk, but we could also be checking out the South Tynedale narrow gauge railway (35 mins to Slaggyford costs around £16 for day rover ticket), or heading underground to explore lead mining at
Nenthead Mines.
(More details on that as numbers are confirmed and if the mine volunteer guides are available).
The  evenings will be sociable and relaxed so you may want to bring board games, cards etc. Someone is sure to bring Uno !
Dogs:
Normally this is a "no dogs allowed" hostel but they have agreed that there is space for two dogs. The leader will be taking his dog, so one dog space remaining. You must contact the leader if considering bringing your dog, before booking please. Just so we don't exceed numbers.
Photos:
Photos used by OutdoorLads with full permission of the leaders
Difficulty & Distance: The walks will more be of fell than mountain terrain

Title: London Bouldering Morning - All Abilities welcome!
Summary:
Come and join OutdoorLads for some bouldering in central London!
If you have not been to one of the London Climbing Centres you will need to pre-register
HERE
. You only need to register once to use any of their 8 centres in London.
This event is for members of all levels of experience and all are welcome to come along to any of the sessions.
Bouldering is a form of climbing where instead of long high routes, the 'problems', as they are referred to, tend to be shorter and focus on technique, balance, body positioning and sometimes power. Because of this different emphasis, bouldering doesn't have the hassle of climbing with ropes, harnesses and belaying, which makes the activities excellent for newcomers to the sport.
If you're a seasoned climber you won't need convincing about the benefits of bouldering, but for those who've never climbed more than a staircase, the benefits are wide ranging: from the physical - improved strength, tone, balance, power and coordination; mental - problem solving, focus, concentration and body awareness; to the emotional - relaxation, a sense of achievement and a feeling that mind and body are working together perfectly ('feeling psyched'). On top of all this, bouldering tends to be a very social sport where you can hang out with mates and each have a go as you feel regardless of what level you climb at.
First time boulderer?
If you have never tried bouldering before, the event leader can give you a short introduction to get you started and give a few a few pointers on technique. After which we will join in with the rest of the group.
All those attending the event must also be aware of the centre's own terms and conditions of participation and abide by them, along with any instructions given by the centre’s staff. Everyone must also take responsibility for their own safety while participating in Indoor climbing events.
Experienced boulderer?
Simply register with the wall and turn up on the day!
As above, all attending the event must abide by the centre's T&Cs etc. As an experienced boulderer you are at all times responsible for your own safety and so should use your own judgement in taking up advice given either by OutdoorLads volunteers or other experienced climbers.
Difficulty & Distance: With climbs at every grade from complete beginner to seasoned pro, you decide how hard it gets!

Title: Big Tree Country Weekend
Summary:
Travel to the heart of Perthshire, where the lowlands meet the highlands in what is known as Big Tree Country!
We have
three nights
away to discover the delights of the area, and probably get out for a few walks..... For the mountain goats Schiehallion (Fairy Hill), Glen Lyon round and the Ben Lawers range are nearby, or there are plenty of easier options in the Tay Forest Park such as the Birks of Aberfeldy or along the River Tay by Kenmore to the shore of Loch Tay. You could even take a short drive and discover the excitement of the village of Dull - twinned with the USA town of Boring & Bland in NSW Australia as part of the 'Trinity of Tedium'!
Photos:
With permission to use for this event page from Glassie Farm, Thomas Haywood & AMD
- - - - - - - - - - - - - - -
Want to keep in touch with what's going on in Scotland?
Join our Telegram
chat
and
notification
groups, and bookmark our upcoming events list via our
Scottish Calendar
.
Difficulty & Distance: Three nights in Perthshire

Title: Harrisons Rocks and the Sussex High Weald
Summary:
A moderate 11 mile hike taking in part of the Sussex High Weald landscape in a loop around Ashurst Rail Station (50minutes from London Bridge).  Making our way through wooded valleys, we will cross chalk streams and heritage rail tracks before eating our packed lunches at Harrisons Rocks, a large sandstone outcrop popular with local climbers (including ODL!).
Passing by the picturesque Groomsbridge Place, a 17th Century moated manor house, there is an opportunity for a refreshment drink at the nearby Crown Inn, before continuing back to Ashurst Rail station.
Dogs : this walk is suitable for dogs.
Photos: all photos taken personally by the walk leader
Difficulty & Distance: 11 Miles with 250 metres elevation

Title: From Heathland High Society to a Victorian Valhalla
Summary:
An urban walk in three parts, the last of which is open and hilly enough to suggest a rural one. Part 1 is a tour of Hampstead Village, the pretty, leafy home of the liberal intelligentsia such as DH Lawrence and John Keats. Part 2 is a tour of where many of such people ended up, the fashionable metropolitan necropolis of Highgate Cemetery. Part 3 crosses an 'island' of wild-seeming countryside, Hampstead Heath, which Victorian campaigners protected from development and facilitated access for all, something which suave Kenwood House, being free to enter, also offers. Not accessible for all is the Men's Bathing Pond, which is where we'll end the walk and where you might want to cool off.
The sights and
route
(please click the link to go to the Ordnance Survey website)
:
Part 1: Hampstead Village
(This part is borrowed, with some changes, from
33 Walks in London That You Shouldn't Miss
by Nicola Perry.)
We'll start in the centre of leafy, literary Hampstead on Hampstead High Street, outside...
1. Hampstead Underground Station:
Designed in his signature oxblood-red glazed tiles by Leslie Green in 1907. Its platforms are the deepest on the London Underground network, at 58.5 metres below ground level, and with the deepest lift shaft at 55 metres.
We'll turn down Church Row, one of the loveliest pockets of Hampstead, to see...
2. HG Wells's House:
No. 17, a lovely Queen Anne house where he wrote
The History of Mr Polly
and would have received George Bernard Shaw and the author Rebecca West who became Wells's lover.
And then...
3. St John-at-Hampstead:
The parish church, designed in 1747 and expanded in 1870 and 1911, In 1958 the dark Victorian interior decoration was removed and the lighter, whitewashed scheme closer to the original appearance was reinstated. Elegant and playful in white with gold details, the baroque interior is delightful. The enchanting churchyard teems with notable burials too many to list here (so see
here
) including the pianist Alfred Brendel, the painter John Constable, the actor Peter Cook, the Labour leader Hugh Gaitskell, the actor and presenter Nicholas Parsons, and the measurer of longitude John Harrison. We'll get time to search for some graves.
We'll then walk downhill along Frognal Way and enjoy views over London, plus...
4. Gracie Fields's House:
No. 20, an attractive inter-war villa for the Lancastrian star of cinema and music hall, 'Our Gracie'.
On Frognal is...
5. 66 Frognal:
Modern house from 1937-38 built to a design by the architects Connell Ward & Lucas. Grade II* listed. Hugely influential two decades later.
We'll circle back round to the Parish Church, along Church Row, and turn briefly up Frognal Gardens and back to see...
6. Alastair Sim's House:
A Victorian house where the Scottish actor of famously comically sinister roles lived.
Back on Church Row,  we'll turn left up Holly Walk. We'll then turn right on to Mount Vernon and left on Frognal Rise then cross the street to Windmill Hill to see...
7. Bolton House:
The home of the poet an dramatist Joanna Baillie, one of the first women to be honoured with a blue plaque. Visitors to the house would've included Wordsworth, Byron and her near-neighbour, Keats.
The next street is Admiral's Walk, in which we'll see...
8. Admiral's House:
The roof resembles a ship's quarterdeck and the occupant, one Lieutenant Fountain North, would fire a cannon from it to celebrate the anniversaries of British naval victories and royal birthdays, the inspiration for Admiral Boom in
Mary Poppins
.
At the end of Admiral's Walk, we'll turn right on to Hampstead Grove, another street of beautiful houses and see...
9. Fenton House:
An exceedingly handsome C17 house with unusual collections and picturesque walled gardens owned by the National Trust. (We won't have time to enter.)
We'll get back on Holly Hill and go back down the lane of Holly Mount to go to...
10. The Holly Bush:
A famous C18 tavern, still lit by gas lamps and frequented by figures such as Charles Dickens and Samuel Johnson.
We'll cross back over Back Lane to Flask Walk to see another pub...
11. The Flask:
A pub mentioned in Samuel Richardson's scandalous 1748 novel
Clarissa
, a tragic story of a young woman, Clarissa Harlowe, whose quest for virtue is continually thwarted by her grasping, status-conscious family. The street it's on is a haven of old-world exclusivity and charm.
Further along Flask Walk is...
12. The Old Bathhouse:
A sign reading 'The Wells & Campden Trust Bath & Washhouses 1888', is a reminder of Hampstead's function as a spa town.
At Well Walk, well see...
13. Burgh House:
A Queen Anne house Built in 1703 and later occupied by the daughter of Rudyard Kipling, it is now Hampstead Museum and a licensed buttery.
We'll then turn left up New End Square and right up Well Road to see...
14. Daphne Du Maurier's House:
A Regency house where the novelist lived in the 1930s.
Taking an alleyway right off Well Road, we'll get to back to Well Walk to see...
15. The Chalybeate Spring:
a source of iron-rich water exploited as a clean, if bitter, water source.
And almost opposite the spring...
16. John Constable's House:
One of two residences the painter had in Hampstead from 1819 to 1837.
Turning back down Well Road, we'll turn left down Christchurch Hill an on to Willow Road, where we'll find...
17. No. 2 Willow Road:
The family home of Ernö Goldfinger, which he designed along with numbers 1 and 3, with Gerald Wanklyn Flower, in 1937. Goldfinger was a Hungarian-born British architect of startlingly modern buildings such as Trellick Tower and this, a house of movable partitions, folding doors and art by Max Ernst and Henry Moore, which visitors can all see all unchanged since the 1930s courtesy of the National Trust.
Turning right down Downshire Hill, we'll find...
18. Lee Miller and Roland Penrose's House:
No. 21, an end-of-terrace Regency house, in which the
Vogue
war correspondent Lee Miller lived with surrealist painter Sir Roland Penrose and another lover in a ménage à trois. A place of parties which the art world elite, including Pablo Picasso, attended in the 1930s and 1940s.
And further down the road, the church of...
19. St John's, Downshire Hill:
Grade I-listed. Regency stuccoed, cream-painted facade. Its notable features include a Doric porch, portico and cupola. The church has no recessed chancel or pulpit characteristic of the emphasis on preaching in evangelical Anglican ('low') churches.
Turning east down Keats' Grove, we'll reach...
20. Keats' House:
No. 10, where the young poet came to lodge in 1817 after he met Charles Wentworth Dilke and Leigh Hunt who introduced him to Shelley. While living here, Keats met the love of is life, Fanny Brawne, a member of the Dilke Family. It is said that while wandering the garden here he composed "Ode to a Nightingale" and another poems. The lovers became engaged only for Keats to die in Rome of tuberculosis before they could marry.
Then, escaping the village, we'll use a track between Hampstead Ponds to reach the open expanse of...
Interlude - lunchtime
21. Hampstead Heath:
Four miles from Central London and covering 330 hectares (790 acres). It survives as a fairly untamed-seeming island of countryside in the urban sprawl thanks to the efforts of Victorian campaigners. It sits astride a sandy ridge, one of the highest points in London running from Hampstead to Highgate, which rests on a band of London Clay. The heath is rambling and hilly, embracing ponds, recent and ancient woodlands, a lido, playgrounds, and a training track, and it adjoins the former stately home of Kenwood House and its estate. The famous cruising ground (where George Michael admitted going) is behind Jack Straw's Castle pub on the West Heath, which we won't be visiting.
And on to...
22. Parliament Hill:
The gateway to the Heath. The famous panoramic view of London, including the Square Mile, is a view protected by law, and one of
England's 100 Best Views
, according to Simon Jenkins. The name comes from the story that it was the spot that Guy Fawkes told his band of conspirators to meet to see the destruction of Parliament on 5 November, 1605. We'll have lunch on the grass on that same spot.
Part 2: Highgate Village and Cemetery
We'll leave the Heath at Highgate West Hill and follow Swain's Lane all the way to the equally desirable and prosperous village of Highgate, including...
23. Highgate Cemetery:
Established in 1839 as part of London’s 'Magnificent Seven'. Famed for its Gothic Revival design and romantic, overgrown landscape. Designed by Stephen Geary, it includes the dramatic Egyptian Avenue and Circle of Lebanon. It became a fashionable Victorian burial ground, with elaborate monuments reflecting status and taste. Notable interments include Karl Marx (reinterred 1883 and again in 1956), the scientist Michael Faraday, the author George Eliot, the presenter Jeremy Beadle and the author Douglas Adams. After mid-C20 decline, restoration began in 1975 under the Highgate Cemetery Trust which charges £10 to visit. We will have a two hour-long self-guided tour of the West and East Cemeteries. I will provide you with maps of the graves.
If you would like to visit the cemetery, please be prepared to pay the entrance fee of £10 nearer the time when I have made the group payment.
Continuing up Swain's Lane, to South Grove, we'll reach...
24. The Scientific and Literary Society of Highgate
: Founded in 1839, and embodying Victorian ideals of education and self-improvement. Its premises include a lecture hall and library. It hosted lectures, debates, and exhibitions, forming a centre of local intellectual life.
Walking left along Pond Square and left down Highgate West Hill, we'll see...
25. The Flask, Highgate:
C17, The Flask developed alongside Highgate’s spa culture, when mineral waters attracted visitors. The present building reflects Georgian and later alterations. It is associated with William Hogarth, who is said to have stayed or visited, and with local legends of the highwayman Dick Turpin, who reputedly frequented the area.
Then after turning right down The Grove, we'll see...
26. George Michael's House:
No. 5, a house from 1688 and remodelled late C18, the home of the singer George Michael, who lived here from 2002 until his death on Christmas Day in 2016. The house became a site of public mourning, with fans leaving tributes.
And almost next door is...
27. Samuel Taylor Coleridge's House:
No. 3, a late C18 Georgian townhouse reflecting Highgate's emergence as a desirable suburban retreat. The poet lived here from 1816 until his death in 1834. The house thus became a site of literary importance, where Coleridge wrote, received visitors, and spent his final years.
Down Highgate West Hill is...
28. St Michael’s Church:
Highgate Parish Church, built in 1832 to reflect Highgate’s growth as a suburban parish. Poet Samuel Taylor Coleridge worshipped here while living nearby in the early 19th century, reinforcing Highgate’s strong literary associations during this period of expansion.
Walking southwest down Holly Terrace, then turning right down Merton Lane, we'll reach Millfield Lane and turn right onto a track across the Heath to...
Part 3: Hampstead Heath, Kenwood House and the Men's Pond
29. Kenwood House:
Originally a C17 villa, transformed between 1764–1779 by Robert Adam for the Earl of Mansfield into a neoclassical masterpiece. Its celebrated library exemplifies Adam’s refined decorative scheme. The estate later passed to the Iveagh family, who gifted it to the nation in 1927. Set within landscaped grounds, it houses a major art collection, including works by Rembrandt, Vermeer and Turner. It is owned by English Heritage but admission is free. I am in the process of booking in the group of us for 16.00 (my thanks go to Alex Young for his advice here). Find out more about the house
here
.
Crossing the Heath south, we'll pass the...
30. Highgate Open Air Theatre:
Established in 1956, it reflects post-war community arts initiatives. Set within a garden environment, it is designed to integrate performance with nature. Known for seasonal productions, particularly Shakespeare, it offers an intimate and informal theatrical experience.
Then, veering southeast past the Model Boating Pond, we'll reach our last stop...
31. The Men’s Pond:
Created in the early C18 by damming streams to form reservoirs, the Men’s Pond later became designated for male bathing. Surrounded by woodland, it preserves a naturalistic character distinct from formal swimming facilities. Swimming costs £4.80, and the water is opaque, deep and unheated, and lifeguards are present. There are also showers. The temperature should be about 17°C in early June. Find out more
here
.
If you don't want to go swimming, you can head back west across the Heath to Hampstead Underground Station where we started, which is also the route we'll follow if you do.
Booking swimming at Highgate Men's Pond:
A bookable slot system for swimming comes into operation in May. Pay £4.80 and book your ticket for the evening session on Saturday 6 June
here
if you wish to swim (the walk will finish later than 18.30 if you do).
Disclaimer regarding swimming:
Although appearing to be a wild swimming location, Highgate Men's Pond is owned and managed by the City of London. The website states that 'the water is deep and opaque and suitable for competent swimmers only', and contains additional guidance for cold water swimming, but lifeguards are in attendance and the venue has public liability insurance.
Dogs:
This walk is partly suitable for dogs. The urban nature of it with lots of stops might prove boring for them, and they are not permitted in Highgate Cemetery or Kenwood House. They will enjoy the stretches across the Heath.

Difficulty & Distance: Distance is 13 km (8.5 miles); total ascent is 302 m; relief is moderately hilly; terrain is gravel, grass, tarmac and concrete.

Title: Liverpool Social
Summary:
Join us for our Liverpool Social in the City Centre!
We gather at a, friendly bar-restaurant on St John Lane, Liverpool. With a great selection of food, and drinks, it’s the perfect spot to relax and enjoy a chilled-out atmosphere.
This is a brilliant chance to meet new people, catch up with familiar faces, and chat about upcoming events. More than just a night out, it’s a space to connect and strengthen our supportive community.
Come along, grab a drink, and give it a go!
If you’ve got any questions, feel free to message the event leader 😊
All Photos owned and given permission to use by event leader
Difficulty & Distance: Social event

Title: Gay City Bowlers  Leeds - April
Summary:
OutdoorLads joins the UK's premier gay bowling night, Gay City Bowlers, for a fun social night in Leeds
It doesn't matter about skill level or experience - it's a fun inclusive night! We will meet at Roxy Lanes and will be allocated into lanes for two games.
There is a friendly competition across the night and the top 3 scorers of the night win medals. After the bowling we will gather to finish off the evening with a refreshing beverage.
More details about Gay City Bowlers, including details of events in other cities, can be found on their
Instagram
page.
IMPORTANT:
You must purchase your ticket for this event through the
Gay City Bowlers website
and then
sign up on this event page
! If you don't purchase a ticket prior to the event you will not be able to take part.
The tickets are £20 for two games. £1 is added by Gay City Bowlers to support their events across the UK, you can remove this or donate more.
Interested in keeping up-to-date with GCB news? Join their WhatsApp Community Group for Leeds:
https://chat.whatsapp.com/HfJVoLq7GhW6XqgEEfzJhU?mode=ems_copy_t
Image credits: Images supplied by OutdoorLads members, Gay City Bowlers and Pixaby Free Use - used with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: All bowling abilities welcome.

Title: Southampton April social
Summary:
Making a return to our favourite social venue, we return to Maritimo Lounge in Ocean Village for drinks and food - self described as a relaxed, family friendly venue with casual dining in an eclectic, quirky setting.
This is a perfect event for members old and new to catch up with each other.
We'll also have details of forthcoming events celebrating ODL's 20th anniversary!
We'll likely head to the London Hotel afterwards for a drink!
To see all the walking events in the southeast, please click this
link
.
Photographs courtesy of Maritimo Lounge website / User Geeky James

Title: BIG20: New Forest Celebration Weekend!
Summary:
It's OutdoorLads' 20th anniversary! Come join us for a Summer weekend in the New Forest to celebrate this huge milestone... the BIG 20 events this year are a showcase of some of our very best events from the last 20 years, where we can enjoy a weekend celebrating all that OutdoorLads has to offer with some great activities and a few little surprises to mark the occasion, too!
We have a full weekend of events, details will be added once confirmed:
New Forest Walk 1
New Forest Walk 2
Cultural & Heritage event - visiting Beaulieu House & Motor Museum
Paddle to Pub on the Beaulieu River (TBC)
Cycle through the forest (subject to leader availability)
Wander into Brockenhurst to sample its cafes and pubs
Brockenhurst Park Run
Visit the beautiful coast
The Accommodation
We have used this bunkhouse/hostel a number of times and you can expect dormitory style accommodation with some spare beds available allowing us to spread-out throughout the building, there is a decent size lounge and dining area.  Showers are in a separate block 10 metres from the Bunkhouse
Itinerary
The following is all subject to change but should give you an idea of what to expect if this is your first ODL hostel event.
Friday
You'll be able to arrive at the site from around 4.30pm on the Friday, and find a bed in the dorms. Once settled in, there is plenty of room to relax in the lounge, with hot drinks available throughout the weekend. If your preference is for an alcoholic tipple you'll need to bring these yourself.
Friday evening will be the traditional OutdoorLads buffet. Bring along something to feed one or two people, and home-cooked is always preferred.
Once we've had a briefing over the plans for the weekend, we'll be looking for some volunteers to tidy up the kitchen and dining room, whilst the others can head back to relax in the lounge (or outside if its warm!), chat to others or perhaps enjoy some board games (don't forget to bring your favourites!)
Saturday
After breakfast on Saturday morning you'll head out on your choice of activity (we'll list these out above as they are confirmed), returning to our accommodation early afternoon. This means you will likely need a packed lunch that you will need to bring yourself.
For those of you that like your Parkrun on a Saturday morning, Brockenhurst has you covered and we'll time our daytime activities to give you a chance to do both.
OutdoorLads hostel events always rely on everyone mucking in and helping in the kitchen over the weekend, so whilst a few volunteers are busy in the kitchen, we'll hopefully have some anniversary themed activities in the lounge.
We will provide dinner for the Saturday night, so please make sure your dietary requirements are up to date. We'll also raise a glass to OutdoorLads tonight with dinner! We will need some volunteers to clear up in the kitchen and dining room, but then it's a chance to natter to fellow OutdoorLads, listen to some music and likely that there will be board games available to partake in. Being mid-Summer, lets hope it's nice and we can enjoy being outside again, too!
Sunday
Start the day with breakfast and as much tea/coffee as needed to recover from the night before, and then we'll spend an hour or so tidying up the venue and leaving it as clean as we found it, we will need to be out by midday.
Our plan is to offer a short walk or cultural event on the Sunday for those that are not in a hurry to leave, but this is subject to volunteers stepping up to lead this for you!
Good to Know....
Dogs
Sorry, this event is not suitable for dogs. Registered animals are welcome, but please let us know at the time of booking if you will be bringing an assistance dog.
Car Sharing
The is parking available but sharing is good, so if driving to the event please consider offering spare seats to other members heading the same way.
Want to Get Out More?
To be informed of all our Southeast events please click on this
Telegram link.
OutdoorLads Foundation
Fancy coming along but having a difficult time of it (for whatever reason), and/or finding it hard to justify the expense? Apply for a place on this event through the OutdoorLads Foundation! Nobody apart from staff members will know and we don't need lots of financial info from you either. To apply through the Foundation, you'll need to complete a very short form, which can be found along with more information
here
.
Want to Pay By Instalments?
You can arrange to pay in instalments if that helps. Please contact the office on
support@outdoorlads.com
who will help you to secure a place and set up paying in instalments. The event must be fully paid up a week before the event takes place.
Images:
Big thanks to Colin, Glenn and James (ODL Leaders) for the event photos, used with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: A weekend of events and celebration!

Title: Saturday Sport Climbing in North Wales
Summary:
Whether you're an indoor climber wanting to try the real thing outdoors or a seasoned veteran, Castle Inn has something to offer, with a great selection of well protected routes, with a good bunch at the easier end of the scale.
If you are not confident lead belaying or lead climbing, please message the leader as we may need to balance the number of experienced and novice climbers.
Once we have exhausted all the climbing here, the plan is to head over to Tram Stop Quarry in Llandudno, which is a small crag overlooking the town. It has a smaller selection of routes, but is closer to town and some good pubs which would a fitting way to end the day.
Event full?
Please join the waiting list. Places usually open up as the event gets nearer and, where necessary, we try to add additional leaders to increase the numbers, so you are highly likely to get a place.
Image
/
photo
credits
:  All images (c)
Andy
with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse.
Difficulty & Distance: A sport crag is a good section of climbs at differing grades.

Title: The Fairfield Horseshoe
Summary:
Rescheduled from 29-March-26 to 10-May-26
Join Outdoor Lads for an classic early spring hillwalking day, knocking off seven Wainwrights in the Lake District.
Points of interest on the route:
Rydal Hall:
As we make our way to the fells, we'll pass Rydal Hall, a historic Grade II* listed building with origins in the 16th century. The estate is renowned for its nationally significant grounds, which feature one of Britain's earliest viewing stations, with formal Italianate Arts and Crafts gardens
The Horseshoe:
A classic Lakeland walk that traverses an eight-summit high-level ridge, including the fells of Low Pike, High Pike, Dove Crag, Hart Crag, Fairfield, Great Rigg, Heron Pike, and Nab Scar. The walk offers sweeping panoramic views of the surrounding fells, the entire U-shaped Rydal valley below, and stunning vistas over Windermere and other major lakes.
Scandale Beck:
As we come off the hills, we'll follow this pretty beck back into Ambleside, through some native woodland and then into the town.
Other Infomation:
Dog Suitability:
Dogs are always welcome on my walks, though their behaviour and safety is their owner's responsibility. This includes cleaning up after them, controlling their behaviour around other animals or people, and assisting them over any stiles or streams we may cross.
New Members
We know that new members in particular like to know in advance more about what an OutdoorLads event will be like.
If this is your first OutdoorLads event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the OutdoorLads website.
What if there is a Waiting List?
If this event is fully booked and you want to attend, just add yourself to the waitlist, because places inevitably become available closer to the date.
Difficulty & Distance: This hike is about 19km (12 miles) with ~1000m (3280ft) of ascent. While there's nothing overly difficult on this route, due to the time of year, it may be very wet and muddy or icy.

Title: Cardiff Social
Summary:
Come & join for us a night on the town in Cardiff.
This is the perfect event for members old and new to catch up with each other, find out about upcoming events and let their hair down.
We also encourage potential new members to come along and find out what we are all about. If you've not been on any of our events before, this is a great opportunity to chat to existing members and leaders about the group and to find out how friendly and welcoming we are.
If you are in any way uncertain about joining OutdoorLads, are concerned about fitness/ability or whether OutdoorLads is for you, come along, say hello, have a drink with us and judge for yourself.
We will begin our evening at the Golden Cross pub. Once the main social ends, some or all of us may well decide to head on to other bars or clubs in Cardiff.
If you have any questions before the event, please feel free to drop the Leader a message.
Join us for a night of drinks & laughter at a pub or two around Cardiff.
Want to Get Out More?
Why not check out the South Wales and South West Calendar
here
.
Photo Credits - Photos provided by OutdoorLads members
Difficulty & Distance: It's a social!

Title: “From Quills to Cobblestones: A Stratford Escape”
Summary:
Come and join us for a cultured weekend break in Stratford-upon-Avon, and enjoy fun times exploring the town's rich heritage!
City breaks are less formal and structured than our usual hostel breaks, you’ll have free time or time to do the suggested activities below, but they are all suggestions. City breaks do not have the usual Friday night buffet, (and indeed no food or drink is included in the price) however places to eat as a group will be suggested, or you may wish do your own thing or break into smaller groups.
Stratford-Upon-Avon is a renowned market town famously known as the birthplace and burial place of playwright William Shakespeare. Situated along the River Avon, this historic town draws over 2.7 million visitors annually to experience its Tudor-style architecture, the Royal Shakespeare Company theatres, and various Shakespearean heritage sites.
What’s going to happen
Itinerary:
Day 1:
Arrive late afternoon on Friday, meet & greet reception before heading off out for dinner and drinks.
Day 2:
Saturday morning, Optional Breakfast at the hostel before we head into the town centre for a leisurely
city morning walk
with local guide - (£10 - not included in event price.)
This is followed by an optional afternoon visit to Shakespeare’s Birthplace
Shakespeare's Birthplace
and perhaps afterwards the beautifully picturesque
Anne Hathaway’s Cottage
which is a 30 minute walk away.
In the evening we will reconvene at the hostel before heading to an Optional Ghost tour at 7.30pm at a cost of £12.00
Stratford Town Ghost Walk
or dinner followed by drinks in a few local bars.
Day 3:
Sunday morning, Optional Breakfast at the hostel then free time before checkout - option of a
local short walk
Accommodation
Hostel accommodation - 2 x  6 bed ensuite rooms which will only be shared with those attending the city break.
Please note that unlike most of our other hostel breaks food is not provided and we will not have sole use of the hostel, however you will only be sharing a room with other OutdoorLads members.
Dogs:
Unfortunately isn't an event for our four legged friends. Registered assistance dogs welcome, but please let us know when you book.
Photos:
pixabay users Steve Oprey, Susli and Various Photography plus users GeekyJames & Cassidyi, used by OutdoorLads with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Weekend City Break & Social

Title: A Classic Highland Double from Bridge of Orchy
Summary:
A joint event with the
Gay Outdoor Club
.
Join us for a full‑day mountain adventure tackling two of the West Highlands’ most celebrated Munros: Beinn Dòrain (1,076 m) and Beinn an Dòthaidh (1,004 m).
Rising steeply above the West Highland railway line, these neighbouring peaks offer a spectacular combination of rugged corries, airy ridges, and unforgettable views across Rannoch Moor and the Black Mount.
A challenging but achievable outing for regular hillwalkers with a good fitness level. You can expect steep rocky sections, occasional boggy ground and exposed ridges in places.
We will follow the route described on
WalkHighlands
.
Photos:
taken on previous visits to these hills with ODL in April 2015 and GOC in October 2010.

Title: BIG20: Islands on the Edge
Summary:
Saturday 13th to Saturday 20th June - 7 nights!
Join us for a
week away on the Outer Hebrides
to explore an archipelago of over 100 islands that stretch for 130 miles across the Atlantic Ocean., to
celebrate OutdoorLads turning 20 years old!
This is a trip not to be missed - it'll be a real highlight of your year of adventure!
There is history to discover, with the Callanish Stones dating from around 2900BC, Dun Carloway broch from around 1AD to the Blackhouses from the late 1700s, in which we are staying part of the week.  There are many other sights to take in during our stay on the islands - with otters, birds and sealife to spot, hills to climb, long sandy beaches to wander along and possibly long golden sunsets to take in. We will decide upon activities for each day when we are there, taking into account weather and the desires of the group at the time.
We will spend the first four nights staying at the amazing Gearrannan Blackhouses on Lewis before transferring on Wednesday to the five star accommodation of Nunton House on Benbecula for the remaining three nights.
As is usual for OutdoorLads hostel, we will cook and eat together each night. The cost of this is included in the price, the exception being the first Saturday night when we will be on the ferry and we suggest you buy dinner on the ferry.
Travel
Travel is NOT included in the price. This event page is for the standard hostel event costs only. We will soon make available an event page to book on the minibus. This will include pick ups in Edinburgh, Stirling, and Inverness.  You will also need to pay separately for ferry crossings - please await further travel info when you have signed up before making bookings. The minibus will be used during the event to for trips out as well.
PAY IN INSTALMENTS
If you would prefer to pay in monthly instalments, please
contact the office team
(or
raise a support ticket
) and we can discuss the options available to you, depending on the time available between booking and the event taking place. There will be additional terms and conditions to agree to before you commit. You won't pay anything extra to pay in instalments. You will need to be paid up on month before the event starts.
Dogs:
This event is not suitable for dogs.
Photos :
Taken by AMD and used by OutdoorLads with full permission.

Title: Outdoor First Aid Course for Leaders - Glasgow
Summary:
This 16-hour outdoor first aid course deals with issues involved with the long term care of casualties in a remote setting. It is often referred to as a Wilderness First Aid or a Remote First Aid.
The outdoor first aid course includes primary and secondary surveys of a casualty, we also look at first aid equipment and how you may adapted and improvise your own equipment to support your first aid kit.
A lot more emphasis is placed on incident management and relaying information to rescue teams in the outdoors. We assume minimal outdoor equipment is available and there are plenty of opportunities to practice first aid.
This 2 day (16 hour) course is recognised by the National Governing Bodies such as the Mountain Leader Training, British Canoe Union, British Cycling, British Association of Snowsport Instructors etc. Certificates are issued through the OfQual Awarding Organisation
This 16-hour outdoor first aid training course is a requirement for Mountain leaders, outdoor climbing leaders, and mountain bike leaders. Those looking to become leaders of those event types, or who are those leader types but need a refresher, will be given priority on this course for places. (If you're unsure if you qualify - just drop us an e-mail to
support@outdoorlads.com
and we'll discuss with you.)
Leaders of other event types are also encouraged to join and this is great experience - just be aware if over subscribed, we may need to prioritise others. This course is exclusively for leaders and those who have applied to become a leader.
OutdoorLads will pay your course fee - a £15 fee applies to secure your place, refunded when you have completed the course. You are responsible for your own travel, food and accommodation costs. The course lasts for two days, starting early each morning and finishing in the evening each day and you are required to attend both days.
Please note that places on this are for leaders, and those who have already applied to become leaders. If you have any doubts, please contact the OutdoorLads office to discuss.
Event cost
To help us ensure that those that book on to this event are committed to it, we're asking Leaders to pay £15. This payment will be refunded in full once attendance on the course is confirmed after the event. If you cancel before the event and we can fill your place, you will be refunded in full, but otherwise your booking on this event is subject to the
OutdoorLads refund policy
.
IMPORTANT! - Participation Statement
You MUST complete a
Participation Statement
,
in addition
to booking your event space before attending an OutdoorLads event. You only need to complete this Participation Statement once, not for each event you attend.
Photos
: Taken by Will Allaway, Liam Russell and James Couldry and used with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: This is inside and in non-challenging terrain outside

Title: Icknield Way: Weston Hills
Summary:
Join Alex for this 11-mile wander to Wallington and the Weston Hills via the Icknield Way.
We will set out from the historic coaching town of Baldock in North Hertfordshire and head southeast out of the town. Following the route of the ancient Icknield Way, we will gradually ascend the Weston Hills.
From here we will turn northeast and follow the Icknield Way along the ridge of the hills for a couple of miles, enjoying wide-ranging views across Hertfordshire, Bedfordshire and Cambridgeshire to the north. We will head to the village of Wallington, passing The Stores, a 17th century cottage and the former home of the author George Orwell. We will stop briefly at the first of three St Mary's churches, which contains a small exhibition on Orwell's life.
From Wallington we will turn southwest and head across fields and through woodland towards the small settlement of Clothall, passing through the churchyard of our second St Mary's church, dating from the 14th century. Here we will stop for lunch with views of both the church and the hills beyond.
After lunch, we will head towards the village of Weston across open countryside. Shortly before Weston we will turn northwest and return towards Baldock. Descending sharply into the town and passing a remarkably grand Tesco Extra, we will amble along Baldock High Street towards the imposing spire of our final and oldest St Mary's church. From here it is a short walk back to the station, with an optional pub stop.
Don't waste that space!
Spring means lots of members looking to get out more so please only book if you know you can make it. If your plans change please free up the space for someone else.
New members
Please feel free to join this as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions then feel free to message me, or go to the "My First Event" section under "About Us".
Is it dog friendly?
Dogs are welcome but please bear in mind not everyone likes dogs as much as you. Some of the fields we cross may contain livestock or horses so make sure this will not cause issues before bringing your dog along.
Image / photo credits:
All leader's own images - with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse
Difficulty & Distance: Around 4-5 hours walking time with 175m ascent, mostly at the beginning.

Title: Oxford Indoor Bouldering
Summary:
Hold on tight fellas (pun intended), OutdoorLads is now running a monthly indoor bouldering session in Oxford!
I'm sure many of us are no strangers to this kind of event, having become a very popular addition to ODL's catalogue with other chapters established in London, Bristol, Reading, Manchester and Sheffield.
For those new to the sport indoor bouldering is a form of rock climbing performed
without ropes or harness
on artificial walls (~3-4m) with crash mats underneath for safety. Here your technique and your problem solving ability counts just as much as your strength, so be prepared to work out both body and mind, all in a very social and friendly atmosphere (seriously, the puzzle solving nature of this sport acts as an amazing ice breaker when chatting to new people). I will also be there to welcome you into the group and answer any questions you may have, as well as offer climbing tips should you need them 😊.
We will be meeting at the
Gallery Bouldering
center located in the heart of Oxford, offering over 100m of bouldering wall (and growing) with a diverse range of routes catering for beginners all the way to Font 7C, so that there is something for all skill levels to enjoy. Afterwards we may also pop in for a pint at one of the many nearby pubs.
As with most climbing gyms there is a
waiver
that you will need to sign to check that you understand the risks involved and the proper climbing etiquette to follow to keep others and yourself safe while climbing. This can be filled out at reception but can also be done ahead of time by following the link
here
.
Please also note that this is a
liquid chalk only
gym, which can be readily purchased at reception.
Hope to see you there!
Difficulty & Distance: A range of climbs are available for all different skill levels to enjoy

Title: Tar Lakes & Rushy Common Nature Reserve Leisure Walk
Summary:
Join us for a relaxed 4.5-mile circular leisure walk around Rushy Common Nature Reserve and the Tar Lakes, a peaceful wetland landscape just outside Witney.
This easy-going route follows a network of flat gravel paths, paved sections and grassy tracks, looping around lakes, reedbeds and open meadows. Formerly a gravel extraction site, the area has been carefully restored and is now an important local nature reserve, with plenty of birdlife and open views.
We’ll walk at a sociable, steady pace, with time to chat and enjoy the surroundings. Around halfway through, we’ll stop at The Bell Inn in Ducklington for a drink. Ducklington is a traditional Oxfordshire village just outside Witney, making it a natural and welcoming halfway stop on our route. The walk is planned to finish around 14:00.
The walking element itself is around two hours, with the pub stop making this a relaxed and social Sunday outing.
Dogs are welcome on this walk. Please ensure they are kept under control and are comfortable with walking in a group.
Photos taken by the event leader, with permission for OutdoorLads to re-use.
Difficulty & Distance: Distance is 4.5 miles. Terrain is  flat gravel paths, paved sections and grassy tracks.

Title: BIRMINGHAM Indoor Climbing - MONDAY - Experienced
Summary:
Join OutdoorLads for an evening at Redpoint Birmingham Climbing Centre.
"EXPERIENCED" in this instance is those that have previously climbed & can answer YES to all three of these questions:
Can you fit a climbing harness correctly?
Can you tie in to a rope using the correct knots?
Can you belay another climber correctly & confidently?
Please ensure you only sign up for this event if you
satisfy all three of the prerequisites as an experienced (independent) climber!
Never climbed before? Need a refresher on the basics? Answered NO to any of the three questions above? You will need to
attend one of the beginners (supervised) events
instead, where we will happily sign you in to be supervised (two people per leader maximum), show you the ropes & teach you everything you need to know to climb safely! Beginner (supervised) events are not offered every week.
Bouldering
- Most of us do roped climbing, but if you prefer to do bouldering, you can sign yourself in & go bouldering (unsupervised) instead of, or as well as, roped climbing. Please meet the leader & the group at the start though; some others might wish to do bouldering too!
Please note that we will not teach lead climbing or lead belaying on this event -
we would suggest having some
lessons in Lead Climbing
by a qualified member of staff if this is something you're interested in pursuing!
Whilst this event finishes at 8pm, the centre is open until 10pm if you wish to continue climbing for longer!
If you are subsequently unable to attend, please cancel off the event as 'no-shows' are frustrating for the leader & other attendees.
Redpoint Birmingham Climbing Centre
Indoor climbing centres offer the challenges of climbing that you'll find on natural rock crags - simply indoors! It's a fun way to stay fit, make new friends & develop your climbing skills.
Climbing
- Unlike Indoor Bouldering there is a certain amount of skill to be mastered, such as tying in knots & belaying a partner, as well as the climbs being higher than in bouldering. If you are new to climbing or would like a refresher, then this is just for you!! If you’ve climbed before, can safely tie into a harness & can competently belay, then there’s plenty of choices of top-rope & lead climbing on offer here.
Bouldering
- Instead of tackling long high routes, the 'problems' (as they are referred to) are shorter & focus on technique, balance, body positioning & sometimes power. With the different emphasis, bouldering doesn't have the hassle of climbing with ropes, so it’s excellent (but not necessarily easier) for newcomers.
Redpoint Birmingham Climbing Centre
has more than 75 lines with over 300 routes, set through some spectacular ground including its notorious 'stalactite'. It even features one of the few indoor 'Yorkshire VS cracks' in the country, to test your jamming skills. The bouldering rooms are just as interesting as its climbing routes, over 200 bouldering problems have been organised into graded circuits to provide something for everyone.
You MUST register via this event page before coming to the centre!
To climb, you will need to:
Complete a
Registration Form
(please arrive early to do this in-person the first time) & pay a Registration Fee:
Annual Registration
is
£5
or
Day Registration
is
£3
Pay an
Entry Fee
directly to the climbing centre:
Standard Entry
is
£12.50
&
Bouldering Only
is
£8.50.
Hire
climbing shoes (
£3.50
) & a climbing harness (
£3.50
) if you don't own your own.
Please see Redpoint's
website
for current prices & note that prices are subject to change.
If you have any questions or need any more information just get in touch with the event leader by clicking on their profile below, or take a look at the
My first climbing event page
for more information.
PLEASE NOTE:
All those attending the event must also be aware of the climbing centre's own terms & conditions of participation & abide by them, along with any instructions given by the centre's staff.
Photos:
taken & used with permission of
R. Casabuena
,
D. Evans
&

Title: BIRMINGHAM Indoor Climbing - MONDAY - Beginners
Summary:
Join OutdoorLads for an evening at Redpoint Birmingham Climbing Centre.
*** Beginner (supervised) events are not offered every week ***
If you have never climbed before (or need a refresher on the basics), then this BEGINNERS EVENT is ideal for you! We will happily show you the ropes & teach you everything you need to know to climb safely!
Event pages are split into
BEGINNER & EXPERIENCED.
Please ensure you sign up to the correct event as we can only supervise two beginners at once per leader.
This event is for beginner climbers (those needing to be supervised) only!
If you are competent with how to tie in & belay (safely & confidently), then you will need to
sign up to the accompanying experienced (independent climber) event
instead.
Bouldering
- Most of us do roped climbing, but if you prefer to do bouldering, you can sign yourself in & go bouldering (unsupervised) instead of, or as well as, roped climbing. Please meet the leader & the group at the start though; some others might wish to do bouldering too!
Please note that we will not teach lead climbing or lead belaying on this event -
we would suggest having some
lessons in Lead Climbing
by a qualified member of staff if this is something you're interested in pursuing!
If you are subsequently unable to attend, please cancel off the event. Spaces for beginners are limited & 'no-shows' are frustrating for the leader & other attendees.
Redpoint Birmingham Climbing Centre
Indoor climbing centres offer the challenges of climbing that you'll find on natural rock crags - simply indoors! It's a fun way to stay fit, make new friends & develop your climbing skills.
Climbing
- Unlike Indoor Bouldering there is a certain amount of skill to be mastered, such as tying in knots & belaying a partner, as well as the climbs being higher than in bouldering. If you are new to climbing (or would like a refresher), there are plenty of top-rope routes to choose from after learning the essential climbing & safety techniques with one of our friendly, experienced climbing leaders.
Bouldering
- Instead of tackling long high routes, the 'problems' (as they are referred to) are shorter & focus on technique, balance, body positioning & sometimes power. With the different emphasis, bouldering doesn't have the hassle of climbing with ropes, so it’s excellent (but not necessarily easier) for newcomers.
Redpoint Birmingham Climbing Centre
has more than 75 lines with over 300 routes, set through some spectacular ground including its notorious 'stalactite'. It even features one of the few indoor 'Yorkshire VS cracks' in the country, to test your jamming skills. The bouldering rooms are just as interesting as its climbing routes, over 200 bouldering problems have been organised into graded circuits to provide something for everyone.
