Title: BIRMINGHAM Indoor Climbing - MONDAY - Experienced
Summary:
Join OutdoorLads for an evening at Redpoint Birmingham Climbing Centre.
"EXPERIENCED" in this instance is those that have previously climbed & can answer YES to all three of these questions:
Can you fit a climbing harness correctly?
Can you tie in to a rope using the correct knots?
Can you belay another climber correctly & confidently?
Please ensure you only sign up for this event if you
satisfy all three of the prerequisites as an experienced (independent) climber!
Never climbed before? Need a refresher on the basics? Answered NO to any of the three questions above? You will need to
attend one of the beginners (supervised) events
instead, where we will happily sign you in to be supervised (two people per leader maximum), show you the ropes & teach you everything you need to know to climb safely! Beginner (supervised) events are not offered every week.
Bouldering
- Most of us do roped climbing, but if you prefer to do bouldering, you can sign yourself in & go bouldering (unsupervised) instead of, or as well as, roped climbing. Please meet the leader & the group at the start though; some others might wish to do bouldering too!
Please note that we will not teach lead climbing or lead belaying on this event -
we would suggest having some
lessons in Lead Climbing
by a qualified member of staff if this is something you're interested in pursuing!
Whilst this event finishes at 8pm, the centre is open until 10pm if you wish to continue climbing for longer!
If you are subsequently unable to attend, please cancel off the event as 'no-shows' are frustrating for the leader & other attendees.
Redpoint Birmingham Climbing Centre
Indoor climbing centres offer the challenges of climbing that you'll find on natural rock crags - simply indoors! It's a fun way to stay fit, make new friends & develop your climbing skills.
Climbing
- Unlike Indoor Bouldering there is a certain amount of skill to be mastered, such as tying in knots & belaying a partner, as well as the climbs being higher than in bouldering. If you are new to climbing or would like a refresher, then this is just for you!! If you’ve climbed before, can safely tie into a harness & can competently belay, then there’s plenty of choices of top-rope & lead climbing on offer here.
Bouldering
- Instead of tackling long high routes, the 'problems' (as they are referred to) are shorter & focus on technique, balance, body positioning & sometimes power. With the different emphasis, bouldering doesn't have the hassle of climbing with ropes, so it’s excellent (but not necessarily easier) for newcomers.
Redpoint Birmingham Climbing Centre
has more than 75 lines with over 300 routes, set through some spectacular ground including its notorious 'stalactite'. It even features one of the few indoor 'Yorkshire VS cracks' in the country, to test your jamming skills. The bouldering rooms are just as interesting as its climbing routes, over 200 bouldering problems have been organised into graded circuits to provide something for everyone.

Title: Rail & Ramble: A bit of rough up Top Withens
Summary:
Join us for a walk to mark 210 years since the birth of Charlotte Brontë, born on 21 April 1816. As we explore the moors, reservoirs and valleys around Haworth, we’ll be walking through the very landscapes that shaped her life and her imagination.
We’ll take a ride out on the heritage Keighley & Worth Valley Railway service from Keighley to the charming village of Oxenhope to start our walk. We'll amble steadily onto the moors before skirting the edge of Lower Laithe Reservoir, a lovely stretch of water framed by rolling hills. From here we'll continue towards Ponden Reservoir, enjoying open views as the landscape becomes wilder and more dramatic.
The climb up to
Top Withens rewards us with panoramas across good old Brontë country! This area is often misquoted as the inspiration for Emily Brontë's
'Wuthering Heights'
. After taking in more beautiful views, we'll descend on a moorland path to the Brontë Waterfall and Bridge, a picture‑perfect place to pause before the final leg.
The route finishes with a gentle return into Haworth, where cafés and cobbled streets await. We'll then catch the steam train back to Keighley.
Dogs:
Well-behaved, socialised and under-control dogs are welcome on this event.
Dogs should be kept on a lead whenever this is signposted, whenever we are in the vicinity of livestock, and at the event leader's request. If it's hot, you'll need to make sure you're carrying sufficient extra water. If you're unsure, contact the leader for guidance.
If the event is fully booked:
Please
join the waiting list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event and you are likely to get a place on the walk.
If this is your first OutdoorLads walk event:
If this is your first event, we’ll make sure you feel welcomed. Please
check out the My First Event page
, and don’t hesitate to drop me a message if you have questions or want to know more about this walk.
Image / photo credits:
All leader's own images - with permission for OutdoorLads to use.
Difficulty & Distance: Total distance about 17 km (approx. 10.5 miles) with 500m (1640 feet) ascent in stages. Approximately 5 hours including lunch. Some areas of moorland will be boggy even in dry conditions.

Title: Think you know Croydon....think again!
Summary:
Yes that's right, a walking tour of Croydon! Well not quite the town centre, but it’s been my home for the past ten years and it's certainly had it's share of issues. But in this circular walk, I will show you an alternative side to the town, full of the places I love to visit, that are often overlooked by the media. Hopefully you may discover something about the town, you didn't know before!
We'll discover a
ghost station
and a
disused railway line,
part of which now forms the Croydon tramlink. We'll take in the views from
two different viewpoints
, ascend the site of a late
Mesolithic
Settlement
and
Bronze Age hill,
visit a
windmill
built in 1854 situated in a new housing estate, and stop for tea in a former
Victorian  stable block!
Points of interest:
South Croydon
Quieter than central Croydon, we'll meet here. Much of the land of South Croydon has been associated through the years with the Whitgift Foundation,  whose charitable work has benefited and influenced Croydon since its foundation in 1596. The Whitgift School and Whitgift shopping centre bear the name of the foundation.
Spencer Road Halt ghost station and disused railway line
The station was part of the Woodside and South Croydon Joint Railway, which opened in August 1885 and ran between Woodside in the north to Selsdon Road in the south. They also opened a small station — a halt — at Spencer Road. Much like the rest of the railway, it wasn’t a success, and having opened in 1906, it closed less than a decade later in 1915.
You can see the remains of the closed railway in the undergrowth and as you head over the bridge twice on this walk, notice that it still has official railway bridge marker numbers.
Look around the bottom of the footbridge. You’ll see some large concrete blocks — these are leftovers from WWII defences, which were put up around many railway sites to be used to prevent the enemy from advancing by rail had they invaded the UK.
After the railway closed, it was left dormant, but in 2000, about half the railway track was reused between Woodside and Lloyd Park station for the Croydon tramlink. It was a failure as a railway, but is huge success as a tramlink.
Croham Hurst
There is some evidence of a late Mesolithic Settlement which would have been occupied between 5000‑3000 years B.C. The flint tools of Neolithic or New Stone Age man were also found on the Hurst. Around 2100 B.C. the Bronze Age began – Bronze Age man was religious and he buried his dead in round barrows, one of which was found on the top of Croham Hurst.
Croham Hurst covers 35 hectares and the top most point is 145 metres above sea level. The hill is formed of Thanet Sand (which makes up the greater part of the Hurst) topped with a pebble bed and resting on an eroded surface of chalk.
Heathfield Estate
Acquired in 1964 from the estate of Raymond Riesco, the building is Grade II listed. Great views and varied specimen trees make this an unusual place in Croydon, although now somewhat neglected. The Croydon Ecology Centre is here with its orchard and ponds.
Addington Hills
Addington Hills reaches 460 feet (140 m) above sea level. It is a peaceful area with many pathways, close to central Croydon. There is a viewpoint with fine views across Croydon and to north London, including Docklands and Parliament Hill.
Shirley Windmill open for our visit
One of only four windmills open to the public in Greater London, Shirley Windmill is a Tower Mill, built in 1854 by Richard Alwen to replace an earlier (1808) timber post-mill on this site, lost by fire. It ceased working in about 1890, but most of the original machinery, including its two pairs of millstones, is still in place, and has recently been restored, so there is much to see. Note, we won't have time for the 60 minute guided tour.
Combe Wood Gardens & Café
Coombe Wood is a small woodland and garden area in the old village of Coombe, South Croydon. The Coombe Wood Gardens are divided into a series of rooms which together give an all-year-round display of shrubs and plants set against a woodland backdrop. The former stable block with the Coach House cafe visible through the arch. In 1898, Arthur Lloyd, brother of Frank Lloyd built Coombe Wood House.
Lloyd Park
It was created from land owned by Frank Lloyd (son of Edward Lloyd) a newspaper proprietor who died in 1927 which was bequeathed to the Borough of Croydon by his family, after his death.
Route
You can view the route in OS Map
here
.
We'll meet at south Croydon Station which is a short ride from East Croydon Station with connections to Central London and the South Coast.
We'll head through South Croydon, along numerous alleyways and cross a bridge over the disused Woodside and South Croydon Joint Railway and location of the former Spencer Road Halt. We'll continue south walking through an area full of mainly 1930s housing stock.
We reach Croham Hurst an ancient woodland with evidence  of a Mesolithic Settlement and climb to to enjoy far reaching views south across Surrey. From here we descend  and walk around the golf course. After passing the outskirts of Selsdon we navigate through Little Heath Wood and cross the Vanguard Way. We follow the path through Bramley Bank to reach the Heathfields estate. Once a large estate, now overgrown and rundown. We'll follow the London Loop through here.
Take care crossing  over the Tram line and continue until we reach the Addington Hills viewpoint. From here you can see central London and Canary Wharf in the distance. We'll descend through woodland and to visit the Shirley Windmill. Well stop here for a late lunch. There's a small visitor centre and café. Afterwards we'll walk through the golf course until we reach Combe Wood & Gardens where we'll stop for tea. There's a café here with outdoor seating in the former stables. You also can spend some time enjoying the gardens and use toilets.
The final part of the walk takes us through one favourite parks, Lloyd Park, and then we'll head back through South Croydon, once again crossing the disused railway line (look out for the old tracks below you) at the former Spencer Road Halt. From here its a short walk back to South Croydon station.
Afterwards
We'll grab a drink at a local pub.
New Members
New members are very welcome. Please message me if you have any questions or concerns before the day.
All Images were taken by Neil Prior & permission given to OutdoorLads for re-use.
Difficulty & Distance: 12 km/7 miles mainly paved and gravel paths. Could be muddy in places if wet. Total ascent 220m

Title: A Gothic chapel, planes, boats, and a very old Castle
Summary:
Join us on this pleasant 12 mile circular walk starting from Shoreham by sea walking over the downs behind Lancing College and its Chapel, an
impressive example of Gothic Revival architecture
(AKA Hogwarts) then down to
Bramber Castle.
Then a gentle post-lunch saunter back along the River Adur just in time for a bit of
plane spotting
at Shoreham Airport café.
The walk is 12 miles long with 260m (850 feet) of ascent mainly at the beginning, then it's downhill and flat after that. The surfaces will be a mix of uneven tracks, made paths and pavements, some could be
muddy!
Leaving the meeting point we will pass Shoreham
Artisan Market
where we will spend a little bit of time.
We will then cross the Adur using the Old Shoreham
Toll Bridge
built in 1781, now a public bridleway and free of charge. Crossing the A27 at Sussex pad, thankfully with traffic lights, we will
visit Lancing College Chapel.
Passing through Lancing ring a nature reserve we continue to head up and start to get
impressive views
of the South Downs, with the quarry behind the old cement works clearly visible in the distance and the Adur below.
We make a sharp turn east at Steyning Bowl and a rapid descent along Monarch's Way towards Bramber.
Bramber Castle
was founded by William de Braose as a defensive and administrative centre for Bramber in 1073. Although the castle is now in ruins and only fragments remain, it is still a fascinating place.
After lunch we pick up the trail using the Downs Link, a footpath and cycle track remade from the old Steyning Line railway. Heading south we will walk along side the River Adur passing the old cement works that closed in 1991 and is due for redevelopment. Passing under the A27 flyover, Lancing College Chapel will come into view in the distance. Once again we will use the toll bridge and walk though Shoreham airport.
Shoreham Airport
also known as Brighton City Airport was founded in 1910. It is the oldest airport in the UK and the oldest purpose-built commercial airport in the world still in operation. The
1930s Art Deco terminal
building designed by R Stavers Hessell Tiltman is listed Grade II. Here we will stop and visit the cafe for refreshments with their bar fully-stocked with local beers and take in a bit of plane spotting. The airport has often been used in films with a few to note:
The Da Vinci Code
Woman in Gold
Richard III
A Dark Reflection
For Those in Peril
High Flyers: How Britain Took to the Air
Poirot
The Crown,
Hyde Park Corner.
We leave the airport behind, refreshed, and cross Adur recreational ground to join a footpath beside the moorings of Shoreham's house boats. This is an
eclectic, bizarre and bohemian mixture of boats
and barges used by people to live in. Crossing the Adur using the footbridge, we'll head back to Shoreham Railway Station.
OS map route:
Here!
Dogs:
I love having dogs on my walks. I just ask that owners please ensure they are kept on a lead as appropriate, especially around livestock and when near busy roads. Please, also remember that not everyone is a dog lover.
This particular walk does have a lot of stiles so please ensure your dog is comfortable with these.
Don’t Waste That Space!
Walks get filled up quite quickly, so please only sign up if you know you are free to attend. Plans do sometimes change, but remember to free up your space so someone else can attend.
New Members:

Timings
Whilst every endeavour is made to make the timings of this event accurate, I cannot be guaranteed that we will finish on time and there may be delays.
If you have read this far, thanks and comment your favourite colour below to confuse all the skim readers. :)
Images are the leader's own.
All pictures were taken by the leader.
Difficulty & Distance: The walk is 12 miles long with 260m (850) feet of assent mainly at the beginning then it's downhill and flat after that.

Title: Peacehaven to Seaford Fun Walk
Summary:
Join me for a relaxed coastal adventure from
Peacehaven to Seaford
, taking in cliff‑top drama, maritime history, sea air, and a well‑earned pub at the end!
Peacehaven is just (6 miles) east of Brighton city centre.
⭐ What to Expect
Start at Peacehaven’s Meridian Monument
Stroll the cliff tops with sweeping ocean views
Drop down into
Newhaven
,
passing the fort and harbour
Walk along shoreline into Seaford
💧 Water Break at Newhaven Fort
A short hydration pause at the Fort — a perfect opportunity to enjoy the coastal backdrop before continuing.
🚢 Ferry Sightings: Newhaven → Dieppe
As you pass Newhaven Harbour, you might spot the DFDS/Transmanche ferries operating on the historic             Newhaven–Dieppe route - the UK’s longest continuous ferry link to France, today carrying around 410,000 passengers a year, most of them tourists.
The ferries you’re likely to see are the
Seven Sisters
or
Côte d’Albâtre
, modern vessels branded under the Transmanche service and run by DFDS. This crossing has a remarkably long story, stretching back over 200 years.
(credits to  Newhaven Port/DFDS)
📸 Scenic Photo Opportunities Along the Route
Clifftop Walk (Peacehaven → Newhaven) – dramatic chalk cliffs and wide coastal panoramas
Fort Highpoint – elevated views of the harbour and Channel
Newhaven Harbour & Ferries – capture ferries arriving or departing
Tide Mills Ruins – moody abandoned village and ghost station
Seaford Beach & Bay – big‑sky shingle beach, perfect for shoreline shots
Seaford Head – sweeping panoramic finish
🥾
Distance and Difficulty
8.5 miles-cliff top path and shoreline
Easy–moderate
⚠️ Safety Briefing
This walk follow cliffpath footpaths and some roadside sections. Please stay aware of traffic, wear suitable footwear, and bring water and weather-appropriate clothing. If you’re walking with dogs, please keep them under control and clean up after them. As always, walk at your own pace and let the lead know if you need to take a break
New Members
If this is your first flight or you are new to OutdoorLads passenger we’d love to see you. This type of event is perfect for you to get to know other ODL members. Please message me if you have any questions.
Pictures courtesy of martiebear
🚍 Transport-Getting to the event.
I would suggest travelling by Bus or Car (free parking at Roderick avenue car park)
Linear walk
– if you drive to Peacehaven, you’ll need public transport back
Peacehaven is served by the
12 / 12X buses
from Brighton & Eastbourne
Train options via Brighton; buses run between
Brighton ↔ Eastbourne
Seaford
Buses 12/12x and local train station
🍻 After walk
We’ll wrap up in Seaford with a visit to a local pub.
Difficulty & Distance: 8.5 miles, and cliff top path and shoreline

Title: Eryri Scrambles - Moving On From Grade 1 - Day 1
Summary:
This event takes place on
Saturday 13th June
and is the first in a series of two, with a follow-up event taking place the following day -
Sunday 14th June.
(You can book into either or both, and its not a requirement to have attended one event to attend the other. Different routes will be chosen for each day).
This event is ideally suited for those members who are
experienced scramblers
, and who are comfortable completing
Grade 1 routes without a Leader or Instructor
, and who want to build their confidence to progress to
more challenging routes (Grade 2).
The Ogwen Valley offers a fantastic venue for scrambling with a large sections of routes across all grades, condensed into a small area which enables us to
link routes up
to deliver a
truly memorable scrambling day
.
Route choice will be made on the morning of the event and will dependant on the weather.
You must bring a
climbing helmet.

IMPORTANT!
Please note that every person participating should be aware of (and accept) that those members perceived as ‘in charge’ or leading the event are not experts, do not need to be experts, and are not regarded by OutdoorLads, or themselves as experts, but are amateurs with some experience in the event type and who are happy to impart their knowledge.  Any advice given should be considered with this in mind by the recipient.
By signing up to and attending an OutdoorLads event, you are confirming that you have read, understood and accept the content of this statement.

Title: Eryri Scrambles - Moving On From Grade 1 - Day 2
Summary:
This event takes place on
Sunday 14th June,
and the second event in a series of two, with the first event taking place on
Saturday 13th June.
(You can book into either or both, and it's not a requirement to have attended one event to attend the other. Different routes will be chosen for each day).
This event is ideally suited for those members who are
experienced scramblers
, and who are comfortable completing
Grade 1 routes without a Leader or Instructor
, and who want to build their confidence to progress to
more challenging routes (Grade 2).
The Ogwen Valley offers a fantastic venue for scrambling with a large sections of routes across all grades, condensed into a small area which enables us to
link routes up
to deliver a
truly memorable scrambling day
.
Route choice will be made on the morning of the event and will dependant on the weather.
You must bring a
climbing helmet.
Please note that the event may be liable to change or cancellation due to inclement weather.
Photos:
Pictures taken by Andy Hewlett and used by OutdoorLads with permission.
IMPORTANT!
Please note that every person participating should be aware of (and accept) that those members perceived as ‘in charge’ or leading the event are not experts, do not need to be experts, and are not regarded by OutdoorLads, or themselves as experts, but are amateurs with some experience in the event type and who are happy to impart their knowledge.  Any advice given should be considered with this in mind by the recipient.
By signing up to and attending an OutdoorLads event, you are confirming that you have read, understood and accept the content of this statement.
Difficulty & Distance: Exposure to drops and loose rock. A big mountain day.

Title: Over the Orme: an early evening Friday walk
Summary:
This 4 mile walk follows the
afternoon cruise on The Waverley
and will take you on a circle over the Great Orme's Head which we will see on the cruise from the sea. (The event is open to all members even if not on the cruise). You'll see some great views of the town and out to sea towards Llandudno bay and the famous pier from which the cruise departs, and the massive windfarm. Also in the other direction, towards Conwy Bay and the Menai Straits and in clear weather many of the peaks of the Carneddau mountains. We will also pass by the terminus of the historic tramway, and the cable car.  The terrain will be a mixture of tarmac loose stone trails and grass land paths.
After the event some of us may linger in town and have a drink or  bite to eat.
Founded in the 6th-century
Llandudno
, known as the "Queen of the Welsh Resorts," evolved from an ancient mining and fishing village into a premier Victorian seaside town, featuring the Great Orme Tramway (1902), an historic pier, and unique Victorian architecture.
The Great Orme
has 4,000-year-old copper mines, some of the largest prehistoric mining sites in the world. A cable car to the summit and a dry ski slope have subsequently been added.  The 'Orme is also known for the wild Kashmiri goats that live there, and the marine life of the coastline, including seals and dolphins.
Photos are property of AndyM leader and used with consent.
Bad Weather
This event may be altered or cancelled if persistent heavy rain or poor visibility is forecast - you will be notified by at least 18:00 hours the night before in this case
Dogs
are welcome to join us on this event but please note we may pass through areas with livestock where your dog needs to be on a lead.
Are you new to Outdoorlads and considering your first event?
Have a look at this link, hopefully it will help
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
Difficulty & Distance: We are walking 4 miles but with 900' of height gain

Title: Cairngorms 2026 - Camping
Summary:
🏴󠁧󠁢󠁳󠁣󠁴󠁿⛰ 🌲 🏄‍♂️🚴‍♂️ 🥾 🏃‍♂️🪴🌿🌳🔥 🍽️
Fancy camping in your tent in the middle of the Glenmore forest, in the Cairngorm National Park surrounded by forests, Loch Morlich, Loch An Eilein and the Cairngorm mountain range?
Hillwalking, cycling, running, kayaking and paddle boarding all on your doorstep!! We also offering conservation events which proved popular in 2024.
This page is for camping in your own tent - or you could choose to
stay in a lodge, see this separate event page.
Booking via OutdoorLads is the only way to camp at the site. The price is per person camping.
5 nights of camping and a contribution to communal expenses is included
in the event cost
(communal hiring of activities hall, welcome drink on the 1st evening at the event briefing, BBQ area hire, cost of charcoal, snacks and desserts for the BBQ) so you do
NOT
need to also sign up to the
Multi Activity Event
.
Please bring your own drinks and food to cook on the BBQ.
Evening events:
Friday 20:30 -
Welcome Drink & social (activities hall)
Saturday 19:00
- BBQ (BBQ bothy)
Sunday  20:00 -
Quiz (activities hall)
Monday 20:00
- Social around the campfire
🔥
Tuesday 18:30
- Drinks, group meal & Ceilidh 🏴󠁧󠁢󠁳󠁣󠁴󠁿🕺 (The Penny Bun)
Telegram group (set up one week prior to the event):
A whiteboard will be placed under the gazebo and updated daily with a list of all activities and events available each day. Each evening, a daily update will also be sent to group members with the schedule of activities planned for the following day.
The location is 1100ft high in the forest with great views and lots of different options on the doorstep without having to move your car! The closest railway station is Aviemore (5 miles away) with several trains and buses links to Inverness, Glasgow and Edinburgh. Direct flights from London Gatwick and Bristol to Inverness airport only 30 mins drive away.
The actual activities will vary depending on the weather at the time but we already have a variety of different walks, mountain bike routes, forest cycles and running routes available. A short briefing each evening along with a message will be sent to all attendees so you’ll be able to see what’s on offer each day and to keep you updated of any changes! The meeting place for all events is outside the Wigwam kitchen.
On our 1st evening there will be a welcome drink, short event briefing & social in the activities hall and on Saturday a BBQ (Please bring your own food to cook on the BBQ) and an opportunity to socialise each evening in the communal
kitchen & social space for campers
(shown in the photos) - an ideal space when it’s raining!
PLEASE NOTE
: OutdoorLads will be sharing the site with other guests. No noise is permitted after 11pm and participants must respect the other guests staying on site.  This is therefore a quiet camping event.
Dogs and campervans / caravans etc
: Sorry, no dogs allowed and campervans are not permitted at Badaguish.
PAY IN INSTALMENTS
If you would prefer to pay in monthly instalments, please
contact the office team
(or
raise a support ticket
) and we can discuss the options available to you, depending on the time available between booking and the event taking place. There will be additional terms and conditions to agree to before you commit. You won't pay anything extra to pay in instalments. You will need to be paid up on month before the event starts.
OUTDOORLADS FOUNDATION
Fancy coming along but having a difficult time of it (for whatever reason), and/or finding it hard to justify the expense? Apply for a place on this event through the OutdoorLads Foundation! Nobody apart from staff members will know and we don't need lots of financial info from you either. To apply through the Foundation, you'll need to complete a very short form, which can be found along with more information
here
.
Photos:
taken by event leaders and with permission from Badaguish and free to use by ODL
Want to keep in touch with what's going on in Scotland?
Join our
Telegram chat group
and bookmark our upcoming events list via the short link
:
https://outdoorlads.events/scotland
Difficulty & Distance: Mountainous terrain, variety of options in the area

Title: RSPB Troup Head - Nesting Birds and Aberdeenshire Cliff walk
Summary:
The cliffs at Troup Head are home to some 50,000 sea birds for their nesting season. This walk will take in the RSPB site and give us a chance to see razorbills, gannets, guillemots, kittiwakes and puffins, as well as, if we're really lucky, bottlenose dolphins, porpoises and minke whales. There will be plenty of time to watch for wildlife with binoculars. This is the focus of today's walk, plus fantastic cliff and coastal scenery in beautiful Aberdeenshire.
It's a six mile circular walk, starting and finishing in a small, historic former fishing village. As well as the dramatic cliffs, we will follow some quieter paths and country lanes on this walk.
We should have time to get refreshments in one of the neighbouring villages before we head home.
There is no charge for accessing the RSPB site, but there may be a charge for car parking - not known at present.
The pictures are of the birds we might see, but are not taken at Troup Head.
Dogs are welcome on the walk but must be under complete control because of the wildlife and the closeness to the cliff edges.
Photos :
by Mark D and used by OutdoorLads with full permission.

chat
and

groups, and bookmark our upcoming events list via our
Scottish Calendar
.
Difficulty & Distance: Cliff walking and some ascent - c. 285m

Title: Nottingham Summer Social
Summary:
Summer Social: Sun, Street Food & Sneinton Market
Join us for a summer session in the heart of Nottingham. This is a brilliant chance for everyone to catch up, and if you haven’t joined us before, it’s a great, low-pressure way to meet the group and see what we’re all about.
We’re heading to Bustler at Sneinton Market. Originally a 1930s wholesale hub, it’s been transformed into a massive social space with a great outdoor courtyard. With plenty of disco balls and a rotating selection of independent Street food vendors, it’s the ideal spot for a Saturday evening.
Later in the evening, we’ll move on to The Lord Roberts, one of the city’s long-standing LGBTQ+ landmarks, to finish the night in style.
Why not make a full day of it? A few of us will be on a
Peak District walk earlier in the day
—feel free to join us for the hike before heading back to the city for the social.
Any questions? Just drop me a message. See you there!
Image Credits: All images taken by the event leader with permission for ODL use.
Difficulty & Distance: Social Event

Title: Hathersage circular leisure walk with ice cream stop
Summary:
Join Mark for a relaxed stroll through the heart of the Peak District before we head into the city for the evening. This is a low-intensity "leisure walk"—perfect for getting some fresh air and a bit of a catch-up before the
Nottingham Summer Social
later the same day.
We’ll meet in Hathersage and head out to the famous Stepping Stones (bring your balance!) before following the winding paths of the River Derwent towards Bamford.
The route takes us through the greens of Sickleholm Golf Course as we make our way to the real highlight of the day: Thorpe Farm.
Thorpe Farm is the home of the legendary Hope Valley Ice Cream, made right there on the farm. We’ll take a proper break to sample a few scoops (the honeycomb is a firm favourite) before meandering back along the river to Hathersage.
This walk is timed perfectly to get you back with plenty of time to freshen up before we meet at Bustler in Sneinton Market. Whether you’re joining us for just the walk, just the social, or the full "Peaks to Pub" double-bill, it’s going to be a great weekend.
If you’ve got any questions about the route or the plan for the day, just drop me a message. See you there!
Difficulty & Distance: Total distance is around 11km (7 miles) with 209m (685 feet) of ascent. Approximately 4 hours. Some paths over fields are uneven and may be boggy in wet conditions.

Title: NOTTINGHAM Indoor Climbing – THURSDAY  - Experienced
Summary:
Join OutdoorLads at Nottingham Climbing Centre
Climbing evenings are held regularly and we tend to go to the local pub – The Lion for refreshments and a social gathering afterwards where they have a wide variety of beverages both alcoholic and non-alcoholic.
NOTE: If you are climbing with us for the first time
, please send a note to the
Event Leader
telling them about your experience and goals.
Nottingham Climbing Centre
Nottingham Climbing Centre is equipped with walls and routes to suit all skill levels and groups of climbers, whether you are a novice looking to try climbing for the first time, or an experienced lead climber, you’ll find an area to challenge you. With 28 Top Ropes routes available as well as 24 Lines of Lead climbing for the more advanced individuals. In addition, 6 auto-belays are available, so there is always the opportunity to climb, even if the numbers don’t match up. The available bouldering walls also provide that extra physical and mental challenge for those looking for something slightly different from rope work. Both Bouldering and Rope Climbing routes range from 4a – 8a.
Do I need to be Supervised as a Climber?
If you answer
NO
to any of the following three questions, then you will need to arrange to be supervised and will need to book onto a future beginners event.  If you can answer YES to all these questions, you can sign up to this event as normal:
Can you fit a climbing harness correctly?
Can you tie yourself to the end of a rope using the correct knots?
Can you belay another climber correctly and confidently?
To come along, you will need to register via this event page and to climb you will need to pay an entry fee directly to the climbing centre when you arrive, please see their
website
for up-to-date prices. We ask that all members sign up through the event page to make sure the event leader has all information of the members attending.
To climb at Nottingham Climbing Centre you will need to:
-     Complete a climbing waiver for the centre (please arrive early to do this the first time, or better still complete it online before you arrive)
-     Pay the Lifetime Registration Fee - £6.00
-     Pay an Entry Fee directly to the climbing centre - £9.50 (ensure you mention ODL)
-     Hire shoes (if you don’t have your own) - £3.50*
-     Hire a harness (again, if you don’t have your own) - £3.50*
-     Hire shoes and harness - £6.00*
*Equipment hire charges will be waived for your first visit to the centre!
Can I just come along and boulder?
Absolutely! Most of us do roped climbing, but if you prefer bouldering, you can sign yourself in and do it, as long as you can fill in the climbing centre's form correctly, watch their
safety video
and can answer the simple safety questions at reception on your first visit. Meet the group at the start; some others might be bouldering. We will make sure we come and find you when we have our tea break!
If you have any questions or need any more information just get in touch with the event leader by clicking on their profile below, or take a look at the
My first climbing event page
for more information.
IMPORTANT! - Participation Statement
You MUST complete a
Participation Statement
, in addition to booking your event space before attending an OutdoorLads event. You only need to complete this Participation Statement once, not for each event you attend.
Anyone with COVID-19 symptoms, even if they are extremely mild, or who has recently tested positive for Covid-19, is asked not to attend.
Photos:
All images supplied by event leaders and used by OutdoorLads with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Beginners welcome.

Title: Bouldering MANCHESTER - Beginners welcome
Summary:
Join OutdoorLads For An Evening of INDOOR BOULDERING in Manchester!
This event is intended as a safe space for the LGBTQ+ community; everyone will be warmly welcomed
Bouldering is a form of climbing that focuses on shorter "problems" emphasising technique, balance, body positioning, and sometimes power, rather than long, high routes. Without the need for ropes, harnesses, and belaying, bouldering is an accessible activity for newcomers and is often considered the "purest" form of climbing.
Bouldering offers a wide range of benefits, including:
Physical:
Improved strength, tone, balance, power, and coordination.
Mental:
Enhanced problem-solving, focus, concentration, and body awareness.
Emotional:
Relaxation, a sense of achievement, and a feeling of mind-body connection.
Bouldering is also a fantastic way to meet new people and build lasting friendships, as it's an inherently social sport where climbers share tips and strategies to conquer the routes.
New to Bouldering?
If you're new to bouldering, it is recommended that you have a guided session from the event leader to learn the basics of safe bouldering. The leader can then introduce you to the rest of the group where you can refine your newly learnt skills in a social environment.
Experienced Boulderer?
If you’re new to climbing with ODL, the leader will help to introduce you to the rest of the group so you can explore the centre together.
You are responsible for your own safety and actions while bouldering. Please use your own judgement when accepting advice from OutdoorLads Volunteers or other experienced climbers, regardless of your bouldering experience.
Ready to boulder?
You will need to sign up on the ODL event page. We would ask you to do this each time so that the leader has access to important information
If you are not already registered with Rock Over Climbing, please do so
here
to save time when you arrive. You only need to do this once.
When you arrive at the centre you will need to pay for entry and hire climbing shoes if you don't own a pair. ODL members benefit from the concessionary rate so please mention this when paying.
Get bouldering! You’ll find most of the group already bouldering so feel free to join them. If you’re new to bouldering with ODL, the leader will be looking out for you so if you spot them, please go speak with them first.
Any questions?
If you have any questions, please contact the leader directly.
Photos : Copyright granted to OutdoorLads by
Sieh
and joenellist.
Difficulty & Distance: Suitable for all abilities. If you're new to bouldering, just let the event leader know and we'll get you started!

Title: FRIDAY FELLS - Fairfield and Seat Sandal from Grasmere
Summary:
Start your Bank Holiday early and make it a four-dayer!  Join Friday Fells for an 8 mile (1,000m ascent) circular mountain walk from Grasmere to the summit of one of England's highest mountains.
From Grasmere, the climb is steep, but we'll soon be rewarded with fine views from Stone Arthur (503m), our first Wainwright of the day, from where we'll continue to climb to Great Rigg (766m) and Fairfield (873m), our second and third.  Fairfield is also the highest point of the day.  After enjoying the views (hopefully!), we'll drop steeply to Grisedale Tarn and clamber straight back up to the little visited, but very lovely, Seat Sandal (736m), which is our fourth and final Wainwright.  From there, it's a leisurely descent back to Grasmere with fine views all the way.
At the end of the day, we'll go for an optional drink at
Tweedies
Bar & Lodge
in Grasmere.
You can find the planned route
here
, though we may vary this depending on the abilities and ambitions of the group and weather conditions.
Event full?
Please join the waiting list.  Places usually open up as the event gets nearer and, where necessary, we try to add additional leaders to increase the numbers, so you are highly likely to get a place.
New Members:
Please feel free to join this as your first OutdoorLads event, do note that this walk is likely to require some experience. If you have any questions then feel free to message me, or go to the "My First Event" section under "About Us".
Is it dog friendly?
Well-trained dogs are welcome but please note and abide by the
Countryside Code
. Please also remember not everyone loves dogs as much as you do so please be mindful of other members.
Image
/
photo
credits
:  All images (c) JPV with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse.
Difficulty & Distance: Ascent (1,000m) and terrain (steep, loose rock in places).

Title: Cawdor Castle Walled Garden and Laburnum Avenue
Summary:
This is another in the series of Scottish garden visits which are designed to be as accessible as possible to the widest range of members. We are hoping that the date we have chosen will coincide with the flowering of the Laburnum Avenue at Cawdor Castle (weather dependent, of course). If we are in luck, this should be a spectacular sight!
The gardens at Cawdor are well worth visiting and the entry fee is £10 per person to be paid at the ticket office. For an extra £6.50 you can also tour the castle itself which, again, is well worth exploring and this could be done independently as an add-on to our planned walk. You don't have to decide until the day itself how long you want to spend at Cawdor. There's a very nice cafe for refreshments...
We will meet at the ticket office at 11am sharp.
This event has been planned as a relaxing walk to follow Highland Pride the day before and the Pride Social on the Saturday evening, although it is obviously open as a stand alone event to those who have not attended Pride events.
The link to the Pride event is here:
Our First Ever Highland Pride, Inverness | OutdoorLads
The link to the post-Pride evening event is here:
After-Pride Inverness Social | OutdoorLads
Photos :
by Mark D and used by OutdoorLads with full permission.

Title: Post-Pride Inverness Social
Summary:
Time to chill, relax, make new friends and renew older acquaintances!
We'll start with food in one of the central Inverness eateries at 7.00pm, then make our way to Eden Court where we can chat at ease.
The Eden Court Theatre and Cinema is a LGBTQ+ friendly venue. Join us in the bar area for a relaxed evening of chat. Whether you've attended ninety or no events, you'll get a very warm welcome. Find out a bit more about OurdoorLads and what we have planned.
We may, as a group, head out to other venues if people wish.
You may want to consider these events too, over the weekend:
Saturday 6th June daytime - Highland Pride:
Our First Ever Highland Pride, Inverness | OutdoorLads
Sunday 7th June: Cawdor Castle Gardens:
Cawdor Castle Walled Garden and Laburnum Avenue | OutdoorLads

Title: Our First Ever Highland Pride, Inverness
Summary:
This is OutdoorLads' first time attending Highland Pride, we want to build upon our membership and range of events in northern Scotland, so, whether you like climbing, bouldering, hostelling, cycling, camping, canoeing or day walks grab your OutdoorLads RED T-shirt and come join in the fun and help show the Highlands that OutdoorLads does #GetOutMore!
The procession starts and ends at Eden Court Theatre and Cinema on the banks of the River Ness. Eden Court is also where our stall will be where we intend to engage directly with potential members and existing members who may not have joined an ODL event for some time.
It would be great to see as many members as possible to help staff the stand. **Timings will be published nearer the time**
Why not make a Highland Weekend of it and join us at the post Pride social on Saturday evening:
After-Pride Inverness Social | OutdoorLads
and at Cawdor Castle Gardens  on Sunday 7th June:
Cawdor Castle Walled Garden and Laburnum Avenue | OutdoorLads

Title: Don't get "bowled" out at the Oval!
Summary:
Come spend the day at the beautiful Kia Oval, home of the Surrey County Cricket Club, and host to the very first Ashes match way back in 1882 (England lost that 3 day contest by 7 runs, by the way...)!
Today's battle won't have the historic implications of that match 144 years ago, but will pit county against county, as the home team Surrey Men welcomes Hampshire Men.  This is Day One of the four day
Rothesay County Championship
against Hampshire, and is expected to be a cracking good day of "
red ball
" cricket!  Fans are even allowed onto the pitch during the innings break.  Even if you know nothing about the game (runs are scored by the batters running back and forth between the wickets - 1 point for each successful "run", or by hitting the field boundary ropes - 4 points if the ball rolls to the ropes; 6 points if the ball goes over the ropes without touching the ground) the social aspect of the day is enjoyable.
Seating is all general admission, at a cost of £15 (tickets
must
be bought in advance) and tickets can be purchased
here
.  We will all meet at the Hobbs Gate entrance to The Oval, which is less than a 5 minute walk from Oval Northern Line station, and a 10 minute walk from Vauxhall station.
There is no ODL cost to join this event; you will only need whatever spending money you wish.  If the match is called due to inclement weather we may pop round to a local pub.
If the event page is full, please waitlist and I can add you!  I've indicated 10 attendees primarily so I can gauge interest but can always increase the numbers!
Photos are courtesy of
www.unsplash.com
and
www.outdoorlads.com
and used with permission and thanks.
Difficulty & Distance: Sitting watching cricket all day!

Title: Spanning the Humber: Hessle to Barton Circular Walk
Summary:
Join Chris for a 10-mile (16 km) walk along the Humber Estuary, including a crossing of the Humber Bridge with wide views across the water. Opened in 1981, the bridge has a main span of 1,410 metres and was once the longest single-span suspension bridge in the world. It remains one of the longest bridges you can walk across in the UK.
After crossing, the route continues into Barton-upon-Humber and through Far Ings National Nature Reserve. We then visit the remains of the old cement works on the foreshore before walking beneath the Humber Bridge and stopping for a drink at the Viking Way Café, located at the start of the 147-mile Viking Way trail.
We then head back across the bridge towards Hessle. On the north side, we pass under the bridge again and walk down to the Humber foreshore. There is another stop at The Country Park Inn before continuing through Humber Bridge Country Park and returning to the start point.

Summary:

Games vary based on what those attending would like to play, but we mostly choose easy to learn games that anyone, from brand new players to seasoned veterans, can easily pick up and enjoy.

Title: Sunday jaunt over Blackstone Edge (circular)
Summary:
7.5 miles (12km) exposed circular walk on top of the hills (approx. 4.5 hours)
A Sunday afternoon circular upland walk across the Grasslands and stones of Blackstone Edge. These are the hills you see as you drive past Junction 22 of the M62. Rolling grassland scarred by fishers and fires. The walk will start close to Junction 22 (for ease of access) and head into and over the hills towards top of Cragg Vale. The route takes in views over Rochdale, Oldham, Manchester and beyond.
This is a very exposed walk. There are no shelters, trees or woodlands on route to shelter in - so if it's sunny and hot expect it to be very sunny and hot, and if it's wet and windy expect it to be very wet and very windy (although we do walk past the White House pub on the way around).
Please bring appropriate clothing and footwear.
Dogs:
Well-behaved, socialised and under-control dogs are welcome on this events. Dogs must be kept on a lead whenever we are around livestock, or when requested by the event leader.
Waiting List:
If this event is fully booked and you would like to attend, please add yourself to the waiting list. Places often become available closer to the date, and it is likely you will still get a place.
New Members:
If you are thinking of making this your first event with ODL and have any concerns or questions, please don't hesitate to drop me a message, or go to our
'My First Event
' page.
Start times:
We will aim to start on time, but appreciate different people have different needs. To accommodate this, I will aim to be at the meeting point 20 minutes before the start time (I will be wearing a bright red Outdoor Lads T-shirt). We will wait up-to 10 minutes after the start time for people to arrive before setting off.
Route mapped on OS Maps App, available from: BlobFish85
Photo: All taken by event leader
Difficulty & Distance: Upland walk on narrow, uneven and eroded paths - can be difficult underfoot in areas.

Title: Fiskerton, Thurgarton, a Train and the Trent
Summary:
Join Alex for a slow and largely flat 11.5km / 7 mile walk through Nottinghamshire villages and along the River Trent.
This walk starts and finishes at two different railway stations with a stop for a late lunch at a well regarded riverside pub.
Thurgarton was once so important it was the name of an
ancient subdivision of Nottinghamshire.
Now it is a quiet rural village.
On the way we pass two lakes and a village cricket pitch - a match was well underway when the route was recently recced.  The river section is long and gentle, with traditional Trent white clapper gates. We are walking part of the Trent Valley Way.
It's a linear walk - you will either need to park near one of the stations or arrive by train. Please make sure you read the details available to logged-in users below.

My First Event

Title: Birding Day in Lancashire and optional 5 mile walk
Summary:
Our day will be a perfect opportunity for anyone already a keen birder or an absolute beginner to explore the iconic birds and wildlife on one of Lancashire's prestigious reserves. We will hope to see and hear many common species up close, as well as birds including Marsh Harriers, Egrets, Bitterns, a variety of ducks and also the possibility of watching Otters and Red deer.
The afternoon will give a choice of further exploring the birdlife, or take the alternative option of a five mile walk with
Stephen
.
The latter 6 photos are for the walk
This 5 mile walk will be on the flat mainly with one steep climb near the beginning.
There will be a lot of woodlands on the walk. Passing Leighton Hall, a stone circle , Haweswater and walking through The Trough , the remain of an old quarry.
On our return we will reconvene with the other birders

to access the main reserve in the morning.
No dogs are allowed on the reserve.

Title: Wick, Upton Cheney and Bitton
Summary:
Join Andrew for this 9.5 mile walk covering the Golden Valley and villages of Wick, Upton Cheney, Swineford, North Stoke and Bitton. We will begin by in Swineford before ascending to North Stoke before continuing to Upton Cheney and Wick, where we will most likely eat lunch. Title: BSC26: New Member Camp Area
Summary:
Our biggest annual event can appear a little overwhelming, but don't panic or be put off from joining us.
If you're new (or relatively new) to OutdoorLads or
BIG Spring Camp
and worried about not knowing anybody when you arrive, then worry not! This is a super friendly event, and through taking part in activities, volunteering, or joining in the fun on-site, it's so easy to get to know others at the event.
You can also join one of our smaller 'new member camps', led by some of our amazing, approachable volunteers, who'll help introduce you to others on the first evening. You'll camp together with other new members, and have use of an event shelter for socialising near your tent.
What to expect;
You will be camping within a small defined area with other newbies with a central gazebo.
A couple of our leaders will be camping with you, happy to help put up tents, answer any queries and be a welcoming face to OutdoorLads.
On arrival on the Friday, there will be welcoming drinks and nibbles within the gazebo.
You will have access to the gazebo throughout the weekend.
Though the leaders welcoming duties end on the Friday, the gazebo will become a focal point through out the weekend, with members often cooking and chatting throughout the weekend.
You will need to sign up for the
BIG Spring Camp 2026
event page first.
If you're new to OutdoorLads and fancy a stop off in Chester en-route, check out
this social event the night before
in Chester!
Photos provided by OutdoorLads Members and used with permission.

Title: Bridgewater Canal Spring Stroll: Nature, Heritage & Pizza
Summary:
Join Amir and the OutdoorLads for a relaxed spring walk along one of Greater Manchester’s most scenic canal corridors, combining peaceful waterside paths, fascinating industrial heritage, and a great Italian finish!
We’ll begin at the RHS Bridgewater car park, setting off south towards the historic Bridgewater Canal. Once we reach the canal, we’ll follow the tranquil towpath through the beautiful surroundings of Bridgewater Park, enjoying calm waters, wildlife, and open countryside views as we walk.
Continuing along the canal, we’ll pass the marina at Boothstown before heading further west towards Astley and Whitehall Meadows. Along the way we’ll discover reminders of the region’s rich mining heritage, including Lancashire Mining Museum and the impressive mining headgear tower, standing as proud landmarks of the area’s industrial past.
After exploring the area, we’ll retrace our steps along the canal and return to our starting point.
To round off the day, those who fancy it can join us for a drink or bite to eat at the lovely Stables Café in RHS Bridgewater complex. This relaxed Italian café serves excellent coffee, light drinks (including limited alcoholic options), and some very good pizzas if you’re feeling hungry after the walk.
Optional Visit: RHS Bridgewater Gardens
If you’d like to extend your day, you can also join us to explore the nearby RHS Bridgewater Gardens.
Early spring is a particularly lovely time to visit — flowers are starting to bloom, the gardens are vibrant, and pollen levels are still relatively low in the air.
You can book your visit here:
Book RHS Bridgewater tickets online
Booking online is recommended as it’s cheaper than buying tickets at the entrance and guarantees your entry slot.
Ticket Prices
Adult (online pre-booked): from £16.80
Adult (at the gate): £17.80
Dogs
Well-behaved and socialised dogs are welcome on this walk.
Dogs must be kept on a lead whenever this is signposted and whenever we are in the vicinity of livestock, and at the event leader's request. It's a good idea to check what the route may be like with the event leader before booking to find out more about the potential suitability of the walk for dogs.
If you’re planning to visit RHS Bridgewater Gardens after the walk, it’s worth noting their dog policy.
For the protection of the plant collections and visitor experience, dogs are generally not allowed inside RHS Bridgewater Gardens. The only exception is registered guide, assistance or support dogs, which are always welcome.

Title: A Wilde Time In Reading @ The Picture of Dorian Gray
Summary:
Join us for a cultural afternoon supporting a local independent theatre for a production of one of Oscar Wilde's famous novels. Reading is synonymous with Oscar Wilde who was imprisoned at Reading Jail (hence the poem the Ballad Of Reading Gaol) after being convicted for gross indecency with other men in 1897, and served a 2 year sentence.
Tickets:
As this is a local production it is a very affordable event with general tickets just £16.55 (and concessions are available).
Please note that you are responsible for buying your own tickets in advance, which you can do by clicking
here
ensuring that you select the Saturday afternoon production. Tickets are on a first come-first serve basis, you can chose you own seats (we won't be able to sit together but it's a very small venue) and as this is the last night of the production I would suggest that you purchase asap.
Picture Of Dorian Gray:
This is the wording from the Progress Theatre's own page:
'The Picture of Dorian Gray tells the story of a beautiful young man who makes a Faustian bargain: offering his soul for his portrait to age and bear the toll of his sins, while he remains eternally youthful.
In this inclusive production we witness Dorian’s descent into decadence, corruption and depravity. Yet he remains unblemished whilst his portrait manifests his age and degradation.
Our cast and crew are drawn from diverse parts of our community, adding richness and meaning to this vibrant adaptation of Oscar Wilde’s groundbreaking work and respecting and incorporating Wilde’s allusions to gay relationships within the work'.
Reading's Progress Theatre:
Located near the Reading University Campus, the theatre is the oldest in Reading, opening in 1946, is self funded and run by volunteers. With just 96 seats it's an intimate venue and prides itself as an accessible and inclusive space.

Title: 1000m Malvern Circuit Challenge
Summary:
This is one of the toughest walks in the hill walks category, with as much ascent as a mountain like Snowdon, and incredible views across swathes of England. The scenery is breath-taking, and the flora, geology and traditions fascinating - we will touch on those along the way.
The full walk is a circuit of 14K including 1000m of ascent. The first half of this goes to Wyche. If you wish to do half of the walk (which is 6K and 600m ascent) you may leave here and take a bus back to the car park (details below).
The challenge begins with a steep ascent above North Quarry and onto
North Hill
, followed by a steep descent into Green Valley. After this warm up, a gentler panorama around the eastern side of the hills gives us time to see some of the features of the plants and rock to be found. Note the pink granite in places, and the Penny Wort clinging to cracks.
Next, it’s a steep climb right to the top of
Worcestershire Beacon
, at 425m the highest point on the Malvern Hills. If visibility is good, we’ll have a go at recognising hills on the horizon. It is said even Exmoor is visible on a clear day.
After this we walk alongside Shire Ditch, one of many ancient earthworks on the hills dating back over 2000 years. A steep descent follows, zig-zagging to a disused quarry and clear, still lake.
Malvern quarries
provided excellent hard road stone, and this quarry was even searched for gold, but did not yield enough for commercial mining. Shales and silts here contain fossils 435 million years old. The rocks are very hard and good for use in construction.
After a second quarry, there is a scrambly slope up to re-join the ridge line. We descend to the cutting at Wyche where there are toilets and a shelter, if needed, for our lunch stop. You’ve now covered just over half way.
Continuing, we make a circuit of
Perseverance Hill,
then follow the ridge line northwards. After a mile or so, we descend westwards to find a
spout
, one of the most popular collecting points for the Malvern waters, reputed to have healing properties. It’s then an ascent back to Worcestershire Beacon, for our second visit to the highest point during the day.
The authors
Tolkien and C.S. Lewis
were good friends and frequented the Malvern Hills together. The gas lamps in Great Malvern are a reminder of Narnia. As Tolkien walked the same paths as you are now taking, he imagined the White Mountains of Gondor and other scenes famous in his Lord of the Rings trilogy.
We now complete the ridge northwards over
Sugar loaf hill, Table Hill and End Hill
, descending down a zigzag path to the Clock Tower where we started.
Check your progress with these stages:
Location                               Covered               To go
North Hill                             1.16K 237m        12.44K 803m
Green Valley                       1.79K 237m        11.81K 803m
Worcestershire Beacon  3.48K 484m       10.12K 556m
Quarry lake                         4.52K 488m       9.08K 552m
Wyche Toilets                     5.90K 601m       7.70K 439m
Perseverance Hill              6.79K 658m       6.81K 382m

Follow the
route
on OS Maps.
At the finish you will be awarded a Champion’s Medal for taking part in this demanding and rewarding circuit. You can rightly feel proud of your achievement in completing one of the very best hill walks in Britain, and have a well deserved drink and meal having burned off around 3000 calories over the walk!
If you wish to do half the walk, you can come as far as Wyche, from where
Bus 675
(depart 1333  or 1542) takes you back to the car park. Pay contactless.
Difficulty & Distance: 14K with 1000m of height gain and brisk pace

Title: Newcastle Beer Festival Social
Summary:
Join Craig and OutdoorLads for this social event in Newcastle.
The
Newcastle Beer Festival
is organised by
CAMRA
. They are expecting to have 100+ real ales and 30+ ciders and perries selected from a wide range of independent national brewers.
Please note that there will be an entrance charge. There is a discount for CAMRA members. It is cheaper to book ahead. Ticket prices start at £10 for admission only, but I'd recommend the starter pack or whole hog, which includes tokens. The entrance price includes your commemorative glass, but this can be returned for a £2 refund. Additional tokens can be purchased for 85p. Each beer/cider will have a tariff sheet requiring multiple tokens. CAMRA expects the average beer to be 2 tokens for a third or 3 tokens for a half. You can buy your ticket
here
. Remember to choose the right day, as the event runs over 4 days.
Come and join us for a few drinks, find out about planned and forthcoming events, make new friends and catch up with people you have met before! If you’re not familiar with Newcastle, it’s a wonderful city renowned for its friendliness.
If the event is fully booked:
Please join the wait list. People often drop out in the week leading up to an event, so you are likely to get a place.
Image credits:
Banner image
Adam Wilson
on
Unsplash
. Main image
Paul Lievens on Unsplash.
Other images provided by event co-leader. OutdoorLads has permission to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: Social event

Title: Telford to Shrewsbury - Easy Road Cycle - Any bike (35km)
Summary:
Join us on this easy-ish road ride from Telford train station to Shrewsbury (35km) with the option of cycling back again or catching the train.
You don't need a fancy bike or lycra, but you will need a fully functional bike and have a good cycling proficiency.  Mountain bike, gravel bike, road bike, electric bike or Brompton, the choice is yours, though you will need to be able to cycle 35km (approximately 2-3 hours) along quiet country lanes.
At Shrewsbury you can either call it a day and catch the train back to Telford or put your big boy pants on and cycle back again (after having lunch in Shrewsbury - I'm not an animal).
Any questions, send the leader a DM.
Keep in touch
Want to hear more about what we're up to? Then join us on
Facebook
and
Strava
Photos:
All photos taken by the Leader Will and used with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: 35km with a little ascent.

Title: Welsh boathouse three-night adventure
Summary:
Join us for an unforgettable long weekend retreat (three nights!) in a remote and idyllic coastal hostel in Ceredigion at the stunning Ty Cwch. Nestled in the picturesque cove of Cwmtydu, this unique accommodation offers breathtaking ocean views, glorious sunsets, and dark skies perfect for stargazing. Cwmtydu is on one of the most spectacular stretches on the Ceredigion Heritage Coast, with picturesque towns and villages, a wealth of wildlife, and a large variety of plants and insects.
Cardigan Bay has the largest population of bottlenose dolphins in Europe, seals come on to Cwmtydu beach to have their pups, there’s an abundance of sea birds nesting on the cliffs, (lesser black backed gulls, oyster catchers, guillemots, razorbills, kittiwakes, fulmers, shags, and peregrine falcons), and the next valley is home to one of the few sites for the pearl-bordered fritillary butterfly in Wales.
Expect plenty of time for clifftop walks, coastal exploration, and enjoying the company of your fellow OutdoorLads in this truly magical setting. Don’t miss out on this one-of-a-kind retreat!
We’ll have two and a half days to explore the stunning coastline and relax in this unique boathouse made from repurposed shipping containers for a perfect fun nautical theme. With fully insulated and heated living spaces and outdoor infra-red heaters under canopy, its an ideal for enjoying an evening sun-downer while soaking in the beauty of the Welsh seascape. Nemo will be mixing up some optional sea-themed cocktails too!
Feast with Nemo on a weekend of fabulous food including a barbecue, delightful desserts and fun entertainment.
With its incredible location and a touch of ‘Nemo’ magic, this promises to be an extra special summer 2026 getaway. Don’t miss out—sign up now for a long weekend of adventure, relaxation, and memorable moments on the Welsh Coast!
Dogs:
Sorry, this event is not suitable for dogs.
Note:
This event is for three nights (Arrive Friday, leave Monday morning.) If you can only do two nights, then that is fine, you can leave Sunday if needed.
Want to Get Out More?
Why not check out the South Wales and South West Calendar
here
.
OutdoorLads Foundation
Fancy coming along but having a difficult time of it (for whatever reason), and/or finding it hard to justify the expense? Apply for a place on this event through the OutdoorLads Foundation! Nobody apart from staff members will know and we don't need lots of financial info from you either. To apply through the Foundation, you'll need to complete a very short form, which can be found along with more information
here
.
Want to Pay By Instalments?
You can arrange to pay in instalments if that helps. Please contact the office on
support@outdoorlads.com
who will help you to secure a place and set up paying in instalments. The event must be fully paid up a week before the event takes place.
Cover photo credit:
Image taken Spring 2025 by findingnemo
Image photo credits:
Photo #1 & #2 courtesy of
https://tycwch.wales
Other images taken Spring 2025 by findingnemo
Difficulty & Distance: 8-10 miles walk

Title: Pride (the musical!)
Summary:
Don't miss this opportunity to see this production before it transfers to London's National Theatre.
1984, the miners are on a national strike, a group of gay men and lesbians form an unlikely alliance with a small Welsh mining town.
Funny and poignant, the play brings together the film's writer and director set to an original score of protest songs, pop, rock and traditional Welsh choral music as two vastly different communities come together in friendship  and mutual support in the face of adversity and oppression.
Please don't wait too long if you want to come along, remaining tickets are low, at the time of writing there are several seats in rows K and J. I have booked J23 and there is other seating available in this area so we can sit together if you are quick.
Tickets are available directly from the theatre website priced at £39, £37 concession and £19.50 for the under 25 year olds.
Please secure your ticket from the theatre BEFORE adding yourself to the event page.
Want to Get Out More?
Check out our
South Wales & South West Calendar View

miner portrait Tunaolger Pixabay.com
Difficulty & Distance: Getting to the venue is the only effort

Title: Harrogate Stray and Showground Stroll
Summary:
Join Mike on this walk which starts in the centre of Harrogate. The walk will encompass The Stray, a 200-acre protected area of open parkland which is famous for its crocuses and cherry blossom trees. We will also visit the Yorkshire Showground, home of the Great Yorkshire show, England’s largest agricultural show, which is held annually.
Well behaved dogs are allowed on this walk but would need to be on a lead for large parts of the route as we will be walking on footpaths and pavements in quiet residential areas. We will stop halfway for our packed lunches. After the walk there will be an opportunity to go to a local pub/café in Harrogate town centre.
Distance: Approx. 8-9 miles
Image credits: All photographs taken by walk leader. OutdoorLads has permission to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: Some inclines and declines but mostly flat

Title: Osmotherly Day Walk - North Yorkshire Moors
Summary:
Join Lee on one of his regular ODL walks that takes in great views around the picturesque village of Osmotherley, with lake, river, forest, and moorland vistas. This includes views across to the Yorkshire Dales and Roseberry Topping, Cod Beck Reservoir, as well as the village itself! There may also be the chance of a post-walk ice cream in the car park and/or visit to one of the village pubs or cafe.
Well behaved dogs welcome one this walk.
If the event is fully booked:
please join the waiting list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event and you are likely to get a place on the walk.
Your First OutdoorLads Event?
We know that new members in particular like to know in advance more about what an OutdoorLads event will be like.
If this is your first OutdoorLads event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the OutdoorLads website.
Image credits: all images provided by event Leader. OutdoorLads has permission to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: 13.5km with some hills

Title: Hike in the Howardian Hills
Summary:
Join Lee on a 12km (7.5 mile) hike in the heart of the picturesque Howardian Hills in North Yorkshire. The route will take us along the Fryton Millenium Trail and up onto a ridge, which will give us great views of the North Yorkshire countryside.
If it is open when we pass, we may have the opportunity (if the group wishes) to stop off at
The Grapes Inn
in the village of
Slingsby
, for a quick beverage before continuing the walk. The pub is dog friendly according to their site, indeed "friendly to any 4-legged animal" ...just leave your goats and sheep at home please ;).
Well-behaved dogs are welcome on this walk; however, please note we will be going through some fields containing horses so they will need to be kept on a lead a certain parts of the route.
Although we won't be walking nearby,
Yorkshire Lavender
is a short drive away for those wanting to include this on their visit.
If the event is fully booked:
please join the waiting list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event and you are likely to get a place on the walk.
Your First OutdoorLads Event?

If this is your first OutdoorLads event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the OutdoorLads website.
Image credits: all images provided by event Leader. OutdoorLads has permission to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: The 12km (7.5 mile) route is mainly on mud and gravel tracks. The first quarter of the walk is very flat with the whole walk being in total 130m of ascent (gentle slopes).

Title: Riverside walk in York
Summary:
Join Lee for a 13km (8 mile) fairly flat walk down the picturesque River Ouse at York. This walk will take us along the river where we'll make a stop by
The Riverfront
in Bishopthorpe where you can buy some food or drink or munch on your packed lunch. We'll then head down river and stop at a pub about half-way round the walk. After which we'll loop inland, across fields and the racecourse.
Although not part of the walk, if anyone wishes to stay and go to a pub in York for food, there will be that option.
Well-behaved dogs welcome.
If the event is fully booked:
please join the waiting list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event and you are likely to get a place on the walk.

If this is your first OutdoorLads event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the OutdoorLads website.
