If this is your first OutdoorLads event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the OutdoorLads website.
All photos taken by the event leader
Difficulty & Distance: Social and leisure walk

Title: A Gay Summer Day in Blackpool
Summary:
Join the OutdoorLads for A Gay Saturday Summer Stroll in Blackpool !
Join us for a delightful stroll along the famous promenade of Blackpool, where the fresh sea air, lively atmosphere, and sweeping views across the Irish Sea create the perfect setting for a memorable day out !
We will wander along the seafront, and take in the iconic sights and sounds that make this classic seaside resort so special — from the cheerful chime of amusement arcades to the laughter drifting from the beach.
Along the way, we’ll pass some of Blackpool’s most beloved landmarks, including Blackpool Tower rising proudly above the skyline, (a symbol of the town since 1894). There will be plenty of opportunities to pause for photos and have a look in the shops.
We usually stop for refreshments at the beautiful North Pier, the oldest and most tranquil of Blackpool’s three Victorian piers. Here, we can relax with a drink, and enjoy traditional seaside treats.
After soaking up the atmosphere and stunning views, we’ll make our way back at a comfortable pace and grab some lunch.
This is a fun, friendly, and flexible day out, tailored to what the group would most enjoy. If there’s somewhere else you’d like to visit — whether it’s the dazzling promenade lights, a cosy café, or another local attraction — we can adapt and make the day truly our own.
There are plenty of car parks in Blackpool, and there is free parking in the streets. A charge applies if you wish to park on the prom. Blackpool North and Blackpool South train stations are easily accessible. There are, of course, the trams which you can use. Alternatively, you may wish to utilise a horse-drawn fairy princess carriage !
Well-behaved, socialised and under-control dogs are welcome on this event.
Dogs must be kept on a lead whenever this is signposted and whenever we are in the vicinity of livestock, and at the event leader's request. It's a good idea to check what the route may be like with the event leader before booking to find out more about the potential suitability of the walk for dogs.
Please note that many establishments in Blackpool are dog friendly, however, some establishments may not allow dogs on the premises.
Many of us usually book accommodation over this weekend, so why not  make a weekend of it and take advantage of the variety of hotels and B & B's in the area ?!

here...

Title: Crook Peak,  Wavering Down and Shute Shelve
Summary:
This is a 17 km (10.5 mile) circular route over the Western end of the Mendip Hills, taking in the carboniferous limestone ridge of Shute Shelve, Crook Peak and Wavering Down, as well as the ancient woodland of King's Wood.
Starting from the car park at Cheddar reservoir, the route heads out across farmland beside the Cheddar Yeo towards Compton Bishop, before ascending to to the summit of Crook Peak (191 meters).  From here we walk along the ridge of the Mendip Hills taking in Wavering Down (210 meters) and Shute Shelve Hill (233m) before dropping down through woodland into the small market town of Axbridge and from here we return to the Cheddar reservoir.
The Mendip Hills are designated a National Landscape, and with good visibility can provide far reaching views across the Somerset levels towards Glastonbury Tor, Brent Knoll and the Quantock Hills.

Title: Reading Indoor Bouldering
Summary:
This bouldering session presents an ideal opportunity for a fun evening of what I call sociable workout where we take on a wide range of physical (and in fact mental) challenges with the atmosphere always friendly and cheerful. All levels are welcome!
The beauty of bouldering lies in its simplicity: you don't need ropes or a harness, just some climbing shoes which can be hired at the centre and a drive to get the top and overcome some trepidation. It's also a fundamentally social experience, with most of the session spent discussing how to solve particular climbing routes, aka problems, or catching up with each other in between climbs. Bouldering is a great way to get into climbing, come and see what all the fuss is about!
All skill levels are welcome
. If you're new to climbing, we've got you covered with tailored guidance, boosting your skills and confidence while ensuring your safety on and around the wall. Don’t worry about needing to invest in gear before you try. The Climbing Hangar offers
free shoe rentals
so you don’t have to shell out for shoes until you know bouldering is for you.
Experienced boulderer? Dive in and embrace the challenges independently. Just remember to follow the centre's rules for your own safety.
Our ethos is inclusivity. I keep our group tight-knit to foster connection and ensure personalised coaching for everyone, regardless of your experience level. Many climbers started their journeys relatively “late” in life, incl. some session leaders
🤫
We convene at the
Climbing Hangar in Reading
, one of the largest climbing centres in the region. The generously spacious and friendly cafe/lounge is my personal favourite where you can find us socialising, having a cuppa, snacks or even pizza before and after the climbs.
If you have never climbed at the Climbing Hangar in Reading before, you must book an induction slot:
https://www.theclimbinghangar.com/locations/reading
- follow instructions to book an induction slot around the time that this event starts.
You also need to
complete the waiver form that is linked in the confirmation email
once you've booked an induction slot.
This only needs to be done once.

* Berkshire, Oxfordshire, Buckinghamshire, Hertfordshire & Bedfordshire

Title: Board Game Cafe - Birmingham
Summary:
An evening of board games, food, drink and probably polite company.  Join Outdoorlads for an evening at the popular board game cafe in Birmingham.
There's a small cover charge of £2.50 per person, per hour. This is paid when the leader books the table, so expect a message the day before to confirm your attendnace.  We will likely play in small groups of 5.
If the event is full, join the waiting list and I'll book a additional tables if there is enough interest.
Social events are great way to forge new friendships and talk about your experiences on OutdoorLads events, and to find out what is going on in the coming months.
Want to keep in touch with what's going on in the Midlands / ODL Central Region?   Sign up for

and the
Central Region Telegram Chat Group

https://outdoorlads.events/central
.
Photos by Outdoorwill and used with his permission.

Title: NOTTINGHAM Indoor Climbing – THURSDAY – Beginners Welcome
Summary:
Join OutdoorLads at Nottingham Climbing Centre
Climbing evenings are held regularly and we tend to go to the local pub – The Lion for refreshments and a social gathering afterwards where they have a wide variety of beverages both alcoholic and non-alcoholic.
NOTE: If you are climbing with us for the first time
, either as an experienced climber or beginner, please send a note to the
Event Leader
telling them about your experience and goals. There is a limit to the number of beginners that we can accommodate at each session. Each beginner needs to be supervised so we need to ensure there are enough experienced climbers to help you out. Climbing is great fun! It's a fab workout, in a social environment, and anyone can do it! Give it a go!
Nottingham Climbing Centre
Nottingham Climbing Centre is equipped with walls and routes to suit all skill levels and groups of climbers, whether you are a novice looking to try climbing for the first time, or an experienced lead climber, you’ll find an area to challenge you. With 28 Top Ropes routes available as well as 24 Lines of Lead climbing for the more advanced individuals. In addition, 6 auto-belays are available, so there is always the opportunity to climb, even if the numbers don’t match up. The available bouldering walls also provide that extra physical and mental challenge for those looking for something slightly different from rope work. Both Bouldering and Rope Climbing routes range from 4a – 8a.
I'm new to Climbing - can I come along?
If you've never climbed before or need a refresher, the leader will sign you into the climbing centre as a
supervised climber
so that you can come and give it a try (it can be helpful to message the leader to let them know). If you enjoy your climbing experience, and would like to come climbing more regularly, we will also happily show you the ropes and teach you everything you need to know to climb safely.
Do I need to be Supervised as a Climber?
If you answer
NO
to any of the following three questions, then you will need to arrange to be supervised. If you can answer YES to all these questions, you can sign up to this event as normal:
Can you fit a climbing harness correctly?
Can you tie yourself to the end of a rope using the correct knots?
Can you belay another climber correctly and confidently?
To come along, you will need to register via this event page and to climb you will need to pay an entry fee directly to the climbing centre when you arrive, please see their
website
for up-to-date prices. We ask that all members sign up through the event page to make sure the event leader has all information of the members attending.
To climb at Nottingham Climbing Centre you will need to:
-     Complete a climbing waiver for the centre (please arrive early to do this the first time, or better still complete it online before you arrive)
-     Pay the Lifetime Registration Fee - £6.00
-     Pay an Entry Fee directly to the climbing centre - £9.50 (ensure you mention ODL)
-     Hire shoes (if you don’t have your own) - £3.50*
-     Hire a harness (again, if you don’t have your own) - £3.50*
-     Hire shoes and harness - £6.00*
*Equipment hire charges will be waived for your first visit to the centre!
Can I just come along and boulder?
Absolutely! Most of us do roped climbing, but if you prefer bouldering, you can sign yourself in and do it, as long as you can fill in the climbing centre's form correctly, watch their
safety video
and can answer the simple safety questions at reception on your first visit. Meet the group at the start; some others might be bouldering. We will make sure we come and find you when we have our tea break!
If you have any questions or need any more information just get in touch with the event leader by clicking on their profile below, or take a look at the
My first climbing event page
for more information.
Photos:
All images supplied by event leaders and used by OutdoorLads with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Beginners welcome.

Title: Fire Bird Double Weekend - Day 2 of 2 Walk
Summary:
This is a circular walk in the beautiful countryside of West Sussex. Meeting in the delightful village of Rudgwick we cross the upper reaches of the River Arun, then through fields and woods, passing through the lovely village of Slindon.
Fancy making a weekend of it?
We are camping at Riverside Farm for the weekend and you are more than welcome to join us.
http://www.riversidefarm-camping.uk/
Day 1 walk is also live:
https://www.outdoorlads.com/events/fire-bird-double-weekender-day-1-2-walk-260523
Rudgwick and the Low Weald
Around Rudgwick, the landscape is defined by the
Low Weald
. It has a 'patchworky' feel with small, irregular fields are bordered by thick, ancient hedgerows and "shaws" (narrow strips of woodland). Expect to see dense woods with oak, hazel, and hornbeam. In spring, these woods are famously carpeted in bluebells. Our route takes us along the
Downs Link
—a long-distance bridleway following a dismantled railway line that offers a flat, green corridor through the countryside.
Slinfold
Slinfold is a quintessential Wealden village located about 4 miles west of Horsham. It is often cited as one of the most picturesque villages in West Sussex, characterized by its "linear" layout and a deep sense of historical continuity. We'll see a mix of timber-framed medieval cottages, Georgian houses, and Victorian brickwork. The village is home to over 50 Listed Buildings.
St. Peter’s Church
was rebuilt in 1861 by Benjamin Ferrey, this handsome church with its prominent spire sits at the village's center. It was built on the site of an earlier medieval church and remains a focal point for the community.
Dogs:
I love having dogs on my walks. I just ask that owners please ensure they are kept on a lead as appropriate, especially around livestock and when near busy roads. Please, also remember that not everyone is a dog lover.
This particular walk does have stiles so please ensure your dog is comfortable with these.
Don’t Waste That Space!
Walks get filled up quite quickly, so please only sign up if you know you are free to attend. Plans do sometimes change, but remember to free up your space so someone else can attend.
New Members:
If this is your first event or you are new to OutdoorLads we’d love to see you. Please message me if you have any questions.
Timings
Whilst every endeavour is made to make the timings of this event accurate, it cannot be guaranteed that we will finish on time and there may be delays.
All photos used for this event have been taken from the Outdoorlads image library with deemed permission from the contributors of those images.
Difficulty & Distance: Distance is approximately 15 km with a total elevation (climb) of 173 metres. There are no steep hills but paths may be narrow or overgrown in places.

Summary:
This bouldering session presents an ideal opportunity for a fun evening of what I call sociable workout where we take on a wide range of physical (and in fact mental) challenges with the atmosphere always friendly and cheerful. All levels are welcome!
The beauty of bouldering lies in its simplicity: you don't need ropes or a harness, just some climbing shoes which can be hired at the centre and a drive to get the top and overcome some trepidation. It's also a fundamentally social experience, with most of the session spent discussing how to solve particular climbing routes, aka problems, or catching up with each other in between climbs. Title: Derwent Water, Back Tor and Lost Lad
Summary:
This 10-mile walk in the Upper Derwent Valley, often referred to as Derbyshire's Lake District, features beautiful landscape views from the rugged wilderness, peace and tranquility of open moorland.
From Fairholmes, the walk begins from the mighty Derwent Water (reservoir) dam constructed between 1902 and 1916 to supply fresh water throughout the region. It was used during WWII as a practice run by 617 Squadron for the famous "Dam buster’s" bouncing bombs. We take in great views from the water's edge towards Howden dam before we head upwards along a hidden valley and up onto open moorland.
Howden Moor and Howden Edge form one of the wildest, least-disturbed upland landscapes in England. This remote expanse of heather moorland, peat bog, and gritstone outcrops is a haven for wildlife such as grouse, mountain hares, golden plovers, and short-eared owls. Howden Edge offers sweeping views across the Upper Derwent Valley and towards Bleaklow.
We head southwards onto Cartledge Stones Ridge to the gritstone outcrop of Back Tor, the highest point of the walk, at 538 metres (1765 feet). There is a short scramble up to the trig point.
Nearby, Lost Lad, standing at 518 metres (1,699 feet) has scenic views towards the reservoirs and the surrounding peaks, and a legend about a shepherd boy who became lost in a blizzard, with the words "Lost Lad" found nearby after his body was discovered.
Back Tor and Lost Lad are two of the 95 "Ethel" hills, a tribute to Ethel Haythornthwaite who pioneered the establishment of the Peak District as Britain's first national park in 1951.
From the ridge we descend via Walker's Clough and back to Derwent Water.
Dogs:
Well behaved dogs are welcome but owners must ensure they are kept under control at all times.
Note:
If there are no places left on this event, join the waiting list, as people frequently drop out during the week before an event, and you are likely to get a place.

Title: A Cut-Acre Above the Rest, with Optional Home Cooked Lunch
Summary:
Get ready for a fantastic Saturday adventure with
Andy
and
OutdoorLads
as we explore the breathtaking
Cutacre Country Park
!
This stunning 7-mile walk takes us through 530 acres of rolling countryside, tucked behind the impressive Logistics North development. Expect sweeping panoramic views stretching from the
Derbyshire Dales
and
Manchester
in the east, across the
Cheshire Plain
, all the way to the
Clwydian Mountains
and the sparkling
Mersey Estuary
in the west. It’s big skies, open landscapes, and fresh air at its very best.
We’ll set off across the park, pausing at peaceful Swan Lake for a relaxed coffee break and a chance to soak in the scenery. After a short stop at Astley Park, we’ll continue on to the historic town of
Tyldesley
.
Here, you’ll have the option to round off the day at the ever-popular
The Union Arms
, famed for its excellent real ales and delicious, freshly prepared home-cooked meals. It’s the perfect reward after a satisfying day on the trail!
Arriving early? There’s also a lovely bakery at the start point serving hot drinks, bacon butties, fresh sandwiches, pies, pasties — and of course, cake.
Lace up your boots, bring your appetite, and join us for a day of great company, great views, and great food! 🌿🥾
For more information visit:
https://www.lancswt.org.uk/nature-reserves/cutacre
(The Wildlife Trust)
https://www.leighjournal.co.uk/news/24279390.union-arms-brings-together-regulars-dementia-groups-tyldesley/
https://www.facebook.com/UnionArmsTyldesley/
(Union Arms)
https://unionarmstyldesley.co.uk/
(Union Arms menu's, including non-gluten and vegan options)
https://www.firstbus.co.uk/greater-manchester/routes-and-maps/vantage
(GM Guided Bus Info.)

This is a dog friendly event so please bring your pooches along - the more the merrier. The Union is also dog friendly and they love our furry friends !
Dogs must be kept on a lead whenever this is signposted and whenever we are in the vicinity of livestock, and at the event leader's request. It's a good idea to check what the route may be like with the event leader before booking to find out more about the potential suitability of the walk for dogs.

Title: The Paths of Port Sunlight
Summary:
Join me as I guide you on another Wirral Wandering trail.
Starting and finishing in the historic village of Port Sunlight, we’ll enjoy a gentle walk out to the lesser-known Port Sunlight River Park, where you’ll be treated to sweeping 180-degree views of the Liverpool waterfront from an angle rarely seen in photographs.
From there, we’ll follow the route along Rock Ferry Promenade, home to what was once a busy ferry terminal, before heading back through New Ferry to explore even more of Port Sunlight’s charm.
Distance:
10 km (6 miles)
Dogs:
Well-behaved, socialised, and fully under-control dogs are welcome. Dogs must be kept on a lead where signposted and whenever we are near sheep or livestock.
New Members:
Please feel free to sign up to this as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions, don’t hesitate to message me, or visit the “My First Event” section under “About Us”.
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event

Title: Schools Out Midweek Royal Park & Gardens Big Circuit
Summary:
Please note that this event is on a Wednesday during the May Half Term Holidays
Midweek Escape - 13 Miles Enjoying Windsor Great Park
Join us for a civilised midweek escape – a walk designed for those who are either enjoying the pleasure of retirement, have flexible work hours, are teachers off for half term, those wishing to explore the boundary limits of working from home or just fancy taking a sneaky day off. This is not merely a walk in the park as being 13 miles long it's still enough for a decent workout, and with an expected smaller group than typical weekend walks attract it should feel relaxed, friendly and more intimate.
We begin in Sunningdale, conveniently accessible by train, and explore the southern and central reaches of Windsor Great Park. Highlights include the Valley Gardens (free entry), Obelisk Pond, the deer park, the Copper Horse with its sweeping views over the Long Walk, Cow Pond, Chapel Wood and an extended stretch beside Virginia Water.
The Sites:
Windsor Great Park
A historic royal park of approximately 5,000 acres, comprising ancient woodland, open grassland, formal avenues and lakes, including
Virginia Water
. It originated in the 13th century as private hunting grounds for
Windsor Castle
.
Valley Gardens
A significant woodland garden on the northern shore of Virginia Water, with undulating valleys planted with azaleas, rhododendrons, magnolias and camellias. Developed from the 1940s under
George VI
, the gardens are particularly striking in spring.
Copper Horse
An imposing equestrian statue of
George III
, commissioned by
George IV
and unveiled in 1831. Standing on Snow Hill, it marks the southern end of the Long Walk and is visible for miles across the surrounding countryside.
Virginia Water
An 18th-century man-made lake created as part of royal landscape design. Notable features include a 100-foot totem pole gifted by British Columbia and ornamental ruins relocated from Leptis Magna. A full circuit of the lake measures approximately five miles.
The Route:
Starting at Sunningdale Station, we tackle the least attractive section first: a short stretch along the A30. We then cut through Coworth Park and the polo grounds before entering via the southern gate to Virginia Water.
We cross Five Arch Bridge and loop through the Valley Gardens (where the rhododendrons should be in full bloom), then follow woodland paths overlooking Obelisk Pond and on towards Cumberland Lodge. A brief stretch along the Three Castles Path brings us to the halfway point at the Copper Horse, where we stop for lunch.
Afterwards, we continue through the deer park, skirt Cow Pond, walk alongside Obelisk Pond, pass the entrance to Savill Garden and the totem pole, and then enjoy an extended stretch along the southern edge of Virginia Water. At the western end, there is an optional stop at an outdoor café. We then exit the park and return to Sunningdale via a different route.
The route can be found
here
:
Dogs:
Well behaved dogs are welcome. Whilst in the Great Park dogs need to be on the lead in some of the sections where we are walking to include the Obelisk Lawn, Valley Gardens, the Deer Park and the visitors centre if we go there. There are some road sections in and out of Sunningdale where dogs need to be on a lead.
Photos

Difficulty & Distance: 13 mile walk with only gentle climbs

Title: Priscilla, Queen of the Desert - The Musical
Summary:
Priscilla, Queen of the Desert
, based on the 1994 Oscar-winning film, is back with a dazzling, brand new 30th anniversary tour!
This joyous Broadway and West End hit musical takes you on a hilarious, heart-warming ride as three friends hit the road across the Outback to put on the show of a lifetime.
Priscilla, Queen of the Desert explores identity, diversity and the journey towards self-acceptance by challenging societal norms as the characters confront prejudice and adversity. It’s a true celebration of the power of love, friendship, unity, being unapologetically yourself and embracing others for who they are.
Starring
Adèle Anderson
(
Fascinating Aïda
) as Bernadette,
Kevin Clifton
(
Strictly Come Dancing, Chicago
) as Tick/Mitzi,
Nick Hayes
(
Groundhog Day, Remembrance Monday
) as Felicia/Adam, and
Peter Duncan
(
Hairspray, Pretender
) as Bob/Preacher, this production is bolder and more fabulous than ever.

Sheffield Theatres website
.

We may go to a nearby bar for a couple of drinks after the performance.

Title: Flamborough Head - Coastal Walk
Summary:
A flat 10.5 miles (17km) hike starting from the elegant grounds of
Sewerby Hall. T
he route leads us straight to the clifftop path where the North Sea opens out beside us. The early stretch is a perfect warm‑up: wide grassy paths, fresh sea air, and panoramic views back across Bridlington Bay.
As the cliffs rise, the landscape shifts from gentle to spectacular. Approaching
Flamborough Head
, the iconic lighthouse stands sentinel over a coastline carved by centuries of wind and waves. This is where the walk becomes truly dramatic. The chalk cliffs tower higher, the sea deepens to a rich blue, and seabirds circle in their thousands. Passing
High Stacks
, you’ll witness one of the most impressive natural amphitheatres on the east coast, sheer chalk walls, echoing surf, and the constant movement of life on the ledges.
The path then curves north toward
Thornwick Bay
, a rugged inlet framed by arches and caves. Whether the tide is out revealing rock pools or in creating a sheltered cove, it’s a place that invites a pause. The scenery here is raw, sculptural, and endlessly photogenic.
Turning inland, the route leads you into
Flamborough village
, a welcome contrast to the wild coast. Stone cottages, traditional pubs, and a peaceful village green.
The return to Sewerby follows quiet footpaths through open farmland, with wide skies and gentle terrain.
(I will be participating at
Sewerby Park parkrun
before this event should you want to join that. I'll be camping for the weekend at South Cliff Holiday Park for the weekend. You are welcome to join me there for all or part of the weekend but this won't be part of the official OutdoorLads event).
Photo Credits: Paul Alison, Matthew Chadwick, Christine Matthews, Neil Theasby, and Paul Harrop - Geograph
Difficulty & Distance: The walk is relatively flat, it is exposed to the elements on the coast and is a reasonable distance.

Title: Parkrun - Sewerby Park - Bridlington
Summary:
Join Richard and lots of other folk for a 5k run at
Sewerby Park - Bridlington
parkrun, is a weekly event that takes place every Saturday at locations across the UK. Whilst I'll be running, you can walk or jog the 5k at your own pace. I've been park running for 5 years and enjoy the positive, welcoming and inclusive atmosphere created at these events. As someone who wouldn't call myself a runner, these events are a great way to motivate you to get up and active on a Saturday morning. There is no time limit and, no one finishes last. The Couch to 5k App can be really useful as you develop your 5k runs.
(Following this parkrun, I will be leading a
Coastal walk from Sewerby around Flamborough Head
, which you might like to join me for also)
Course Description:
The course is one lap. Taking in some beautiful scenery at the entrance to Sewerby Park and the cliff top area.
About parkrun
While parkrun is a public event, if joining as part of OutdoorLads please do join this event page (and drop off it if you can no longer attend) so the leader knows who to expect and who to inform if there is a change of plans..
The course is 5000m (5km) long. This has been accurately measured by parkrun with a professional measuring wheel. It's run on paths and grass, although some sections of the course do accumulate mud, leaves and puddles after rain.  Dependent on availability, marshals will be at key sections of the course, or signs will be in place.
The important bit... This is about personal goals. You run/walk at your own pace, don't be scared that you may be slower than other people. Some walk, some run, some jog, some do a combination of all three.
To take part in a parkrun and get your official time,
you will need to have
registered
to receive your all important barcode
.
You will need to bring this barcode with you either printed or on your phone.
Parkrun events are run nationwide and indeed internationally, so once you're signed up you can attend anywhere.  If you don't have a barcode you can still run but won't get a time.
Please see under Location for meeting plans.
The event link is here:
https://www.parkrun.org.uk/sewerby/
Dogs
parkrun allow one dog per person on a short lead (not extending).  Running with more than one dog, extending leads or waist leads are not permitted at parkrun events.  As a third party event, OutdoorLads does not have any control over this.
OutdoorLads Running Groups
If you want to join the Outdoorlads' national group on the parkrun website, on your parkrun account select Profile, then groups and type "Outdoorlads (UK)" and click the Join button.
You may also wish to sign up to the
Strava OutdoorLads running club:
https://www.strava.com/clubs/439735
On occasions, parkruns can be cancelled due to the lack of volunteers or adverse weather. Should this happen, I will try to email members as soon as I am made aware.
Photo credits - Creative Commons License - Chris Morgan, Arek Adeoye, and Huckster - Unsplash; Colin Renshaw - Other images supplied by OutdoorLads Members
Difficulty & Distance: The course is run on tarmac paths, grass and woodland woodchip

Title: Harewood House Evening Walk
Summary:
A beautiful summers evening walk in the grounds of Harewood House, one of Yorkshire's premier stately homes. This walk around this immaculately managed estate starts with magnificent views of the house and gardens. Our leisurely amble will take us to New Bridge, into Piper Wood and past the set of Emmerdale. After taking in the fish pond and estate cottages our walk will take us into the deer park before we head back through the estate woodland.
Dogs
: well-behaved dogs are welcome on the walk, but must be under control at all times on a lead where necessary and you should abide by the
Countryside Code
. Please also remember not everyone loves dogs as much as you do.
Image 1 by Rick Harrison, available from
https://www.flickr.com/photos/sovietuk/289246735/in/photostream/
and used under creative commons licence.
Difficulty & Distance: 5 miles 2 hours

Title: Saltaire, Druids Altar and St Ives Estate
Summary:
This 10 mile circular walk takes us from Saltaire, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, to Druids Alter and the St Ives Estate. We will start our walk on the Leeds-Liverpool Canal towards Bingley. At the Three-Rise Locks we will make our way across Old Bingley to the River Aire before walking into open pasture and up to Druids Altar. This rocky outcrop has delighful views over the Aire Valley. Our walk then takes us into the St Ives Country Estate, walking across moorland and though woodland via Lady Blantyre's Rock. We finish our walk through Myrtle Park where we re-join the River Aire back into Saltaire, hopefully in time for a cup of tea at the David Hockney Gallery.

https://www.bingleywalkersarewelcome.org.uk/portfolio/bingley-church-with-river-aire/
Saltaire by Jalo. Licensed under Creative Commons.

;
Five Rise Locks with permission of Chris Rimell (ODL Member)

Title: Sheffield Bouldering - All Abilities (beginners welcome)
Summary:
Swap trainers for climbing shoes and join OutdoorLads for an evening of INDOOR BOULDERING at The Climbing Hanger in Sheffield.
Cards/contactless ONLY  - this venue does not accept cash
If you haven't been to The Hangar before, please click
here
to pre-register. Click on the link, scroll down, select Sheffield and follow the on-screen instructions. Completing the waiver prior to arrival will get you climbing faster.
What is Bouldering?
It's a form of climbing where instead of long high routes, the 'problems' tend to be shorter and focus on technique, balance, body positioning and sometimes power. Because of this different emphasis, bouldering doesn't have the hassle of climbing with ropes, harnesses and belaying, which makes it an excellent activity for newcomers to the sport. No wonder it's often regarded as the 'purest' form of climbing.
If you're a seasoned climber you won't need convincing about the benefits of bouldering, but for those who've never climbed more than a staircase, the benefits are wide ranging: from the physical - improved strength, tone, balance, power and coordination; mental - problem solving, focus, concentration and body awareness; to the emotional - relaxation, a sense of achievement and a feeling that mind and body are working together perfectly ('feeling psyched'). On top of all this, bouldering tends to be a very social sport where you can hang out with mates and each have a go as you feel regardless of what level you climb at.
I'm new to Bouldering - can I come along?
If you have never tried bouldering before it is recommended that you have a guided session from the event leader, who will go through everything you will need to know to boulder safely before joining in with the rest of the group.
I've been Bouldering before?
As above, everyone attending the event  must abide by the centre's T&Cs etc. As an experienced boulderer you responsible for your own safety, actions and involvement at all times. As such, you should use your own judgement in taking up advice given either by OutdoorLads Volunteers or other experienced climbers.
Photos : Copyright granted to OutdoorLads by the event leader / venue.
Please note that every person participating should be aware of (and accept) that those members perceived as ‘in charge’ or leading the event are not necessarily qualified, do not need to be qualified, and are not regarded by OutdoorLads, or themselves as ‘technical experts’ but are amateur climbers with some experience and who are happy to impart their knowledge. Any advice given should be considered with this in mind by the recipient.
All those attending the event must also be aware of the centre's own terms and conditions of participation and abide by them, along with any instructions given by the centre’s staff. Everyone must also take responsibility for their own safety while participating in Indoor climbing events.
Difficulty & Distance: Suitable for all abilities. If you're new to bouldering, just let the event leader know and we'll get you started!

Title: The Enchanting Bluebells of South Northants
Summary:
A beautifully rural circular walk over the gentle rolling hills of South Northamptonshire. Our route takes in the villages of Weston, Sulgrave and Moreton Pinkney and includes plenty of wide bridleways, far-reaching views and brief stretches of woodland where bluebells are abundant in the spring.
Starting from the village pub in Weston, we descend into the Tove Valley and make our way across the fields to Sulgrave. Approaching the village, we'll get a sideways view of Sulgrave Manor – a  mid-16th century Tudor house, built by Lawrence Washington, the 3rd great-grandfather of George Washington, first President of the United States.
We'll climb back out of the village and cross the fields to Moreton Pinkney – known for its ironstone houses and historical roots dating back to the Domesday Book. We'll stop for a picnic lunch in the grounds of the 12th century church or – if there's a service – on the nearby village green.
Next we join a long stretch on one of the area's many green lanes. These are historic country roads that missed out on the tarmac surfacing of the 1920s and ’30s, because so few vehicles ever used them. There are distant views of the Grade I listed Canons Ashby House, owned by the National Trust since 1981 and noted for its Elizabethan wall paintings, Jacobean plasterwork and fine gardens.
Walking along the side of Plumpton Wood, the springtime bluebell display is usually spectacular. Red Kites and Muntjac deer are a common sight.
A brief spell on another quiet country lane takes us past the charming Plumpton Manor and into the grounds of the tiny Plumpton Church – a rare late Regency church in a remote pastoral setting, with original stencilling and box pews. Inside, it feels like you've stepped onto the pages of a Jane Austen novel.
The final leg of our walk crosses fields which clearly show the medieval ridge and furrow farming system – a tell-tale sign that they have never seen a modern plough and have only been used for grazing cattle. And that brings us back to our starting point in Weston and an opportunity to enjoy some refreshments at The Crown.
Note:
If there are no places left on this event, join the waiting list, as people frequently drop out during the week before an event, and you are likely to get a place.

and bookmark our upcoming events list via the short link:

Pictures: taken by Outdoorlads members, free to reuse.
Difficulty & Distance: 9 miles, mostly flat with reasonable paths

Title: Ilkley Moors hike, West Yorks
Summary:
This is a 7.5 mile walk (approx). It will take in some good views the moors.  There are a couple of steeper ascents on the way which gives us these great views.
We will stop for lunch part way through to refuel.
There are good paths on some of the walk, but it can be wet and muddy on the moors (especially if has been wet recently). I would advise you wear good walking boots to try to keep your feet dry.
If you wish, after the walk we will try to find a pub to recuperate and refresh.
Photo Credits:
All photos taken by the event leader and permission is granted to OutdoorLads for further use.
Difficulty & Distance: There are some steep ascents / descents at the start and end of the walk. You will need a decent level of fitness.

Title: Buxton - the 'Ring of Trees' Walk, on Good Friday
Summary:
Get your Easter weekend off to a good start by joining us for a walk through woodlands around Buxton on Good Friday!
The Ring of Trees walk links nine woods around the perimeter of Buxton. Each has its own characteristics, with a variety of habitats, plants and wildlife.
This Ring of Trees walk is around 10 miles / 16 kilometres, with 200 metres of ascent
.  It includes a gentle ascent up to Soloman's Temple, above Grin Low Wood, from where there are great views over the town and the surrounding moorlands.  If the weather's good we'll go for the whole circuit; if it's not so good we may cut it a little shorter and take shelter in a bar / café in the town centre.
Most of the woods were planted in the mid 19th century to hide industrial activity in Buxton's quarries and smoking kilns from the eyes of visitors to the newly developing spa town.  The project manager was a man named Philip Heacock, who was the land agent of the Duke of Devonshire.  Surviving documentation in the archives show orders for thousands of trees from Derbyshire nurseries which were planted on hillsides around the town.  Many of these woodlands have now been gifted by the Devonshire Estate to the people of Buxton and are cared for by the Buxton Civic Association.
Today's walk starts and ends in the centre of Buxton, so you have a choice of driving to the event or taking the train from Manchester, which stops at Stockport and elsewhere on route.  Check train times on the day and remember that Good Friday is a public holiday so there may be a reduced service.
Dogs
: well-behaved dogs are welcome but will need to be on leads for parts of this walk as we may encounter horses and farm animals.  The walk starts and finishes in the town centre and some short stretches of this walk are along roads, so there will be traffic around.
Waitlist
:
if this event is fully booked, and you want to come, add yourself to the wait list because places usually become available during the days leading up to the event
.
Photo credits
: all photos by the walk leader, with permission granted to ODL for their use.
Difficulty & Distance: An fairly easy - but lengthy - walk with just over 200 metres ascent. It is likely to be muddy in places.

Title: Leisure Cycle to Newton Heath, Blackley and Prestwich
Summary:
Join Paul for this any bike any rider 17 mile cycle around Manchester, Prestwich and Salford.
This ride is perfect if you have done a bit of cycling before and now want to build up or maintain your fitness levels. We will aim to ride at a steady pace of  about 10 mph along a mixture of cycle paths and some fairly busy roads. as always, we'll stick to the pace of the slowest rider so no one will get left behind.
Starting from Manchester City Centre, we will ride out to Prestwich, passing through Newton Heath and Blackley. Along the way there will be several gentle hills to climb at your own pace, with a chance to regroup and recover at the top if needed.
The return leg through Salford is mostly downhill or flat. We'll do a quick circuit of Kersal Wetlands before following the River Irwell back into Manchester. Due to the mixed nature of the route's surfaces, if you have a road bike with narrow tyres there are some spots where caution will be needed.
We'll aim to cycle non-stop all the way round. There is plenty of choice of places to go for a well-earned drink or a bite to eat when we get back to the city centre.
If you're not a regular cyclist, please do make sure your bike is in good condition - check the tyres are well inflated, chain is oiled, and you've got a spare inner tube, drinks and snacks with you.
Difficulty & Distance: A steady leisure cycle

Title: Mini Tour de Greater Manchester
Summary:
Join Paul for a scenic cycling tour through 8 of Greater Manchester’s 10 boroughs, starting and finishing in Central Manchester, with a quick pit stop in Tameside and a lunch break near Bury.
Who’s It For?
Ideal for intermediate and experienced cyclists, but if you're new to cycling and are comfortable maintaining a pace of around 10mph for about 4 hours, you're also very welcome! The route offers several options for leaving early via bike or train.
Pace & Terrain
Speed
: Around 10 mph, always at the pace of the slowest rider—no one gets left behind!
Route
: Mostly flat with some climbs, mixing roads, trails, and canal paths.
Traffic
: Some busy roads—riders should feel comfortable cycling alongside traffic.
Bike & Rider Prep:
Best suited to hybrid bikes, the route is also doable on a road or mountain bike.
Ensure your bike is in good condition (inflated tyres, working brakes, oiled chain). The ride leader cannot provide repairs.
Bring a spare inner tube, snacks, and water.
Wear suitable comfortable clothing. Layers including something wind/water proof are recommended.
You must wear a
helmet
as this is one of OutdoorLads cycling participation requirements.
Weather Conditions
If extreme weather is forecast, we may postpone the ride. We’ll notify participants as early as possible.
All photos taken by OutdoorLads leaders and used with permission
Difficulty & Distance: Distance - 42 miles | Terrain - roads/trails | Pace - around 10 mph | Elevation 1300ft

Title: Outwood Bound
Summary:
Join Paul on this leisurely bike ride from Manchester to Bolton and back.
Everyone (including beginners) is welcome. No one will get left behind. We'll ride at a gentle pace of 8 to 10 miles per hour for about 3 hours, but the event timing is longer to allow for the natural stops and starts of the ride, plus a short lunch stop in Bolton.
There are frequent trains between Bolton and Manchester, so there's the option of leaving/joining the ride in Bolton if you fancy a shorter cycle of less than 2 hours. If joining us from Bolton, please book onto the event as usual, then message our ride leader Paul for details of where to meet and the approximate meeting time.
Route information
Starting from Central Manchester, we will ride along National Cycle Network (NCN) Route 55 to Bolton. This follows roads with cycle lanes as far as Eccles (or there's a slightly longer off-road option along canal paths and dedicated cycle paths). We'll then use the quiet roads in the leafy suburb of Ellesmere Park to get to Monton. There we join the former Monton Green to Bolton Railway line, known as the Roe Green loop line which keeps us mainly off road until we reach the centre of Bolton.
Our return leg continues mainly off road to NCN Route 6 and the Outwood Trail, another former rail ay line running between Radclliffe and Prestwich. After passing through Drinkwater Park to the outskirts of Salford, we will keep following NCN Route 6 (which has on and off road options) back to the centre of Manchester.
Although much of the route is traffic free or on quieter roads, we may encounter the odd busy road. There will also be some fairly gentle climbs along the way. See the
location
section below for a link to the proposed route map and elevation chart.
The terrain is best suited to hybrid or mountain bikes, though it can be done on a road bike. Some parts of the trail are uneven and can be muddy if wet. Please check that your bike is roadworthy before the ride, as you will be responsible for dealing with your own mechanical issues, should they arise on the day.
Weather Conditions
Cold, wet and windy weather conditions aren't conducive to sociable cycling, and part of the route has been known to flood after very heavy rain. So, if the weather forecast for the day indicates extreme conditions, the event may be postponed with as much notice as possible provided to participants.
All photos taken by OutdoorLads leaders and used with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: This is a 28 mile gentle leisure cycle. Some attendees may prefer to cycle half of the  route (14 miles) and catch a train for the other half.

Title: Altrincham to Stretford -GM19
Summary:
This is stage 19 of 20 on the Greater Manchester Ringway, a 200 mile (322km) walking trail exploring all 10 boroughs of Greater Manchester. You can find out more about it
here
.
This varied route uses mainly traffic-free pathways to explore the waterways, parks and nature reserves of Altrincham and Sale.
Highlights include:
Quiet paths and byways that give a flavour of Altrincham’s past as a Cheshire market town
A little-known nature reserve off the Bridgewater Canal, with ponds and woodland
Worthington Park and Priory Gardens, havens of greenery close to Sale town centre
The shores of Sale Water Park, with its sailing boats and views over Broad Ees Dole nature reserve, rich in birdlife
A surprisingly quiet section of the canal south of Stretford town centre, where the passing boats create a colourful scene.
Note that parts of the trail may be damp, muddy and slippery at any time of year and especially after periods of wet weather.
This is a linear walk
. See the options in the location details below.
Well-behaved, socialised and under-control dogs are welcome on this event.
Dogs must be kept on a lead whenever this is signposted and whenever we are in the vicinity of livestock, and at the event leader's request. It's a good idea to check what the route may be like with the event leader before booking to find out more about the potential suitability of the walk for dogs.
New Members
Please feel free to sign up as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions, don't hesitate to message me, or go to the
My First Event
page.
Photos by kvnmnc.
Difficulty & Distance: Around 15km (9.5 miles), flat

Title: Postcards from Boswinger : Long Summer Weekend in Cornwall
Summary:
Escape the ordinary and join us for a truly special
3 night long weekend
at one of Cornwall’s best-kept secrets. Our hostel is a hidden gem tucked away in the "Deep South" of the Roseland Peninsula—a rare find for groups and a location that feels like a private slice of paradise.
Best of all? We’re hitting the coast on the
perfect weekend.
By visiting two weeks before the school summer holidays kick in, we’ll dodge the peak crowds and have the winding lanes, coastal paths, and pristine beaches almost entirely to ourselves. This is your chance to experience Cornwall’s magic without the bustle!
Whether you’re a hardened hiker, a sunset-seeker, a paddle-boarder, or just someone who needs some serious "Vitamin Sea," this weekend is built for you.
Where are we headed?
We’re based just five minutes from the nearest secluded beach, surrounded by the hypnotic charm of the Roseland.
The Wild Side:
High-energy hikes to
Dodman Point
, the highest headland in South Cornwall.
The Picture-Postcard Side:
Wander through the "fairytale" village of
Veryan
with its iconic thatched roundhouses or explore the sleepy, timeless harbor of
Portloe
.
The Iconic Side:
We’re just minutes from the world-famous
Lost Gardens of Heligan
and the bustling fishing port of
Mevagissey
. If you fancy a change of pace, the
Eden Project
is only 15 miles away.
The Itinerary
Friday: The Social Kick-off
Arrive from 4:30 PM and settle into your coastal home. We’ll kick things off with our legendary welcome buffet—a great chance to meet the crew and plot our adventures.
Note: Please bring a small contribution to share (enough for 2-3 people). Let’s keep it varied—creative salads, local meats, or snacks are great, but maybe let’s go easy on the quiche!
Saturday: The Grand Coastal Loop
We’re heading out on a great walk to explore the rugged coastline, trekking through the charming spots of
Portmellon
and
Gorran Haven
. You can grab a fresh local pasty or a pub lunch in town before we conquer the heights of
Dodman Point
and wind our way back to the hostel for a well-earned dinner.
Sunday: The Western Wilds
Today we head further west on our walk to
Portloe
. Expect dramatic cliffs, crashing waves, and that quintessential Cornish "hidden village" vibe.
Monday:
Breakfast, clean up and head home after a glorious weekend!
Your Weekend, Your Way:
While I’ll be leading walks both days, the weekend is yours! Want to spend Sunday at the Eden Project? Go for it. Fancy bringing your beachwear and chilling on the beach? Go ahead!
Food & Fuel
We’ve got you covered so you can focus on the views:
Included:
Hearty breakfasts on Saturday, Sunday, and Monday, plus delicious group dinners on Saturday and Sunday night.
Tea, Coffee & Cake:
Unlimited supplies provided throughout the weekend—because every hiker needs a sugar hit!
Your Part:
Just bring your Friday buffet contribution, your own preferred snacks/drinks, and either a packed lunch or some cash for a pub lunch on Saturday and Sunday.
Don’t miss out!
Opportunities to book this hostel for groups are rare, and this pre-holiday window is the "sweet spot" of the Cornish summer. Grab your spot and let’s make some memories on the Roseland!
Dogs
: Well behaved dogs are welcome on this event. Maximum 4 dogs overall, at a charge of £50 per dog for the weekend - this is a standard charge by the venue. Dogs and owners will need to sleep in a certain room together. Obviously dogs will need to be cleaned up after and kept out of the kitchen and off furniture.  Please contact the office on
support@outdoorlads.com
to book a place for your dog. Registered assistance dogs are free, please e-mail
support@outdoorlads.com
at the time of booking.
Images
All photos taken from
Pixaby
under their
Pixaby licence
or provided by OutdoorLads Members and used with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Activities to suit all abilities are available.

Title: Lake District Activity Week 2026 - Camping
Summary:
Tuesday 30 June to Sunday 5 July 2026
Join us for
five nights
in the Lake District, based at a campsite in a spectacular setting with quick access to classic fell days and big scenery in every direction. Expect a proper outdoors focused week, good company, and plenty of time on the hills.
Leaders will write their events on a whiteboard during the week, usually the evening before so you'll be able to see meeting times, location and type of event that way (example in the pictures). Leaders will decide from day to day and this laid-back approach isn't always a match for members who like to have their week super planned out.
This isn't an event for the partygoers but in the evenings people will go to the local pubs or sit around in the centre of the field socialising.
Other booking options
Campervan pitches
Arrange your own accommodation and join daytime activities
The location and what is nearby
We are right in the heart of some of the best known Lake District scenery, with big mountain days close by and plenty of brilliant lower level options too. Nearby highlights include the Langdale Pikes, Bowfell, Lingmoor Fell, Loughrigg Fell, and the views around Blea Tarn.
About the week
OutdoorLads events are led by volunteers, so the range of activities will depend on the leaders attending and what they are able to lead. The week will be mostly based around hill walking and mountain days. If we have leaders with the right experience, there may also be opportunities for other activities during the week, but these cannot be guaranteed.
Parking and lifts
Car parking is
very limited
at the site, so please arrange to car share where practical.
When you arrive, please park in the group field car park or around the edge of the campsite as directed. Please do not park on the sides of the access road.
You can use the comments section to offer and ask for lifts, or use the chat group which will be set up closer to the event date.
Dogs
Well behaved dogs are welcome. Dogs must remain on a lead on site and you will need to clean up after them. Dogs listed under the Dangerous Dogs Act 1991 are not permitted at National Trust campsites.
Photos:
Taken by event leaders and used by OutdoorLads with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Highly mountainous environment so very limited easy walks

Summary:
Tuesday 30 June to Sunday 5 July 2026
Join us in the Lake District for a few days of proper outdoors time in a spectacular setting, with classic fell walks close by and big scenery in every direction. This is the same activity week as the camping and campervan bookings, but this page is for people staying elsewhere and joining the daytime activities.
Leaders will write their events on a whiteboard during the week, usually the evening before so you'll be able to see meeting times, location and type of event that way (example in the pictures). Leaders will decide from day to day and this laid-back approach isn't always a match for members who like to have their week super planned out.
This isn't an event for the partygoers but in the evenings people will go to the local pubs or sit around in the centre of the field socialising.

If you are booked on the tent camping page or the campervan page, you do not need to book this page as well.

We are right in the heart of some of the best known Lake District scenery, with big mountain days close by and plenty of brilliant lower level options too. Nearby highlights include the Langdale Pikes, Bowfell, Lingmoor Fell, Loughrigg Fell, and the views around Blea Tarn.
About the week

Title: Fire Bird Double Weekender - Day 1 of 2 Walk
Summary:
This is the first of a two part event weekend.
This is a circular walk in the beautiful countryside of West Sussex. Meeting in the delightful village of Rudwick we head off using the old Cranleigh Line now the Downs Link. We then head west accessing quiet roads and ancient footpaths.
Fancy making a weekend of it?
We are camping at Riverside Farm for the weekend and you are more than welcome to join us.
http://www.riversidefarm-camping.uk/
Day 2 is now live!
https://www.outdoorlads.com/events/fire-bird-double-weekend-day-2-2-wal…
Surrey Canal
Arriving at Sidney Wood picking up the Wey and Arun Canal walking path which follows the gentle contour of the land. The Canal is partially open, at just 23-mile-long, to small craft and narrow boats.
Passing through a rural landscape, there was little freight traffic to justify its continued existence – the canal was officially abandoned in 1871. Without maintenance, the canal gradually became derelict over much of its length.
However, since 1970, restoration by the Wey & Arun Canal Trust has led to several miles being restored to the standard navigable by narrowboats and small tour barges. Work is continuing, with the ultimate aim of reopening the entire canal to navigation.
Star in a reasonably priced car!
Leaving the Canal we head north arriving the Dunsfold Aerodrome famous for BBC Top Gear test track and many other film and tv productions. Walking past on the perimeter we then head south and eventually picking up the Downs Link and make our way back to our finish point the Firebird Brewery, here the walk will end.
https://firebirdbrewing.com/
Rudwick is a few minutes walk down the Downs Link for those that do not want to visit the brewery.
See the route here
Dogs:
I love having dogs on my walks. I just ask that owners please ensure they are kept on a lead as appropriate, especially around livestock and when near busy roads. Please, also remember that not everyone is a dog lover.
This particular walk does have a lot of stiles so please ensure your dog is comfortable with these.
Don’t Waste That Space!
Walks get filled up quite quickly, so please only sign up if you know you are free to attend. Plans do sometimes change, but remember to free up your space so someone else can attend.
New Members:
If this is your first event or you are new to OutdoorLads we’d love to see you. Please message me if you have any questions.
Timings
Whilst every endeavour is made to make the timings of this event accurate, I cannot be guaranteed that we will finish on time and there may be delays.
If you have read this far, thanks and comment your favourite colour below to confuse all the skim readers. :)
Images are leaders own.
Difficulty & Distance: 13 miles with a few ups and downs

Title: Moses Gate, Clifton, and Nob End Day Walk
Summary:
Join Andy and the OutdoorLads for a relaxing Friday stroll through the beautiful landscapes of Moses Gate and Clifton Country Parks, with a visit to the wonderfully named Nob End along the way. It’s a perfect chance to unwind, enjoy the fresh spring air, and admire the wildlife and greenery as the season comes to life.
We’ll begin at Moses Gate Country Park, wandering around its peaceful lakes before joining the canal towpaths. From there, we’ll make our way through Nob End, taking time to appreciate the local statues, bridges, and unique character of the area. Our route then leads us to Clifton, where we’ll stop for lunch. Clifton is rich in wildlife and was designated a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI) back in 1988—so keep your eyes open for nature at its best.
After exploring Clifton Country Park, its lake, and the River Irwell Sculpture Trail, we’ll make our gentle return journey. The full walk is around 8 miles and is suitable for most abilities.
Bolton’s largest nature reserve
At 750 acres, Moses Gate is Bolton’s biggest nature reserve—a place where woodland, mill lodges, and industrial heritage blend into a peaceful haven full of life. There’s always something to see here.
What might you spot at Moses Gate Country Park?
In the 19th century, Moses Gate was home to the Farnworth Paper Mills, run by Thomas Bonsor Crompton—one of the country’s leading paper producers. Back then, the river was far too polluted for paper making, so ‘Crompton’s Lodges’ were created. Today, these lodges are thriving habitats for waterfowl such as mute swans, tufted ducks, great-crested grebes, and even the elusive water rail. In summer, common terns can be seen performing acrobatic flights over the water.
If you’re lucky, you might catch sight of herons snatching brown trout as they leap from the weir. And don’t forget to look out for the dazzling kingfisher—a symbol of the valley’s transformation from industrial wasteland to wildlife sanctuary.
A landscape shaped by history
The industrial past is still visible in features like the impressive ‘blue wall’ supporting the canal and the relatively young woodland, only 40–50 years old. It’s a reminder that not long ago, this area was a patchwork of coal mines, sewage works, bleach works, paper mills, and even landfill.
But the story goes back much further. Look closely and you’ll find red sandstone outcrops—fascinating remnants of the last ice age.
Well-behaved, socialised and under-control dogs are welcome on this event.
Dogs must be kept on a lead whenever this is signposted and whenever we are in the vicinity of livestock, and at the event leader's request. It's a good idea to check what the route may be like with the event leader before booking to find out more about the potential suitability of the walk for dogs.
All photos taken by Growler
Your First OutdoorLads Walk Event?
We know that new members in particular like to know in advance more about what an OutdoorLads event will be like.
If this is your first OutdoorLads event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the OutdoorLads website.
Waitlist
: If this event is fully booked and you want to attend, just add yourself to the waitlist, because places inevitably become available closer to the date.
Difficulty & Distance: A nice relaxing Friday walk. Mainly flat. Nothing exerting.

Title: A Springtime Stroll, Bluebells, Hills and Historic Thrills
Summary:
You are invited to join a Springtime Lowland and Hills walk on the second Bank Holiday Monday in May.
Spring is a season of renewal and transformation. As winter's chill fades away, nature awakens with vibrant colours and fresh scents. while birds return, singing melodies that celebrate the season.
Starting our journey in Hungerford, West Berkshire, we will depart along banks of the River Dun a tributary of the River Kennett. We will pass through Standen Manor, a historic estate that dates back to the 16th century. Continuing along the Mid Wilts Way, we will enter the village of Ham. This quaint village is home to several historical landmarks, including the Church of St. George, which boasts a Norman tower.
As we make our way towards Inkpen Hill, we will (weather permitting) be treated to breathtaking views of the surrounding countryside. Inkpen Hill is part of the North Wessex Downs, an Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty. The hill's geological significance lies in its chalk composition, which has shaped the landscape over millions of years. This summit offers panoramic views.
The
Route
Don't waste that space!
Spring walks get busy so please only book if you know you can make it. If your plans change please free up the space for someone else.
Is it dog friendly?
Dogs are welcome on this walk, there will be livestock on route. Please abide by the Countryside Code (
https://tinyurl.com/rlqvp3h
). Please also remember not everyone loves dogs as much as you do so please be mindful of other members.
New Members
Please feel free to join this as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions then feel free to message me, or go to the
My First Event
section under "About Us".
Image / photo credits:
All images taken by the event leader with permission for ODL use.
Difficulty & Distance: Distance is 13.5 miles; total ascent is 314m; terrain is dirt, grass, chalk and tarmac. There will be stiles to cross, and one steep climb.

Title: Queens of the Castle: A weekend at Kings
Summary:
Join us on this hostel weekend near Dolgellau, with access to the mountains right from the front door. The main plan for the weekend will be to walk up
Cadair Idris
. At 893m she falls just short of being a "Welsh 3000", but she's a "proper mountain" nevertheless, with lakes, cliffs and views aplenty. This is a tough(ish) walk, but at this time of year the weather ought to be kind, with plenty of daylight to enable us to take it slowly. It's a non-technical walk, and certainly within the capability of most regular hill walkers. Of course, if other leaders attend and wish to offer alternative walks, they'd be welcome, but hopefully you'll all choose to join us on this Eryri classic!
Our accommodation for the weekend will be the well-loved Kings hostel, which is set in its own wooded ravine. Alongside the dormitories, there is a lounge, dining room and showers, as you'd expect.
Simon will be co-leading this hostel as part of our Hostel Leader assessment process. If you'd like to consider becoming a leader yourself, speak to your
Regional Coordinator
, or
apply here
.
Rough plan for the weekend (subject to change):
Friday
:
Arrive from 4pm. Get settled in and meet your fellow hostelers, either reconnecting with friends or getting to know new ones.
We'll have the usual OutdoorLads buffet, so please bring along some food to add to the buffet (enough for 1-2 people and please don't just bring pizza/quiche/sausage rolls!). Please bring your own refreshments.
During the evening we'll run through the arrangements for the weekend, and talk through options for Saturday's activities.
Saturday
:
A hearty breakfast to set the day off.
There will be a walk up Cadair Idris, plus potentially other walk options dependant on leaders.
We'll be providing an evening meal, so please ensure any dietary information is up to date on your profile.
Sunday
:
Another provided breakfast. We need to be out of the hostel by 10am, but there may be the option of a local walk into Barmouth or to the Cregennan Lakes.
Parking:
There is free parking at the hostel. However, this is limited, so please car share where possible.
Dogs:
Unfortunately this event is not suitable for dogs, except registered assistance dogs. Please contact
support@outdoorlads.com
when booking to notify us if an assistance dog will be joining.

Difficulty & Distance: Mountain walking

Title: Dorset Double - Saturday MTB Ride
Summary:
Off Road MTB ride on the Isle of Purbeck
Corfe Castle – Kimmeridge Bay – Square & Compass Loop
This ride packs a huge amount of variety into a short distance, with constant changes in terrain driven by Purbeck’s famously complex geology.
Start: Corfe Castle
You roll out beneath the ruins of
Corfe Castle
, riding through the
Corfe Gap
, a natural break in the Purbeck Hills. The track here is generally firm and grassy, threading between pasture fields and hedgerows. Chalk underfoot gives decent grip in the dry, but can be slick after rain. The castle looms above you as you begin the gradual climb south, with the land closing in on either side.
Climb onto the Purbeck Hills
As you leave the village, the route rises onto the
chalk downland of the Purbeck Ridge
. This is classic open riding: wide bridleways, short turf, and big skies. Sheep-grazed slopes keep the trails fast and flowing, with dry valleys cutting across the hills. There’s little shelter up here, and on a windy day you really feel how exposed this ridge is. Views open out toward the coast as you crest the hills.
Descent toward Kimmeridge
Dropping south, the terrain changes noticeably. The chalk gives way to darker soils as you descend toward
Kimmeridge Bay
. Tracks become rougher and more rutted, especially after wet weather. The final approach runs above low, unstable cliffs, where erosion is obvious and the land feels raw and unfinished.
Kimmeridge Bay
At sea level, the ride briefly flattens out along the edge of
Kimmeridge’s wave-cut rock platform
. The surface here is hard and uneven, with slabs of oil shale and clay exposed at low tide. It’s a stark, dramatic place—low cliffs, dark rock, crashing waves—and a complete contrast to the soft chalk hills you’ve just crossed.
Climb Inland toward Worth Matravers
Leaving the coast, the route climbs sharply back inland. This is one of the tougher sections: steeper gradients, looser stone, and thin soils over
Purbeck limestone
. As you top out, the landscape opens into a
limestone plateau
, with large fields divided by dry-stone walls instead of hedges. Old quarry workings sit quietly beside the track, reminders of how long this land has been worked.
The Square and Compass
Rolling lanes and stony bridleways lead you into
Worth Matravers
, where the Square and Compass sits solidly in the limestone landscape. Stone walls, tight lanes, and wind-shaped fields give this section a rugged, timeless feel—and it’s an ideal pause point before the final leg returning
to Corfe Castle and the campsite.
15 - 20 miles at a leisurely pace enjoying the vast scenic views.
The
route
Ideal for most mountain bike riders. You will need good physical fitness and be able to ride up to 20 miles.
E-bikes are welcome, but expect to pause occasionally while non-assisted riders tackle the tougher climbs under their own steam.
BE WARNED! - There will be some hills.
Ride highlights:
Corfe Common
Corfe Castle
Kimmeride Bay
Creech Grange Arch
Swyre Head
The Square & Compass
"Dorset Double" - events over the bank holiday weekend
Saturday:
MTB Bike Ride
Dorset Double Walk, Day 1: Kimmeridge Ledges and Chapman's Pool
Sunday:
Dorset Double Walk, Day 2: St Aldhelm's Head and Dancing Ledge
Acommodation
If doing both days or want to make a long weekend of the trip to the Isle of Purbeck, you might like to stay at a campsite, hostel or B&B nearby, there are lots of options locally. You’ll need to make your own arrangements, but you are welcome to stay at the same place as the event leaders who will be staying at Woodyhyde Campsite.
Difficulty & Distance: 15 - 20 miles

Title: Elenydd Escape: Remote Wales Weekend
Summary:
Tucked deep within the wild heart of the Elenydd, Ty’n Cornel is one of the most remote hostels in Wales and the perfect setting for a true escape from the everyday.
With no phone reception (or WiFi) and miles of open landscape in every direction, this is a rare opportunity to properly switch off, slow down, and immerse yourself in the quiet beauty of Mid-Wales. Once you arrive, the modern world quickly fades... replaced by rolling uplands, empty roads, the occasional phone box, vast reservoirs and uninterrupted horizons that stretch for miles.
Over the weekend, we’ll head out on a scenic group walk through the landscapes that make the Elenydd so special. Expect fresh spring air, big skies, winding tracks, and sweeping views across one of the UK's most sparsely populated regions. This is a place where you can walk for hours through open moorland and reservoir edges, encountering little more than red kites overhead and the sound of the wind moving through the grass.
And when darkness falls, the remoteness reveals one of its greatest rewards. Step outside and, if the skies are clear, you’ll find yourself beneath some of the darkest skies in Britain. With minimal light pollution, the stars here can be breathtaking... a vast canopy of light stretching from horizon to horizon and the perfect way to round off a peaceful day in the wild.
If you’re craving fresh air, friendly company and a genuine off-grid reset, this remote Welsh weekend delivers it in spades, a chance to disconnect from the noise and reconnect with landscape, people and simple, quiet adventure.
Dogs
Sorry, Dogs are not allowed in this hostel.
Want to Get Out More?
Why not check out the South Wales and South West Calendar
here
.

All leaders' own images - with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse.
Difficulty & Distance: A remote hostel with no phone reception

Title: Spring Penpont 2026 - A wonderful private campsite
Summary:
Join one of OutdoorLads' most popular events  - camping in the walled gardens of a stately home, beside the meandering River Usk set at the foot of the Bannau Brycheiniog (Brecon Beacons.)
Many of you will know and love Penpont but for those who don't it truly is a magical place.
We will be running
mountain/ leisure walking,
canoeing
and potentially mountain biking, and climbing days (subject to interest and leader availability)
.
We will have exclusive use of the amazing Penpont campsite set in the gardens of the Penpont estate for
THREE nights.
.
If you're feeling adventurous, you will have the opportunity to explore the Brecon Beacons with some of the finest views that South Wales has to offer. If you'd like a more sedate weekend, we have plenty of less strenuous activities planned and we'll be within easy travelling distance of the towns of Brecon and Abergavenny. Alternatively you can spend the weekend relaxing within the Penpont Estate: exploring the remarkable buildings, gardens, walled kitchen garden the woodland art, maze, tennis courts, croquet lawns, dower house and the 2000 acre estate that surrounds you!
After dusk we will gather together around a campfire, relax and be merry.
**Please note this event is very popular with free spirited, seasoned OutdoorLads, and as such the fireside conversation can sometimes be explicit, coupled with late night music and dancing. If this type of party atmosphere may not be for you please consider one of the many other great camping events as an alternative.**
Campervans
: Small campervans are welcome at the site, but limited in number (5) and so subject to availability. They will be parked separately to the tents in the orchard. They
must
be pre-booked by messaging the office on
support@outdoorlads.com
Unfortunately this site does not accept motorhomes or caravans.
Dogs
: Penpont also welcomes well behaved quiet dogs, but they must be kept under control and with you during your stay on site. There is an additional charge of £6 per dog for the weekend, to be paid in cash on arrival.
Sauna
: There may be the opportunity to use (for a fee) the wonderful hand built wood fired sauna that overlooks the river.
Images
: Image 1 and maps used with permission of Penpont Estate. All other images taken by OutdoorLads members and permission is given for their use.
Want to Get Out More?
Check out our
South Wales & South West Calendar View
Difficulty & Distance: As much or a little as you please!

Title: Penpont Canoeing 2026 - Glasbury to Whitney
Summary:
This is a great canoe trip down the river Wye.
The canoeing is for a full day and will
cost £45
paid to the canoe centre on the day.
Please note by signing up to this event you are committing to paying the fee (as we need to commit to the canoe centre)
. If you do not turn up you may be asked to pay for your canoe anyway!
If this event is full but you are camping at Penpont and you want to come then please sign up on the reserve list.
This event is aimed at those attending the Penpont camp event. If you are not attending the camp but would like to come please sign up and confirm your attendance by messaging the event leader a week prior to the event date. (A place may not be held for you if you have not obtained confirmation from the event leader)
As well as signing up here you will need to complete the centres online health form/disclaimer (only takes a couple of mins):-
https://waiver.smartwaiver.com/w/mjvg4tugrr7udphg4v8zxr/web/?auto_tag=fh_id_220245491
This event will take place whatever the weather (as long as the river is open for watercraft) !
This will involve a drive to the canoe centre, approximately five hours of paddling down the river (this is relatively easy as we are going with the flow of the water), a visit to a nice riverside pub at the end before returning to the campsite.
The canoes are open Indian style ones. A water tight barrel is provided to keep things dry in the event of capsizing. Whilst the barrel is reasonably watertight, this cannot be guaranteed so please do not bring any valuables that are incompatible with water!
Buoyancy aides are also provided.
There may be an option on the day to use a kayak if you have some previous experience and would prefer.
Please bring snacks / packed lunch / drink for the journey and for our lunch stop by the riverside.
There is a garage shop next to the canoe centre where we start from where you can purchase some supplies prior to the trip.
Don’t forget money/card to pay for the canoe trip and  for a pint/food in the pub at the end.
If you have any queries / questions either message the event leader.

information including an FAQ
