Title: Snap, Crackle and Pop
Summary:
The day starts with a bang as once we set off we soon reach Chilworth Gunpowder Mills. From there we move on to an area known as Silent Pool. After taking in this tranquil site we climb up the downs before reaching Newlands Corner. A time to take a break and admire the views before moving on to St Martha's Church, a truly lovely site. Our final trek will be through Chantry Wood. This dense woodland really takes us back to nature and we forget we are near the bustling town of Guildford.
Click on this
Link
to view the route (opens OS Maps)
A Few Features:
Chilworth Gunpowder Mills
One of the earliest examples of gunpowder mills dating back over 300 years. Although many buildings have been destroyed, the remains of over 100 are still visible. Walking around the site really offers a window in to history. Now a protected site many wildflowers have been encouraged, which should coincide with our visit.
Silent Pool
Rumours of plague burials and ghosts but is any of it true? An eerie mist often covers the surface. We'll take a look at the area and enjoy some history in the process.
Newlands Corner
A vast nature reserve just outside Guildford covering some 250 acres. It is owned by the Albury Estate but access is maintained for all. The site contains over 120 yew trees with some said to be over 1000 years old. The area is famous as a place to go and try and spot UFOs and study astronomy.
Chantry Wood
Some 200 acres of woodland and various meadows. The area boasts spectacular bluebell displays, which like Chilworth, should coincide with our visit.
Dogs:
I love having dogs on my walks. I just ask that owners please ensure they are kept on a lead as appropriate, especially around livestock and when near busy roads. Please, also remember that not everyone is a dog lover.
Don’t Waste That Space!
Walks get filled up quite quickly, so please only sign up if you know you are free to attend. Plans do sometimes change, but remember to free up your space so someone else can attend.
New Members:
If this is your first event or you are new to OutdoorLads we’d love to see you. Please message me if you have any questions.
Looking For More:
Check out this
Link
for details of other walks taking place in the South East.
Check out this
Link
for details of provisional upcoming events in the ODL diary.
(All photos taken by the leader and permission is given for OutdoorLads to reuse them.
Difficulty & Distance: Distance is 17 km (10.5 miles); total ascent is 318 metres; terrain is tarmac, dirt, grass, chalk and woodland paths. Two steep ascents.

Title: Brickfields parkrun, Dublin
Summary:
For those of you who enjoy your weekly parkrun fix, I'm heading to Dublin for the weekend with some fellow OutdoorLads folks and heading to Brickfields parkrun in Dublin. If you've not heard of it, parkrun is a free, weekly, timed 5k event that takes place every Saturday at locations across the world. Those of you who are regular parkrunners may be after a letter from the alphabet, or a country flag.
We will meet at 9.15am by the parkrun sign. I'll be wearing a red parkrun t-shirt, and in Ireland parkrun starts at 9.30am. You can register for parkrun and get your own barcode from
www.parkrun.org.uk
to scan at the end of your run to record your finish time if you wish. However, you do not need to do this to take part in the run if you don't want to.
Course description:
The course is 5k long, run entirely on permanent paths and consists of four laps in a clockwise direction finishing in the middle of the park.
Post run:
After the run we will be grabbing some breakfast locally and anyone is welcome to join us. I will be staying in Dublin for the weekend with some fellow OutdoorLads members, so if you'd like to join us as we explore Dublin, please get in touch directly.
On occasion parkruns can be cancelled due to the weather or lack of volunteers. Should this happen I will try to contact members as soon as I can.
Please note:
You will need to arrange your own flights, accommodation and transport to get to the parkrun.
Photo credits: OutdoorLads members wilk82 and RichardL - used with permission. Creative Commons License - Chris Morgan, Arek Adeoye and Huckster - Unsplash; Colin Renshaw.
Difficulty & Distance: Four laps on permanent paths within the park

Title: Helensburgh and Kilcreggan road loop
Summary:
A joint ride with
Out Velo
.
Come join us for a super-scenic spring road ride - warm up the bike legs on some quiet roads just west of Glasgow.
We will start at Helensburgh central train station and cycle north along a fairly quiet A road, before cutting west along a nice quiet road towards Faslane and the Kilcreggan peninsula.  We will then do a loop of the peninsula and return to Helensburgh along the coast - total distance 62km.  There are some cafes along the way so we should be able to stop somewhere for refreshments.
I will be taking the train from Edinburgh - this passes through Glasgow Queen Street and Hyndland and arrives in Helensburgh at 10:46.  If you are arriving by other means then we can meet you in Helensburgh.
This is not planned to be a super fast ride, but it is still a road ride so we would expect to average something over 18km/hr.
The route is available here - plotted on komoot.
https://www.komoot.com/tour/2786663700?share_token=aUD3QNgiFVqrVRfwaYMi…
Difficulty & Distance: This is a medium-length road ride and is not expected to be too fast.  There are also some bail-out points if that is needed.

Title: Maritime, Maytime Magic with West Wight Town and Down
Summary:
A new walk on the enchanted island where the excitement engendered by a sea crossing will be matched by the enchantment evident in the miracle of the month of May. After we've said goodbye to the mainland (and good riddance to the greyness of the preceding months) we'll alight at the delightful small seaside town of Yarmouth where sleek yachts counterpoint Georgian elegance. We'll take in much of the town before setting off to a local highpoint, Mottistone Down, where the whole of South and West Wight can be appreciated. We'll then descend to a historic water mill for tea and cake, and then to pretty villages and wooded cliffs where we can finish our survey of the town we began at.
The sights:
Yarmouth:
A small seaside market town, Saxon in origin but with a Norman grid road layout. Yarmouth Castle, built in 1547 by Henry VIII, is a gun platform to defend The Solent. The elegant Town Hall was rebuilt in 1763. In St James' Church there is a monument to the C17 admiral Sir Robert Holmes. He obtained it in a raid on a French ship, when he seized an unfinished statue of Louis XIV of France and forced the sculptor to finish it with his own head rather than the king's. Yarmouth Pier (Grade II-listed), opened 1876, is the longest timber pier in England (186m). You might like to explore the town more thoroughly at the end of the walk; it really is a lovely little place.
Mottistone Down and the Long Stone:
The Long Stone is the island's only megalithic monument. 'Mottistone' derives from the Longstone, used as a meeting place (or 'moot-stone') in Saxon times. The Long Stone consists of two pieces of local greensand sandstone, the larger stands at 3.9 metres and the smaller lies at its foot. They are likely Neolithic, 4100 to 2500 BCE. Mottistone Down, 203 m up, offers a fine view of the southern side of the island. It is here that we'll have our lunch. Surrounding the open downland is Brighstone Forest, the largest expanse of forest on the island, and part of the Isle of Wight AONB.
Calbourne Water Mill:
The website says: 'Set in 20 acres of stunning rural landscape, Calbourne Water Mill contains one of the oldest working water mills in the country, dating back to the Domesday Book. The Water Mill...has been milling for over 1,000 years. Stunning views and peaceful landscapes, enjoy a homemade cream tea, feed the birds, watch the Water Mill work and take a trip through history...' We'll take tea and cake there at about 15.00.
Shalfleet:
A pretty place of local stone, growing at the head of a creek and now a main road village. St Michael the Archangel Church is attractive and complex, being a mixture of Norman (the huge, solid, keep-like west tower and tympanum above the door of a bearded man), High Gothic (the main nave and chancel, with delicate and elegant geometrical tracery in the aisles) and the Post-Reformation (additions of 1630 and Georgian box pews). Shalfleet Manor, across the road, is dated 1680 and is very conservative in style for its time.
Bouldnor Forest and Cliff:
Part of the Hamstead Heritage Coast. The Hampshire and Isle of Wight Wildlife Trust website says: 'Bouldnor Forest is a fascinating nature reserve with a prehistoric past. Its variety of habitats including pine forest, heathland, cliffs and seagrass beds are home to plentiful wildlife.' We might even spot a red squirrel.
Dogs:
I welcome dogs on my walks and this walk is perhaps suitable for them, although the length and exposure in potentially warm sun may be hazardous. A dog off its lead must be obedient.
The
route
(click the link in red to see it):
A stroll south along Yarmouth's Market Street and St James' Street will lead out of the town to where we'll pick up the bridleway that was the railway line to Freshwater along the River Yar. We'll soon head off east to Barnfields and ascend the hill before reaching Broad Lane which will take us, once we've crossed the B3399 to Wellow Dow and Brook Down where the Tennyson Trail will takes us east to Mottistone Down where we'll have lunch. An optional 20-minute round trip will take those who want to see it to the Long Stone. We'll then turn north and follow a footpath to the B3401 which we'll cross to get to Calbourne Water Mill for tea and cake. Heading further north, Clay Lane will take us to Newbridge. Crossing the B3401 again. we'll head north to Shalfleet. A pavement alongside the A3054 will allow us to connect with the Coastal Path whcih we'll follow to Cranmore and Bouldnor, where it will will return us to Yarmouth via the sea wall, promenade and High Street.

Title: Blossoms, Barrows and Brews (and Maybe Some Late Bluebells)
Summary:
One of the best things about living in Portsmouth isn't the sea, the sailors or the ships, it's being able to escape from it by train into the South Downs National Park in just twenty minutes. And not just any old part of the national park, you understand: only some of the loveliest, loneliest hills and hamlets it has. I am sorry that guys from London will have quite a journey to reach the area, but I hope an advance listing will help them get an advance train ticket once the schedules are published. Delighting our senses will be deep vistas and woodlands, stretching our legs will be the sweeping downland, easing our way will be gravel and tarmac trails and reviving our spirits will be a pub stop or two.
The sights:
Stoughton:
'Brick and flint farms in fine large-scale downland valley...Bow Hill to the SE has some of the best scenery in the whole of the Downs' (Nairn:
The Buildings of England: Sussex
). St Mary's Church 'plain outside but impressively rich inside' (Nairn). A near-complete 11th century Saxon-Norman church with some 12th century mouldings and 13th century windows.
Kingley Vale National Nature Reserve:
Famed for its ancient yew tree forests (which we won't get time to see), other tree species in Kingley Vale include oak, ash and holly. The clearance of dead ash trees has created new clearings in the woods for other plants to grow. The chalk grassland is home to many flowers and herbs that form a diverse mosaic of species. Over 50 species of birds are found and 39 species of butterfly. Mammals include deer, yellow-necked mouse, water shrew and dormouse. It has a rich and diverse heritage with remains of a Romano-Celtic temple at Bow Hill, an Iron Age settlement site known as Goosehill Camp, the Devil's Humps Bronze Age round barrows, and prehistoric flint mines.
Walderton:
Another attractive village of brick and flint farms and cottages. Watergate House has an Ionic portico and some pretty lodges and is a rebuild from the early 1980s of a house originally built by John Soane then rebuilt in 1882 then demolished.
Stansted House:
A remarkably remote and unspoilt setting considering the nearness of Portsmouth. Splendid grounds, with a superb avenue of trees and a sustainable management programme. The house itself is the third version, built in 1900 by Arthur Blomfield in an effective neo-Wren style after the James Wyatt-version burned down. Lodges of 1820. Very fine stables by James Wyatt of 1786.
The
route
:
The Staunton Way will lead out, east, then northeast from Rowlands Castle through Stansted Forest. A short walk south along Forest Road and then a footpath through The Warren will take us to the vicinty of Stansted House. A lane heading past farms northeast again will take us to Watergate Hanger and down that hillside. Then, crossing the B2146, a lane will take us past Watergate House to Piglegged Row and over the hill and down the valley to Stoughton. The Monarch's Way will take us to Kingley Vale, then a bridleway heading west past the Devil's Jumps will take us to Walderton for a pub stop. A crossing of the B2146 will access Woodlands Lane which will take us back to Stansted House via the Monarch's Way and a walk along the Avenue will bring us back to Rowlands Castle.
Dogs:
Dogs are very welcome on all my walks and this is a very good dog walk, with plenty of woods and fields for them to run free, although there will be country lanes and livestock in some fields necessitating the dog going on a lead. A dog off the lead must be obedient.

Title: LONDON BOULDERING Thursday Evening - Beginners Welcome! 😃
Summary:
Come and join OutdoorLads for some bouldering in central London!
This event is for members of all levels of experience and all are welcome to come along to any of the sessions.
Bouldering is a form of climbing where instead of long high routes, the 'problems', as they are referred to, tend to be shorter and focus on technique, balance, body positioning and sometimes power. Because of this different emphasis, bouldering doesn't have the hassle of climbing with ropes, harnesses and belaying, which makes the activities excellent for newcomers to the sport.
If you're a seasoned climber you won't need convincing about the benefits of bouldering, but for those who've never climbed more than a staircase, the benefits are wide ranging: from the physical - improved strength, tone, balance, power and coordination; mental - problem solving, focus, concentration and body awareness; to the emotional - relaxation, a sense of achievement and a feeling that mind and body are working together perfectly ('feeling psyched'). On top of all this, bouldering tends to be a very social sport where you can hang out with mates and each have a go as you feel regardless of what level you climb at.
Do I need a guided session?
If you have never tried bouldering before it is recommended that you have a guided session from the event leader, who will go through everything you will need to know to boulder safely before joining in with the rest of the group.
All those attending the event must also be aware of the centre's own terms and conditions of participation and abide by them, along with any instructions given by the centre’s staff. Everyone must also take responsibility for their own safety while participating in Indoor climbing events.
Independent boulderer?
As above all attending the event  must abide by the centre's T&Cs etc. As an experienced boulderer you are at all times responsible  for your own safety actions and involvement. As such, you should use your own judgement in taking up advice given either by OutdoorLads Volunteers or other experienced climbers.
Can't make this event?

Title: LONDON BOULDERING Thursday Evening - Beginners Welcome! 😃
Summary:
Come and join OutdoorLads for some bouldering in central London!
This event is for members of all levels of experience and all are welcome to come along to any of the sessions.
Bouldering is a form of climbing where instead of long high routes, the 'problems', as they are referred to, tend to be shorter and focus on technique, balance, body positioning and sometimes power. Because of this different emphasis, bouldering doesn't have the hassle of climbing with ropes, harnesses and belaying, which makes the activities excellent for newcomers to the sport.
If you're a seasoned climber you won't need convincing about the benefits of bouldering, but for those who've never climbed more than a staircase, the benefits are wide ranging: from the physical - improved strength, tone, balance, power and coordination; mental - problem solving, focus, concentration and body awareness; to the emotional - relaxation, a sense of achievement and a feeling that mind and body are working together perfectly ('feeling psyched'). On top of all this, bouldering tends to be a very social sport where you can hang out with mates and each have a go as you feel regardless of what level you climb at.
Do I need a guided session?
If you have never tried bouldering before it is recommended that you have a guided session from the event leader, who will go through everything you will need to know to boulder safely before joining in with the rest of the group.
All those attending the event must also be aware of the centre's own terms and conditions of participation and abide by them, along with any instructions given by the centre’s staff. Everyone must also take responsibility for their own safety while participating in Indoor climbing events.
Independent boulderer?
As above all attending the event  must abide by the centre's T&Cs etc. As an experienced boulderer you are at all times responsible  for your own safety actions and involvement. As such, you should use your own judgement in taking up advice given either by OutdoorLads Volunteers or other experienced climbers.
Can't make this event?
We run weekly climbing and bouldering events in the South East, so why not check out what's coming up near you here:

Title: Ben Wyvis
Summary:
NB THE START TIME IS ONE HOUR EARLIER THAN ORIGINALLY PLANNED. 9.30am.
An 8.75 mile walk to summit this Munro to the north of Inverness. It rises 1046 metres above sea level and as it's an isolated mountain, the views from the summit and its plateau should be excellent if we are lucky to have a clear day. It has been said that it is the only place where you can see both the east and west coasts of Scotland, let's see if this is true.
We start our walk through native woodland where evidence of Spring in the flora and fauna should be abundant. We then have a steep ascent (but without any technical difficulties) to reach the summit plateau. Don't get your hopes up too high on the plateau as it's still another 2 km to the summit cairn along the whale-back ridge.
Photos:
taken by Rob Mitchell and used with permission (but hopefully, the snow risk will have gone by late April!)
Well behaved dogs are welcome on this walk.
- - - - - - - - - - - - - - -
Want to keep in touch with what's going on in Scotland?
Join our Telegram
chat
and
notification
groups and bookmark our upcoming events list via our
Scottish Calendar
.
Difficulty & Distance: 935 m of ascent with a steep section. Exposure to wind on the summit ridge for an hour or more, expect wind chill. The summit can be 10C cooler than at the base.

Title: Mounting Mother Dunch's Buttocks And Other Delights!
Summary:
I haven’t gone full Frankie Howerd with the title “Mother Dunch’s Buttocks,” it's a locally well-known colloquial name for Wittenham Clumps (see more details below), inspired by their distinctive twin-hill shape, which locals long ago likened to a pair of buttocks when viewed from certain angles. “Mother Dunch” refers to Mrs Elizabeth Dunch, an 18th-century local landowner, and carries no further literal or scandalous meaning.
This is a circular walk, starting in Didcot because of its excellent rail links. We won’t linger here, however, instead heading into the countryside to the north, much of which is open and flat beyond the Ridgeway. The walk also follows sections of the Thames Path, with another highlight being Dorchester-on-Thames, a historic and picturesque Oxfordshire village with a magnificent abbey, which we will have time to visit.
Key features of the route:
Wittenham Clumps
are two prominent wooded chalk hills in South Oxfordshire, near the River Thames, known as Round Hill and Castle Hill. The site is notable for its Iron Age hillfort, evidence of much earlier settlement, and some of the oldest planted beech trees in England. Managed today as a nature reserve by Earth Trust, it is a popular landmark offering extensive views across the Thames Valley and holds archaeological, ecological, and cultural significance. From the top, visitors can enjoy sweeping views over the Oxfordshire plain, the Ridgeway, the Thames, and, on a clear day, even Oxford.
Dorchester-on-Thames
is a small, historic village and civil parish located about 8–9 miles south-east of Oxford, where the River Thame meets the River Thames. It has roots as a Roman settlement and was an important Anglo-Saxon ecclesiastical centre, with St Birinus establishing a bishopric there in the 7th century. The Dorchester Abbey (St Peter and St Paul) remains its major landmark. Today, the village is noted for its riverside setting, period architecture, quiet charm, and rich history.
Dorchester Abbey
is a historic church with origins as an Anglo-Saxon cathedral and later a 12th-century Augustinian abbey. It features a mix of Norman and Gothic architecture, including medieval wall paintings and a notable tower. The Abbey remains an active parish church and is recognised for its architectural, historical, and cultural significance.
Little Wittenham
is a small village on the southern bank of the River Thames, beneath the Wittenham Clumps. It has a historic parish church, a handful of old houses, and lies in a conservation area with strong rural character. The surrounding landscape and woods form part of a biologically important nature reserve and Special Area of Conservation, managed by Earth Trust, supporting notable wildlife such as great crested newts.
Didcot
is a town in South Oxfordshire, situated on the River Thames floodplain. It developed rapidly in the 19th century with the arrival of the Great Western Railway and became a key railway and industrial hub. Today, Didcot is known for its transport links and the partly decommissioned Didcot Power Station.
The Route:
We’ll quickly leave Didcot, heading North into the open and flat South Oxfordshire countrywide. Eventually we pass through the village of Long Wittenham, and continuing North we meet with the Thames Path later on. We eventually cross the Thames headed into the historic village of Dorchester-On-Thames where we will have an hour for lunch and look around, and here you can visit the Abbey and/or one of the pubs for drink. After lunch we loop back towards the Thames, cross it at a different point and then take short but steep climb up the first of the Wittenham Clumps. Once at the top we have the option to summit the adjacent second clump. After descending we take a different route back to Didcot.
The route can be found
here
:
Dogs:
Well-behaved dogs are welcome, there are some road sections and crossings and maybe farm animals in some of the fields but dogs can be off the lead for some of the time. Please abide by the Countryside Code (
https://tinyurl.com/rlqvp3h
) and remember not everyone loves dogs as much as you do so be mindful of other members.
Photos
: All photos taken by the leader, with permission for OutdoorLads to use.
Difficulty & Distance: 13.5 or 14 miles, well maintained paths, flat but one short steep climb and total ascent 110 meters.

Title: Howden Easter Leisure Walk
Summary:
Join Shaun for a refreshing, Easter Saturday walk from Howden town centre, exploring open land, agriculture, the nature reserve, gardens, Howden Manor, and passing Howden Minster before returning to the centre.
After the walk there will be an opportunity to socialise and grab some refreshments in Howden town centre. Options for food and drinks include a fish and chips shop, two pubs and several cafés.
Well behaved dogs are welcome on this walk, most of the time they can be let off a lead on this walk.
All photos taken are the leader's own and Outdoor Lads has permission to use them.
Difficulty & Distance: 7Km (4.6miles)

Title: Carneddau Skyline: A Classic Eryri Traverse
Summary:
Join us for a spectacular high-level journey across one of Eryri’s most expansive mountain skylines. The Carneddau deliver that wonderful “big mountain day” feel — wide horizons, rolling ridges and views that seem to stretch forever.
Our route climbs steadily onto open ground before reaching the skyline, where the scenery really comes into its own. Across the day, we’ll be treated to ever-changing panoramas... the jagged Glyderau, the Snowdon massif and the sweep of the Irish Sea beyond Anglesey. Spring is a brilliant time to be up high, with longer daylight, fresh mountain air and a real sense of energy in the hills.
We’ll spend much of the day following broad ridges and high plateaus, linking together some of the Carneddau’s finest viewpoints. It’s a route that feels immersive rather than rushed. Plenty of time for chats on the move, photo stops, and relaxed breaks to take it all in.
While these peaks are popular for good reason, the spacious nature of the range means there’s always a sense of room to breathe as we journey along the skyline above the Ogwen Valley.
A classic spring mountain day — big views, great company, and Carneddau magic from start to finish.
The intention of this walk is to summit both Carnedd Llewelyn and Carnedd Dafydd, passing along the (fairly wide) ridge between them.
Dogs
Well-behaved dogs are welcome on this walk. Owners must keep their dog under close control at all times, clean up after them and ensure they do not disturb other people or dogs.
Please be aware that the start and end of the route pass through a farm with both sheep and cattle. Dogs must be kept on a lead in these sections. Farmers are legally entitled to take action — including shooting — if they believe a dog is worrying or out of control around livestock, so strict control is essential.
Image / photo credits: All leader's own images - with permission for OutdoorLads to use and reuse.
Difficulty & Distance: A big 20km (12 mile) mountain walk, with 1300m (4265 ft) of elevation change. A big day out in the hills, but no exposed or technical terrain.

Title: Mountain Leader Training Course in Snowdonia
Summary:
Learn the skills to Navigate and Lead others in the mountains of the UK
The Mountain Leader award was established to promote the safe enjoyment of the hills and mountains by everyone.
The scheme provides 6 -days training in the technical and group management skills required by those who wish to lead groups in the mountains, hills and moorlands of the United Kingdom and Ireland, in any conditions other than in winter conditions.
Many people choose to do the Mountain Leader (ML) training to gain personal competence in the mountains or for leadership development. Others choose to do the award to spend a week learning new skills and spending quality time in the mountains.
There is no obligation to continue onto the assessment phase of the scheme.
The cost of the course is £540 which includes six days of teaching and five nights accommodation in Bens Bunkhouse, Llanberis. Options to jopin without accomodation are also available.
Please book your place on this course
here
with Pride Expeditions. Nick Read is the trainer, as well as an OutdoorLads member,  and Pride Expeditions is the registered Mountain Training provider.
Before you book onto a Mountain Leader training course;
You should have at least a year’s worth of mountain walking experience
You should have an interest in leading groups in the mountains
You must be a member (club or individual) of a mountaineering council (BMC, Mountaineering Ireland, Mountaineering Scotland). Find out why this is
required
You must create an account on the Mountain Training system and register for the Mountain Leader qualification (cost: £67)
Here
Please ensure you have entered your minimum prerequisites in your digital logbook (DLOG) at the time of attending the training course. This is 20 quality mountain days (QMD's)
The course would be useful to any ODL members who are already or wish to become Mountain Walk Leaders. It will cover the Mountain Leader syllabus as set by the awarding body Mountain Training.
This includes the following areas:
Group management and the responsibilities of the group leader
Navigation
Access and the environment
Mountain hazards (including steep ground and rivers) and emergency procedures
Equipment
Expedition skills including an overnight wild camp
Mountain Weather
The course will be based at Bens Bunkhouse, Llanberis. The course cost includes five nights accommodation at the bunkhouse. Food is not included - the bunkhouse is self-catering.
If you can tick all of the above boxes in terms of experience, here's what to do next:
1.Register for the event with OutdoorLads.
2. Create an account on Mountain Training's Candidate Management System or log in if you already have one. Find CMS here
https://mt.tahdah.me/account/registernew
3. Register for the
Mountain Leader scheme
(this costs £67) To attend the Mountain Leader Training course you must register with
Mountain Training.
4. Send a message to
Nick.Read@outdoorlads.com
to say you wish to book on this course and if you would like to access available funding (on condition of leading future OutdoorLads mountain walks).
If you are a regular walk leader for OutdoorLads and working towards being a Mountain Walk Leader or are already one, there is funding available towards the course cost to help you achieve this valuable course. Please contact support or Nick Read to apply.
Image Credits:
Photos provided by event leader Nick Read
Difficulty & Distance: You will be mountain walking for 6-days

Title: Mount Triglav multi-activity Slovenia
Summary:
Visit the beautiful country of Slovenia for a week of trekking and activities in the stunning Julian Alps with OutdoorLads.
An action-packed week. A summit climb. White-water rafting, Canyoning and stunning alpine landscapes!
Our main objective will be to climb Mt Triglav, Slovenia's highest mountain and to trek in the surrounding Triglavski National Park. Triglav is a low altitude peak (2864m) that involves scrambling to reach the summit. The ridge is equipped with an iron walkway called a Via Ferrata to help us reach the top securely and safely. A good head for heights and good walking fitness is required. During this four day trek we will be staying in mountain huts, similar to hostels and eating local cuisine.
After the four day trek in the stunning Julian Alps we will travel to Bovec to raft on the famous emerald Soca river. This river runs through Slovenia and Italy and eventually drains into the Adriatic Sea near Trieste.
Our final night will be spent in a hostel in Ljubljana where there will be the opportunity to do some sightseeing in the capital of this friendly little country.
This price for this action-packed week is
only £1350
which includes 7-nights accommodation in hostels and mountain huts.
What is included?
All Accomodation
UIMLA International Mountain Leader
All Breakfasts
Dinner and lunch when trekking
Canyoning on the first day (this will be a beginners canyon but you can opt out if needed)
Via Feratta kit, harness and helmet and a Slovenian Mountain Guide for Triglav
White-Water rafting in Bovec (this is suitable for beginners)
What is not included?
Flights to and from Ljubljana airport
Food and drinks in Ljubljana
Lunch in Bled
Insurance - you will need travel insurance to include the activities
More information on this trip is available from the
Pride Expeditions website
ITINERARY
Day 1  Arrival, 29th August 2026
Transfer from Ljubljana Airport directly to Bled (45 mins). Dinner in a local restaurant with traditional Slovenian food or Pizza. Accommodation at a hostel in Bled.
Day 2 Half-day Canyoning, 30th August
We will be canyoning in Bled & Bohinj area in Jereka gorge (easy) or Grmecica gorge (moderate level) near Bled. No experience required all equipment provided. Upon return there will be time for lunch and a relaxing afternoon in Bled. Tonight we will have dinner in a traditional restaurant in Bled.
Day 3 Hiking Bled to Kredarica Hut (2500m), 31st August
After breakfast we will travel by bus to the Pokljuka Plateau. We hike up to Kredarica Mt Hut. This is a 6hr steady climb we will stop for a packed lunch along the route.  Dinner and accomodation in Kredarica hut.
Day 4 Hiking Kredarica Hut to Triglav Lakes Hut (1685m), 1st September
After breakfast we will via ferrata climb to the top of Mt Triglav and then descend to lunch. After some rest and photos we will walk to Triglav Lakes Hut. Have dinner at the hut and overnight. 8hrs walking.
Day 5 Hiking Triglav Lakes Hut to Krn Lakes Hut ,2nd September
We will walk from Triglav Lakes to Komna mountain hut. This is where we will stop for lunch before continuing through the Bogatin area and onwards to Vratca saddle. After crossing the saddle we descend to Krn lakes hut. This is where we will have dinner and spend the night.  6 - 7 hrs walking.
Day 6 Hiking and rafting Krn Lakes hut to Lepena valley and onwards to Bovec, 3rd September
We walk down from Krn Lakes into the stunning Lepena Valley. After visiting a few historical sites from WW1 we will be picked up and transferred to Bovec for white water rafting on the emerald Soca River. In the evening we eat and have a few drinks and our evening meal in Bovec.
Day 7  Transfer day Bovec - Ljubljana 4th September
We will be transferred from Bovec to Ljubljana (4hrs) where we will be staying in a boutique hostel in the Metelkova district of Ljubljana. You will have some time to visit the imposing castles and pretty bridges of downtown Ljubljana before our final meal in Slovenia. The hostel will be in walking distance of the city centre.
Day 8  Ljubljana to Home 5th September
We will be transferred to the airport to connect with your flight back to the UK.
GETTING THERE
You are responsible for organising your own flight to Ljubljana. Easy Jet run a daily flight from London Gatwick and from Edinburgh, there are also direct British Airways flights from London Heathrow. Or if you want to fly from Manchester or Bristol you will need to fly with Lufthansa and change in Frankfurt.
There will be two minibus transfers to Bled.
One at 1400 for British Airways and Lufthansa flight
One at 2200 for the Easy Jet flight from Gatwick
Please let me know well in advance which transfer you will be on.
THE ACCOMODATION
Whilst in Bled, Bovec and Ljubljana we will be in hostels in a mixture of triple and quad rooms. In the mountain huts we tend to be in one or two dormitory rooms depending on availability. Single rooms are not possible on this trip. All the hostels we use are locally owned and friendly and welcoming. They will be within walking distance of the local sights.
HOW TO BOOK
Bookings and Deposits: The full price is £1350.
To book your place on this trip (or secure a place on the waiting list) please pay a £250 deposit direct to Pride Expeditions. This will secure your place on the trip.
PRIDE EXPEDITIONS Ltd
Starling Bank
Sort Code:60-83-71
Account Number 19166775
Please message the Event Leader with your full name and email address in order to receive further documents.
On receipt of your deposit a confirmation email will be sent. Attached will be a comprehensive medical form for you to fill in as well as booking terms and conditions. Once six people have paid a deposit the trip will be guaranteed and further payments can be made in instalments. If you cancel after the trip has been confirmed and bookings have been made in Slovenia you will lose your deposit. All monies must be paid by the 2nd of July 2026. Deposits are fully refundable until the trip is guaranteed and bookings are made at which point the terms and conditions will come into force.
Please be aware that it is the booking of the deposit that secures your place on the trip or waiting list, rather than booking onto the trip via the OutdoorLads site.
Image Credits:
All images copyright and supplied by Nick Read.
Difficulty & Distance: 8-action packed days including a 4-day trek

Title: Twilight Ascent: The Glow-Worm Hunt
Summary:
Glow Worm Adventure
Kick things off properly with a relaxed muster at The
BeerEngine
in Southwick — time to fuel the expedition with a well-earned pint of craft ale or a quality gin before we set out. This is your chance to gather the team, check the kit, and raise a glass before trading bar lights for twilight skies.
Once fortified, we head out and climb toward Southwick Hill Nature Reserve, leaving the streets behind as the landscape opens into rolling downland and big evening views. The route builds atmosphere as daylight fades, with skylarks overhead and the first stars beginning to show.
Our mission
: a dusk
glow-worm hunt
. As darkness settles, we’ll slow the pace and scan the grassland banks and paths for tiny green living lanterns — one of the Downs’ most magical nighttime sights. Expect quiet moments, sharp eyes, and that buzz of discovery when the first glow appears.
A sociable start, a steady uphill adventure, and a wild finish under the night sky. Perfect for explorers who like their walks with both good spirits and natural wonders.
The
route
is a 5 mile circular walk with 140 metres in gradual elevation.
Food
Arrive early and enjoy Fish and Chips from the Southwick Fish and Chips Shop.
New Members:
If this is your first event or you are new to OutdoorLads we’d love to see you. This type of event is perfect for you to get to know other ODL members. Please message me if you have any questions.
Difficulty & Distance: 5 mile easy stroll

Title: Boldre Bluebells, Springtime Setley and an ANZAC Day Tribute
Summary:
The New Forest is well known for its free roaming livestock which play an important part in sculpting the landscape, and we should see plenty of ponies (and possibly some new-born foals) in the second half of the walk as we cross the open landscape of Setley Plain. Before that, however, we'll visit one of the best kept spring-time secrets in the Forest in the stunning bluebell woods of Roydon Woods NNR (National Nature Reserve) which will hopefully be in their full finery during our visit.
The
route
takes us immediately out of the town and up to the oldest church in the New Forest. St Nicholas Church was mentioned in the Domesday Book, and it is believed that many Norman and Plantagenet kings such as William II worshipped here when they came to stay at the then neighbouring Manor when visiting the Forest for hunting trips.
Brockenhurst was the site of the No. 1 New Zealand General Hospital during World War I where over 20,000 NZ casualities were treated. The churchyard has 100 Commonwealth War Graves, 93 of which are New Zealanders. Our visit will be particularly timely, as it falls on ANZAC Day, and a service is due to be held on the site the following day.
Our route then heads on into Roydon Woods Nature Reserve which is an impressive patchwork of wildlife rich habitats. After crossing the Lymington River which is known to be the home of otters and kingfishers (I've seen plenty of one, but yet to see any of the other!), we'll climb uphill through the hazel coppices which should be a stunning azure carpet of bluebells at this time of year. We may also be lucky enough to hear our first cuckoos of the season as they usually arrive in the middle of April.
St John The Baptist Church, dating back to 1200, lies on the edge of the woodland in an isolated and tranquil spot above the village of Boldre. Three sarsen stones similar to those used at Stonehenge, have been found in the church foundations, and so it is believed that site has been used as a place of worship for a considerably longer period.
After crossing back over the river, we'll climb up Sandy Down, cross the main road and then head on out onto the open heathland of the forest, stopping for a break by Setley Pond. The pond is a former gravel pit and is a popular location for radio controlled yacht enthusiasts. On Boxing Day, it is the location of the hotly contested Setley Cup.
Most of the remainder of the route takes us over the open heaths among the free roaming ponies, donkeys and cattle. Visitors to the forest often mistakenly refer to the "wild ponies" but all the livestock are owned by forest commoners who turn their animals out to graze the land, but remain responsible for their welfare.
We'll return to Brockenhurst where the walk will finish at the station, but feel free to explore the shops, pubs and restaurants in the town if you wish!
Dogs:
This walk is suitable for dogs but they will need to be on a lead whilst in Roydon Woods NNR. Additionally, much of the second half of the walk is on open heathland during peak bird nesting season. Many rare and vulnerable ground-nesting species are trying to breed at this time of year and is essential that you have close control over your dog to prevent unnecessary disturbance. There will also be free-roaming livestock on the heath with the possibility of new-born foals too.
All images used on this page taken by the event leader and permission granted for use by ODL
Difficulty & Distance: Distance is 15km (9.5 miles) with 160m of ascent.

Title: Jet, Fossils and an abandoned Port Walk
Summary:
This is a gorgeous cliff top walk on the Cleveland way in North Yorkshire that takes in two beautiful beach villages and one abandoned one we can explore. Starting in Staithes we will walk to Runswick Bay, stop there for a bite to eat and rest, and then walk back to Staithes. It is roughly 8 miles in total.
The start of the walk is accessible by the x4 bus service from Middlesborough, Redcar or Whitby. But if you can car share it’s probably a lot easier as we will be meeting in the main car park.
Difficulty & Distance: Three tough hills to climb.

Title: The Witton Weavers Way to Hoghton Bottoms
Summary:
Join Mike and OutdoorLads for a circular 8-mile walk from Witton Country Park in Blackburn to Hoghton Bottoms. We will make our way through the woodland, parkland and farmland of Witton Country Park, former home of the Feilden family.
Witton House was sadly demolished in the 1950s, but the historic outbuildings remain. We will make our way up through Billinge Woods and visit the Wainwright memorial at the top of the hill, paying tribute to famous fell walker, Alfred Wainwright.
We will then pass through the village of Pleasington via Pleasington Priory and the Golf Club before walking alongside the River Darwen towards Hoghton Viaduct, a Grade II listed railway viaduct passing over the River Darwen. Our walk will then take us upstream crossing woodland and farmland before we meet the main road leading back towards Pleasington.
We will stop for lunch mid-way through the walk. There will also be an opportunity to stop for refreshments in one of the local establishments in Pleasington before heading back to the car park.
All photos were taken by walk leader and used with permission on the event page.
Your First OutdoorLads Walk Event?

If this is your first OutdoorLads event, you should check out the information designed specifically for you on the
My First Event
page on the OutdoorLads website.
Difficulty & Distance: Approximately 8 miles. Mixed terrain including a couple of uphill climbs

Title: Chateaus and Chagalls - A Topnotch Tour around Tonbridge
Summary:
This walk includes a visit to a church to see its stained-glass windows. A donation of £3 towards the church's upkeep is requested if you visit it.
"
C'est magnifique! Je les ferai tous!
"
(
"It's magnificent! I will do them all!"
) said artist Marc Chagall at the dedication of his window in the small church of Tudeley. And he gradually did just that, making Tudeley the only church in the world with all its windows designed by him. This is just one attraction on a walk that starts and ends in the historic town of Tonbridge and takes in oast houses and estate houses (Jacobean Somerhill House, Tonbridge Castle), and public houses and God's houses (more stained glass, this time by William Morris). The natural attractions abound too, including the banks of the River Medway and the splendid views across the High Weald Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty.
The sights:
Tonbridge:
A market town on the River Medway, dominated by the C13 castle gatehouse. The core of the town still contains many buildings dating from the C15: the oldest is The Portreeve's House. Tonbridge rose against Queen Mary's marriage to the King of Spain in 1554 and as a result saw the burning of a number of Protestants. During the Civil War, the town was garrisoned by the Parliamentarians who refortified the castle. Royalists failed to retake the town. From 1740 the Medway Navigation Company allowed coal and lime to be transported to the town, and gunpowder, hops and timber to be carried downriver to Maidstone and the Thames. Some of the original warehouses and the wharves remain today. Somerhill Park is a Grade-I listed Jacobean Mansion, built 1611-1613 and now a private school.
All Saints, Tudeley:
Mostly C13 and C14, restored in the C18. In 1963, Sarah, the daughter of Sir Henry and Lady D'Avigdor-Goldsmid of Somerhill House, drowned off Rye, East Sussex. The couple commissioned Russian-French Cubist-Fauvist artist Marc Chagall to design a memorial stained glass window. When Chagall came for the dedication of the window in 1967 and saw the church for the first time, he exclaimed
"It's magnificent! I will do them all!"
. He designed the remaining eleven windows over 10 years. The last windows were installed in 1985 after Chagall's death.
Southborough:
A large village in a formerly heavily wooded setting on the edge of Tunbridge Wells. The Common is a Site of Nature Conservation Interest (SNCI). St Peter's Church was designed by Decimus Burton, architect of Marble Arch, some of Regent's Park and Kew's Temperate House. Southborough Common will be our lunch stop and the Hand and Sceptre pub is adjacent.
Bidborough:
A village on Bidborough Ridge overlooking the Medway Valley and northern High Weald. St Lawrence is a small Norman sandstone church, elevated above the village. A north aisle was added in the C13, and Ewan Christian built one to the south in 1876. Grade II* listed. Most windows made by William Morris and Company, some designed by Edward Burne-Jones.
Haysden Country Park:
64 hectares of countryside with two lakes and a stretch of the River Medway. The park is home to a variety of wildlife including waterfowl, wild flowers, and insect life. A Site of Nature Conservation Interest, and Local Nature Reserve.
Tonbridge Castle:
In 1067 William I charged Richard Fitz Gilbert with guarding the Medway. He erected a motte-and-bailey castle on the site. In 1088, the de Clare family (descendants of Fitz Gilbert) rebelled against William II. His army besieged the castle, which fell, and as punishment the king had both the castle and the town burnt to the ground. Before 1100, the de Clares replaced the wooden castle with a stone shell keep. This was reinforced during the C13, and in 1295 a stone wall was built around the town. The twin-towered gatehouse of was built by Richard de Clare, completed in 1260. The castle was not lived in between 1521 and the late C18. The mansion was added in 1793. Both castle and mansion are Grade I listed buildings. The motte can be climbed up and the bailey is now a park.
Dogs:
I love having dogs on my walks and this walk is suitable for them. They risk getting bored while the group sees the Chagall stained-glass windows in the church, although obedient dogs are welcomed in the church. The walk is of moderate length, but there will be fields with livestock and a number of stiles and roads to negotiate and lanes to walk along. A dog must be obedient if it is off the lead.
The
route
:
We'll set off north from the station as far as the bridge in the centre of the town. We'll then pick up the Wealdway east as far as Postern Lane and The Postern and take that. heading east, as far as Tudeley Hale, before heading south to Tudeley proper. We'll then south along Five Oak Green Road and follow the Tunbridge Wells Circular Walk (TWCW) south to Dislingbury Road where we'll go under the A21 and follow a footpath under the railway line. We'll then rejoin the TWCW to Southborough. After lunch and crossing Southborough Common, we'll follow the Eden Valley Walk to Bidborough. This will lead us along the B2176, then north to Haysden, under the A21 and through Haysden Country Park over and along the Medway back to Tonbridge where we'll have a quick look around the sights before heading back to the station.

Title: For Good Friday: A Downland Rove with Shepherds' Drove and Druids' Grove
Summary:
'When proud-pied April dress'd all in his trim / Hath put a spirit of youth in every thing'. (Sonnet 98)
A Downland Rove:
A rambling route but symmetrical in elevation, ascending and descending Headley Downs and Headley Heath, crossing the River Mole, then ascending and descending Fetcham Downs.
A Shepherds' Drove:
Sweeping swathes of herb-rich chalk grassland fringed with beech woodland (the beech mast blanketed with bluebells if they're early) and thickets of holly, yew, juniper and box.
A Druids' Grove:
A mysterious clump of yew trees in the grounds of Norbury Park mansion. At 1500 years old, among the oldest trees in the land, and according to folklore the site of ancient rituals. Lunchtime will be split between Mickleham Downs for a picnic and Mickleham village for a pub stop.
The sights:
Leatherhead:
The name is from
Leodridan
meaning 'place where people can ride (across the river)', the river being the Mole. The High Street has been much improved recently after it was deemed 'Britain's worst' in a Radio 4 poll (this says more about Radio 4 listeners than it does about the high street). Neo-Georgian Town Hall of 1935. Handsome 14-arched bridge of 1782, widened in 1824.
Nower Wood:
Semi-open woodland on sandy soil with hazel and bluebell wood. Nower Wood is managed by the Woodland Trust.
Headley:
A superb location high up on the North Downs behind Box Hill with views to London. A lot of character.
Headley Heath:
'A wide network of tracks to explore, and stunning views to rival any of its neighbours - the perfect place to enjoy the countryside, whether walking, cycling or horseriding. The Heath is a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI) for its rich, varied, and sometimes rare, wildlife, made up of a wonderful mosaic of open heath, chalk downland slopes and mixed woodland.' (From the National Trust website.)
White Hill:
A variety of habitats on chalk: beech hanger, yew and juniper copse and open grassland, cared for by National Trust.
Mickleham:
In the Mole Valley in the Dorking Gap. Picturesque cottages staggering up a slight incline. St Michael's Church of early C12 with a massive restoration of 1871 giving it a weird side-tower. Lots of character. Weir Bridge over the River Mole is Grade II* listed and from 1840. Old House is superb, Dutch-gabled brick, of 1636. Box Hill School is neo-medieval, Victorian Cherkley Court, now a hotel called Beaverbrook, was the home of Baron Beaverbrook, press baron and Minister for Aircraft Production.
A24 Mickleham Bypass:
A fine piece of road landscaping, using natural and planted vegetation and a true divide between carriageways; 1934.
Norbury Park:
A plain house of 1774 occupying a belvedere site overlooking the Dorking Gap. Rich grasslands and woodlands around, managed by Surrey Wildlife Trusts. The Druids' Grove may have been a pre-Christian meeting place, although 'druids' is fanciful. The yews are c1500 years old.
The
route
(please click the link)
:
A route along roads and lanes ad tracks south out of Leatherhead will bring us to a crossing with the Leatherhead bypass and a long, straight track heading southeast to Cherkley Wood. We'll then pick up Langley Lane (a track) to Headley past Nower Wood, and do a loop around and behind the village. We'll enter Headley Heath and follow a bridleway to cross Lodgebottom Road and ascend White Hill for lunch. Descending Mickleham Downs we'll reach the village itself and go to the pub. Heading north out of the village and crossing the A24 and River Mole, we'll follow a bridleway and tracks heading in a loop west and south and north around Norbury Park and over Fetcham Downs. We'll then find a bridleway that will take us past Bockett's Farm and over Hawk's Hill to Thorncroft where a pleasant walk along the River Mole will bring us back to Leatherhead town centre.
Dogs:
Dogs are very welcome on all my walks and this is a very good dog walk, with plenty of woods and fields for them to run free, although there will be country lanes and livestock in some fields necessitating the dog going on a lead. A dog off the lead must be obedient.

Title: Blackburn to Adlington canal walk (LLC#5)
Summary:
This is a linear walk from Blackburn to Adlington along a stretch of the 204km (127.25 miles) Leeds & Liverpool Canal, blending industrial heritage with peaceful countryside.
This is walk
stage 5 of 8
spanning the full length of the Leeds & Liverpool Canal.
As we leave Blackburn, we'll say goodbye to the industrial heritage of East Lancashire as it gives way to open countryside and quiet farmland. Our route passes through a series of stone bridges and old mill buildings that remind us of the canal’s working past. We'll soon find long, uninterrupted stretches beside the water and wide views across the Lancashire hills.
Further along, the canal skirts the edges of Withnell Fold, a historic paper‑mill village, before continuing to Johnson’s Hillock Locks, a flight of seven locks that marks one of the most striking engineering features on this part of the canal.
This is a rewarding linear walk that blends heritage, scenery, and a real sense of journey along one of Britain’s most characterful waterways.
We'll catch a bus or train back to Blackburn (for those that need to return) - the best option will depend on your individual onward travel plans.
Stage 1: Leeds to Bingley - 30 Aug 2025
Stage 2: Bingley to Gargrave - 20 Sep 2025
Stage 3: Gargrave to Burnley - 21st Feb 2026
Stage 4: Burnley to Blackburn - 21st March 2026
Stage 5: Blackburn to Adlington - 11th April 2026
Stage 6: Adlington to Parbold - 9th May 2026
Stage 7: Parbold to Maghull
Stage 8: Maghull to Liverpool
Dogs:
No dogs on this leg of the walk due to the length of walk and narrow towpaths which will be busy with cyclists and other walkers.
If the event is fully booked:
Please
join the wait list as people frequently drop out during the week before an event and you are likely to get a place on the walk.
If this is your first OutdoorLads walk event:
If this is your first event, we’ll make sure you feel welcomed. Please
check out the My First Event page
, and don’t hesitate to drop me a message if you have questions or want to know more about this walk.

Title: Hertfordshire via Hitch Wood
Summary:
Join Alex for this 11-mile circular walk to Hitch Wood's blossoming bluebells
Our route begins at Stevenage station and we will weave our way westwards via the town's dual carriageways and underpasses for a short while until we cross into open countryside. We head towards the small settlement of Langley across undulating countryside of fields and woodland.
After Langley we will head across open fields once more until we reach the village of St Paul's Walden. Here we will turn northwards and skirt around the edge of St Paul's Walden Bury estate before ascending towards the tower of All Saints' church, its medieval façade contrasting with its Georgian interior.
From here we will head around the estate of Stagenhoe, now a centre for neurologucal care, towards the Hitch Wood nature reserve. A waymarked path, the Hitchwood Trail, leads us through the woods, which should be bursting with bluebells in late April. We will stop for lunch somewhere around here.
We will then continue our journey north and past the atmospheric ruins of the 14th-century Minsden Chapel. From here our route turns southeast and takes us back downhill to Stevenage across open farmland. We will head through the Old Town area and may stop at a local pub on the way back to the station.
Don't waste that space!
Please only book if you know you can make it. If your plans change please free up the space for someone else.
New members
Please feel free to join this as your first OutdoorLads event. If you have any questions then feel free to message me, or go to the "My First Event" section under "About Us".
Is it dog friendly?
Dogs are welcome but please bear in mind not everyone likes dogs as much as you. Some of the fields we cross may contain livestock so please make sure this will not be an issue.

Title: The Llyn Peninsular AONB: Yr Eifl and Nant Gwrtheyrn
Summary:
This is a figure of 8 Walk so also suitable for those who would prefer a shorter day.  If you are ONLY coming for Part B the afternoon, please message the leader in advance. If you are coming for the whole event, or just the morning, there is no need to make special contact. You must be registered to take part in either part.  Both parts start at the same place.
Part A. (Departing 10:45) 4.5 miles,  1200' of height gain
Yr Eifl, translates from Welsh to mean "the forks" or "the trident". The name describes the distinctive shape of its three peaks (Garn Ganol, Tre'r Ceiri, and Garn Fôr) as they appear from a distance.  The peaks are also known known as 'The Rivals' perhaps because they appear to rival the big peaks on the main ranges. The Rivals stand right on the coast and create a most impressive backdrop when seen from the north, or from Yns Mon (Anglesey).
After leaving the meeting point we will embark on a circular route to the summit of Garn Ganol, 564m. This is the highest peak on the Peninsular .  The route to the summit is only a mile and a half from the car park, however much of it is a gradient of 25% (1 in 4) and there's 1000' of ascent to this point. We will take it steady and wait for slower walkers to catch up. From the top if the visibility is clear The Wicklow mountains of Ireland, The mountain of Snaefell on The Isle of Man, and the Lake district might be seen. Much of Eryri from Yr Wddfa down to Cadair Idris and right across Cardigan bay may be visible too. Bring your Binoculars. Next we will see the Tre’r Ceiri Hill fort inhabited between 150 BC and 400 AD. Little is known about these early inhabitants, other than they depended heavily on the local iron ore. It is one of the best preserved in the UK, and a fitting place for lunch. The route back down is a bit easier on the knees.
Part B (Assemble at 13:00 ) 3.5 miles 950' of height gain
Setting off in the opposite direction we will embark on a 2nd circular walk with good views of the coastline where you might spot grey seals and plenty of marine birdlife. After a short steep descent we find ourselves at the quaint settlement of Nant Gwrtheyrn which has a Heritage Centre (free) and a rather nice cafe
(details here)
. Once a quarry and for a while a 'hippy commune', the settlement is now a residential school for those learning the Welsh Language. The setting couldn't be nicer. Revived by tea and cake we will circle around for a couple more miles of ascent, back up to the car park.
If you are thinking of making a weekend of it the Llyn Peninsular has some amazing camping sites
like this one
a few miles down the road.
Photos:
AndyM, event leader, except those as stamped and licensed for re-use under the Creative Commons license at Geograph.org.uk
Bad Weather
This event may be altered or cancelled if persistent heavy rain or poor visibility is forecast - you will be notified by at least 18:00 hours the night before in this case
Dogs
are welcome to join us on this event but please note the following:
We may pass through areas with livestock where your dog needs to be on a lead.
Not everyone likes dogs so please ensure your furry pal doesn't worry other members.
There may be obstacles to carry your dog across, and your dog needs to have the stamina to keep up with the group.
Are you new to Outdoorlads and considering your first event?
Have a look at this link, hopefully it will help
https://www.outdoorlads.com/my-first-event
Difficulty & Distance: Part A 4.5 miles and 1200' of overall height gain; Part B 3.5 miles and 950'. Medium pace. Under foot gravel, grass or rocky paths.

Title: BIG20: THE Celebration Event - Hostel
Summary:
Where did the 20 years go!? It's been an epic tens of thousands of miles walked, climbed, skied, kayaked, cycled; thousands of friendships made; and an immeasurable amount of laughter and incredible memories.
We hope you can join us in the Peak District to celebrate this epic milestone!
There are three accommodation types for this event, so please ensure you book on the correct one:
Hostel
(this event page)
Pods
Camping
All accommodation types include the catering and entertainment in the main building.
The hostel has a range of modern, all-ensuite rooms with bunk beds, sleeping between up to 6 people each. They are spacious and well appointed, this is a lovely hostel!
The weekend will include a formal AGM which all Full/Concession Members are very much encouraged to attend to help us formally celebrate our amazing volunteers and members. (If you want to attend just the AGM and not stay at the hostel or for the party, that will be possible and details will be made available in due course, along with details of the AGM live stream.)
You can also expect amazing walks and other events offered by our volunteers, on-site activities (is that a zip wire on top of the building!?), spectacular food, a fully-stocked bar, a late night disco with DJ Dan, and we're going to party like its 2016 with a Ceilidh with Triple Scotch (yes, they want to come back!)!
Whether you have been a member since 2006 when we formed or only just joined us today - all are welcome and all have played a part creating, shaping and continuing this wonderful organisation which has changed lives, brought lifelong friends together (and even husbands!), and given everyone the chance to try something new with our wide range of activities for all.
You don't want to miss out on this epic weekend, so get booking!

support@outdoorlads.com
and we can arrange that. We'll discuss with you how much you'd like to pay each month and a time frame over which you'd like to pay. You will need to have paid in full by the 31 August.

Title: Swanley to Lullingstone Castle with a Pub Lunch
Summary:
Enjoy a mid-Spring walk through the
Kent countryside
exploring war memorials, fields, farms, castles and the remains of an ancient Roman villa.
This is a circular walk from Swanley station, following the tracks for a very short distance before passing through farms and a field or two, then pass under the M25, until (oh look...) more fields and farmlands!!
After about 3.5 miles we will arrive at the
Lullingstone Roman Villa.
It is believed that the first building on the site was built in AD 100.  Little is known of this original structure, but in the early 2nd century a shrine and bathhouse existed here.  The villa served many purposes including that of a Roman governor's home, an enlarged 3rd century villa to its conversion to a Christian temple / church.  Sadly in the 5th Century a fire destroyed the villa, leading to its abandonment and eventual decay.  However many of the Roman mosaics and artifacts were preserved throughout history and are now on display in the museum.  Although closed at the time of publishing, it is hoped that the museum will be open when we arrive.
We will then continue toward the village of Eynsford and the
Norman "enclosure castle"
that gives the village its name.  Built by William de Eynsford between 1085 and 1087, its purpose was to protect the lands of Lanfranc, the Archbishop of Canterbury at the time.  The family owned and occupied the castle until 1261, when the last of the family died, with no line of succession. Access into this English Heritage site is free so we will spend a few minutes exploring the ruins.
We will leave Eynsford to the north, heading along Sparepenny Lane, with a brief stop at the memorial to Flight Lieutenant James A. Paterson, MBE, whose Spitfire X4422 crashed near the location of the memorial.
As we continue along Sparepenny Lane we come to the village of Farningham, which will include a pub lunch stop at
The Pied Bull
, right on the High Street.  Should you prefer something other than the pub lunch, there are two small shops in Eynsford we can pop into or you can bring something from home..  I have arranged pre-ordering with the pub, but as such the event booking will need to close on Friday 10 April so that orders can be selected and sent to the pub.  I will send the menu choices as the event gets closer, but feel free to check out the menu in the link.  Also please note that all food and drink will be at your expense.
After lunch we will make our way back toward Swanley, stopping briefly to view the Farningham Cattle Screen.  Although the true purpose of this unique structure is unknown, it is believed to prevent cattle from "wandering downstream" while crossing the River Darent.
The walk back to Swanley is along the edge of the A20 so care will need to be taken.  This will be roughly 2 miles and will cross several round-abouts and will go under the M25.  Please note that slight route alterations may be needed along this stretch.  We will then cross over the M25 onto London Road making our we back to Swanley station.
I love having our furry friends on my events; they will need to be kept on leashes at all times.
Photos are courtesy of
www.unsplash.co.uk
, and
www.outdoorlads.com
and are used with permission and thanks.
Difficulty & Distance: Relatively flat

Title: Blaenau Ffestiniog: A UNESCO World Heritage Landscape
Summary:
Explore the World Heritage ‘Slate Landscape of Northwest Wales’ on this walk.
The Slate Landscape of Northwest Wales was given UNESCO World Heritage status in 2021).  Recognised for its profound impact on the industrial revolution, this site encompasses quarries, mines, mills, and transport systems that operated primarily from 1780 to 1940.
Starting from World Famous Blaenau Ffestiniog ‘The town that roofed the world’ our route threads its way through the quaries where for decades thousands of men toiled to cut the precious stone that is so durable and lightweight as a roofing material, and has many other uses. We will be fortunate to have ODL member Bedwyr taking part who lives in the area. Bedwyr will share his knowledge of the area as we progress.
An initial steady gradient ascends around 1300’ past a pair of reservoirs to a flattish summit from where much of the scarred landscape and bigger peaks beyond will be visible.
We will pass by Manod Quarry, where during the WW2, the contents of the National Gallery were stored, to protect them from bomb damage. Explosives were used to enlarge the entrance, so that lorries could be unloaded inside the hillside. The project was quite secretive, and many of the smaller paintings arrived either in Cadbury delivery trucks or in Post Office vans, so that their contents were not obvious. Of course the locals soon got wind of what was going on.
After that we will pass down the steep valley of Cwm Teigl, following the rushing river as we go.  Our way back into Blaenau passes a natural lake, and then follows down an incline, being the route of an old tramway used to carry away the slate. Finishing up at the station where the narrow gauge steam railway was used to take the slate down to the coast at Porthmadog, from where is was taken by ship to many destinations. The railway initially ran by gravity downhill, and then a team of horses pulled the empty wagons back up. The steam locos that came later are a unique design with two boilers to create enough power for the gradient.
All in all this promises to be a fascinating day walk, and one that will deliver some good exercise on the hilly mountainsides.
Waitlist?
there's usually a turnover of places in the week leading up to an event. If there's still a waitlist we can often allow extra spaces, so please join the waitlist and you'll amost certainly get a place.
All photos:
AndyM, event leader.
Bad Weather
This event may be altered or cancelled if persistent heavy rain or poor visibility is forecast - you will be notified by at least 18:00 hours the night before in this case
Dogs
are welcome to join us on this event but please note the following:
This route contains unprotected steep drops hazardous to free spirited dogs. We may pass through areas with livestock where your dog needs to be on a lead. Not everyone likes dogs so please ensure your furry pal doesn't worry other members. There may be obstacles to carry your dog across, and your dog needs to have the stamina to keep up with the group.

Title: Hebden Bridge and Heptonstall on a Thursday
Summary:
Fancy taking a
Thursday
off for a walk and mooch in and round Hebden Bridge - then this 7 mile walk hosted by Stephen is for you.
The idea is to spend time exploring Gibson Mill, Heptonstall and Hebden Bridge as part of this walk
You may be aware that
Happy Valley
and
Riot Women
were filmed here.
So Stephen will be able to point some key filming locations en route
Highlights of the walk will be -
Walking from the railway station, meandering through Hebden Bridge.
Then follow Hebden Water through the woodlands
visiting
Gibson Mill
, a National Trust museum and quick look around
Either visit the exhibition there or sit in the cafe
https://www.nationaltrust.org.uk/visit/yorkshire/hardcastle-crags/event…
Wander up through the woods on to the moors with views across the Calder valley
Venturing into the ancient village of
Heptonstall
We will take to some time out to explore  the village and church yard
Some TV series were filmed here including Gallows Pole and Happy Valley
https://www.theguardian.com/tv-and-radio/2023/may/31/the-gallows-pole-r…
Then descend down into Hebden Bridge for a mooch around taking in one or 2 of the pubs and maybe some food.

Title: Spring in Saddleworth
Summary:
A new hostel for OutdoorLads and a slightly different location!! A stones throw from the Dark Peak area of the Peak District, but only a short hop from Manchester.
Our accommodation is situated on the north western edge of the Peak District yet with easy road and rail links (20 mins walk from Greenfield railway station!). It sits on the hillside just outside Greenfield near Dovestone Reservoir.
The centre caters for those who love outdoor pursuits and the website states "fell walking, rock climbing, caving, cycling, pony trekking, swimming, canoeing, sailing, running, angling, orienteering and even llama trekking are all catered for in the area". Not that we are going to be doing all of those activities, but there quite a few options!! Plus we are near the lovely villages of Uppermill, Marsden or a 20 mins train ride into Manchester. A short trip over the hills and you could visit Standedge canal tunnel (highest, deepest, longest canal tunnel) to do the 30mins boat tour (separate booking needed and costs £12/£10 concession).
The centre is a single storey building with four bunkhouse style dorms. There is a large kitchen, 2 toilet blocks, a dinning/social area and a private garden.
For a plan of the weekend, I expect we'll do something like:
Friday
: Arrive from 5pm. Get settled in and get chatting with others to reconnect or get to know new people. We will have the usual OutdoorLads buffet. So please bring along some food to add to the buffet (enough for 1-2 people and please don't all just bring pizza/quiche/sausage rolls, please).
Saturday
: A nice breakfast to set the day off.
Then there will be the option for a lovely walk across Saddleworth (to be decided). I'm not a walk leader so if anyone is and can help to lead a walk, please message me and we'll be ever so grateful! The walk around Dovestone Reservoir and Dovestone Edge is beautiful though.
Other options, will be at your own arrangement unless there is sufficient interest from a large group.
We'll of course have an included evening meal I will cook, I do love to cook so I'll try and make something special!!!!
Sunday
: another provided breakfast. Then you are either free to go home or join in a possible activity locally.
Dogs:
Sorry but the event is not suitable for dogs.
Photos:
Provided by OutdoorLads members and Saddleworth Outdoor Pursuits Association and used with permission.
Difficulty & Distance: Relatively strauightforward hiking

Title: SE500 The Winchester Downs
Summary:
An incredible mixture of medieval city, rolling downs, hill fort, chalkland flowers, habitat of rare blue butterflies and a range of less common birds - all in one walk!
The walk begins in the ancient capital of Winchester, which was actually the capital of England in 900CE. The streets are full of history, with Roman remains, King Alfred the Great’s city walls and a grand medieval cathedral. Jane Austen is buried here.
Our route
takes us south beside the River Itchen. This clear, chalk river naturally filters the water, making it ideal for fly fishing and growing watercress. We then climb the slopes of St Catherines Hill. You may notice giant mounds of the ancient defences on the way up and down.
The land here is based on chalk, laid down in warm seas around 98 million years ago. This produced soil ideal for orchids and bell flowers. The hill has been designated a Site of Special Scientific Interest. Look out for the yellow Rock Rose and the uncommon chalkhill blue butterfly in summer. Other flowers include fairy flax, clustered bellflower, autumn lady's tresses orchids and frog orchid; basil and wild carrot. Species uncommon for southern England found here include bastard toadflax and juniper.
Descending to the south, we cross a dry valley to a hill on the south side and then return a little further along. This gives us the best view of St. Catherine’s Hill. The dry valley was used in the 14th century when plague devastated Winchester and the graveyards were full, so bodies were piled in here.
Continuing on our route we pick up the Pilgrim’s Trail and head over the rounded hills of Twyford Down and then climb steadily over Fawley Down. The whole area is known as the Winchester Cluster of Downs. The ancient Pilgrims' Way begins at Winchester and goes all the way to Canterbury and the shrine of Thomas Becket. The pathway under our feet is older than that; at least 4000 years and likely as old as 10000, as old as the stone age.
The highest point on the route is Cheesefoot Head at 176m. There is a naturally rounded amphitheatre here in the down. In 1944 American and British troops were stationed near here and, just before D-Day, it was here that US President Eisenhower made his famous speech: “The eyes of the world are upon you. The hopes and prayers of liberty-loving people everywhere march with you.”
We pick up the South Downs Way over Chilcomb. This area is excellent for bird watching. Skylark, Red Kit, Kestrel and Peregrines are here, as well as Barn Owl and Tawny Owl. Passing the Norman Church, the route turns downhill, crossing the road to rise again over Magdalen Down Hill with it’s Bronze Age burial barrows. Adonis Blue butterflies may be seen here.
Entering Winchester from the East, we rise to the viewpoint on St. Giles Hill. There used to be a medieval fair here on St Giles’s day, 1st of September. You get an excellent view over the city from here before we return to complete the walk.
The ups and downs add up to 500m of ascent, and so this walk qualifies as an entry in the 'SE500' series of walks; if you attend you'll improve your fitness and burn 2100 calories in the process. This walk will be fairly challenging, but we'll take it at a sensible pace and in manageable stages and we'll do each rise with plenty of time to have rests.
The route involves rolling hills, ridges and fields and is suitable for dogs.
